Below is a corrected proof. I will prove the stronger identity

\[
q_Uq_W=q_Wq_U=q_{U\cap W},
\]

hence in particular \(q_U\wedge q_W=q_{U\cap W}\). The stronger product formula is useful because the obstruction state in the upgrade step is not assumed normal on \(N\); mere finite-meet information is not enough for arbitrary states unless the relevant projections commute.

## 1. DKEP support preliminaries

Write \(H=L^2(M)\), and let

\[
\iota_{\rm nor}:B(H)\to N=(B(H)^\sharp_J)^*
\]

be the DKEP normal-bidual embedding. For an induced \(U\subset V\Gamma\), put \(I=U\cap W\), \(B_U=M_U\), and let \(e_U\) be the projection \(H\to L^2(M_U)\). DKEP define \(X_U=X_{M_U}\) as the hereditary \(C^*\)-algebra generated by \(xJyJ e_U\), \(x,y\in M\); then

\[
K_U^L=\overline{B(H)X_U}^{\|\cdot\|_{\infty,2}},\qquad
K_U=(K_U^L)^*\cap K_U^L,\qquad
K_U^{\infty,1}=\overline{K_U}^{\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}}=\overline{X_U}^{\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}}.
\]

Moreover \(K_U^{\infty,1}\) is the strong \(M\)-\(M\) and \(JMJ\)-\(JMJ\) bimodule generated by \(K_U\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

Let \(q_U\in N\) be the identity/support projection of \((K_U^\sharp_J)^*\subset N\). The DKEP normal-bidual support calculus identifies \((K_U^\sharp_J)^*\) with the corner \(q_UNq_U\). Also, because \(M\) and \(JMJ\) act by multipliers on \(K_U\), \(q_U\) commutes with \(M\) and with \(JMJ\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2211.05298))

A key technical fact is that \(\iota(e_U)\) commutes with the normal support projection \(p_{\rm nor}\), so

\[
\varepsilon_U:=\iota_{\rm nor}(e_U)
\]

is an actual projection in \(N\). This is the same argument as DKEP/Ding–Elayavalli Lemma 3.8: vector states obtained after applying \(e_U\) remain normal on \(M\) and \(JMJ\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2211.05298))

Finally, we use the following approximate-unit description. Let \(\mathcal A\subset M\) be the algebraic graph-product \(*\)-algebra, and let \(\mathcal U_0\subset\mathcal A\) be a set of algebraic unitaries generating \(M\) ultraweakly. Let \(X_U^0\) be the hereditary \(C^*\)-subalgebra generated by \(uJvJ e_U\), \(u,v\in\mathcal U_0\). Then \(X_U^0\) is \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-dense in \(K_U^{\infty,1}\). If \((h_\lambda^U)_\lambda\) is any positive contractive approximate unit of \(X_U^0\), chosen inside \(C^*(uJvJ e_U Jv^*Ju^*:u,v\in F_\lambda)\) for finite \(F_\lambda\subset\mathcal U_0\), then

\[
\iota_{\rm nor}(h_\lambda^U)\xrightarrow[\lambda]{\ \sigma\text{-weak}\ }q_U.
\]

This is precisely the DKEP approximate-unit argument: approximate units of a \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-dense subalgebra converge to the normal-bidual identity. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2211.05298))

## 2. Exact reduced-word finite propagation

For \(a,b\in\mathcal A\), write

\[
L_aR_b:=aJb^*J,
\]

so \(L_aR_b(\widehat z)=\widehat{azb}\). The graph-product Wick reduction gives the following exact finite-propagation lemma.

**Lemma.** For \(a,b\in\mathcal A\), there are finitely many \(\alpha_k,\beta_k,\gamma_k,\delta_k\in\mathcal A\) such that

\[
e_U L_aR_b e_W
=
\sum_{k=1}^n
L_{\alpha_k}R_{\beta_k}\, e_I\, L_{\gamma_k}R_{\delta_k}e_W.
\tag{2.1}
\]

Equivalently, for every \(z\in\mathcal A\cap M_W\),

\[
E_U(azb)
=
\sum_{k=1}^n
\alpha_k\,E_I(\gamma_k z\delta_k)\,\beta_k.
\tag{2.2}
\]

**Proof.** By linearity, reduce to \(a,b,z\) reduced graph-product words. Expand \(azb\) using the standard Wick reduction rules for graph products: letters are shuffled past commuting letters, equal-vertex adjacent letters are multiplied inside the corresponding vertex algebra, and each such vertex product is decomposed into its scalar plus trace-zero part.

Only finitely many letters from \(z\) can be involved in cancellations with the fixed words \(a\) and \(b\). If a letter of \(z\) has vertex in \(W\setminus U\), then for its contribution to survive under \(E_U\), that letter must be cancelled during the reduction. Thus every non-cancelled letter of \(z\) has vertex in \(I=U\cap W\). Hence each surviving reduction pattern has the form

\[
z \mapsto \alpha\,E_I(\gamma z\delta)\,\beta
\]

for fixed \(\alpha,\beta,\gamma,\delta\in\mathcal A\), and there are only finitely many possible cancellation/shuffle patterns because \(a\) and \(b\) are fixed finite words. Summing over those finitely many patterns gives (2.2), hence (2.1) on the dense subspace \(\mathcal A\cap M_W\), and therefore on \(L^2(M_W)\). ∎

The same formula with \(U,W\) interchanged also holds.

## 3. Finite triple propagation into \(K_I^{\infty,1}\)

Let \(Y_U^0\) be the \(*\)-algebra generated by the finite algebraic projections

\[
p_{u,v}^U:=uJvJ e_U Jv^*Ju^*,\qquad u,v\in\mathcal U_0.
\]

Thus \(X_U^0\) is the hereditary algebra generated by \(Y_U^0\). The crucial point is:

**Lemma.** If \(x\in Y_U^0\), \(y\in Y_W^0\), \(z\in Y_U^0\), then

\[
xyz\in X_I\subset K_I^{\infty,1}.
\tag{3.1}
\]

Consequently, for the positive approximate units \(h_\lambda^U\in X_U^0\), \(h_\mu^W\in X_W^0\),

\[
h_\lambda^U h_\mu^W h_\lambda^U\in K_I^{\infty,1}.
\tag{3.2}
\]

**Proof.** A monomial in \(Y_U^0Y_W^0Y_U^0\) is a finite product of algebraic \(M\)- and \(JMJ\)-multipliers and projections \(e_U,e_W\), beginning and ending with an \(e_U\)-block and containing at least one \(e_W\)-block. Thus it is a finite linear combination of operators of the schematic form

\[
A_0 e_{S_1} A_1 e_{S_2}\cdots A_{m-1} e_{S_m}A_m,
\]

where each \(S_j\in\{U,W\}\), \(S_1=S_m=U\), at least one \(S_j=W\), and each \(A_j\) is a finite sum of algebraic \(L_aR_b\)’s.

Whenever a transition \(e_U A e_W\) or \(e_W A e_U\) occurs, apply (2.1). This replaces that transition by a finite sum of terms factoring through \(e_I\). Since \(I\subset U,W\), any subsequent adjacent \(e_U\)- or \(e_W\)-block next to an \(e_I\)-block is again reduced by the same finite-propagation formula with \(I\) in place of one of the two sets. Iterating through the finite monomial expresses it as a finite sum of terms

\[
L_\alpha R_\beta\, T\, L_\gamma R_\delta,
\qquad
T\in e_I B(H)e_I,
\]

which are, by definition, in \(X_I\). This proves (3.1). Functional calculus approximating \(h_\lambda^U,h_\mu^W\) by polynomials with zero constant term gives (3.2), because \(K_I^{\infty,1}\) is closed for \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\) and hence also for norm-convergent approximations. ∎

This is the step that repairs the false shortcut \(X_UX_W\subset X_I\). The twofold product is false because \(X_U\) contains large corners. The valid statement is the **triple** support statement: \(U\)-piece, then \(W\)-piece, then \(U\)-piece is forced through \(I=U\cap W\).

## 4. Support product formula

Because each \(h_\lambda^U\) is built from \(e_U\) and \(M,JMJ\)-multipliers, and \(\iota(e_U)\) commutes with \(p_{\rm nor}\), \(\iota_{\rm nor}\) is multiplicative on the finite \(C^*\)-algebra generated by the relevant \(h_\lambda^U,h_\mu^W\). Therefore (3.2) gives

\[
\iota_{\rm nor}(h_\lambda^U)\,
\iota_{\rm nor}(h_\mu^W)\,
\iota_{\rm nor}(h_\lambda^U)
=
\iota_{\rm nor}(h_\lambda^U h_\mu^W h_\lambda^U)
\in q_I N q_I.
\]

The left side is a positive contraction. Hence

\[
0\le
\iota_{\rm nor}(h_\lambda^U)\,
\iota_{\rm nor}(h_\mu^W)\,
\iota_{\rm nor}(h_\lambda^U)
\le q_I.
\]

Taking \(\sigma\)-weak limits first in \(\mu\) and then in \(\lambda\), using normality of multiplication in \(N\), gives

\[
q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_I.
\tag{4.1}
\]

On the other hand, \(I\subset U,W\), so \(X_I\subset X_U\cap X_W\), hence \(K_I\subset K_U\cap K_W\), and therefore

\[
q_I\le q_U,\qquad q_I\le q_W.
\tag{4.2}
\]

Let \(p=q_U\), \(q=q_W\), \(r=q_I\). From \(r\le p,q\) and \(pqp\le r\),

\[
pqp=r+p(q-r)p\le r.
\]

Thus \(p(q-r)p=0\), hence \((q-r)p=0\). Therefore \(qp=rp=r\), and by taking adjoints \(pq=r\). Thus

\[
q_Uq_W=q_Wq_U=q_I=q_{U\cap W}.
\tag{4.3}
\]

In particular,

\[
q_U\wedge q_W=q_{U\cap W}.
\]

Since the projections commute and their product is the intersection projection, induction gives the finite formula

\[
q_{U_1}\wedge\cdots\wedge q_{U_n}
=
q_{U_1}\cdots q_{U_n}
=
q_{U_1\cap\cdots\cap U_n}.
\tag{4.4}
\]

## 5. The empty set

For \(U=\varnothing\), \(M_\varnothing=\mathbb C1\), and \(e_\varnothing\) is the rank-one projection onto \(\mathbb C\widehat 1\). The hereditary algebra generated by \(xJyJ e_\varnothing\), \(x,y\in M\), contains the rank-one operators

\[
\theta_{\widehat x,\widehat y}
=
x e_\varnothing Jy^*J,
\]

and these span a norm-dense subalgebra of \(K(H)\). Hence

\[
X_\varnothing=K(H),\qquad K_\varnothing=K(H),\qquad q_\varnothing=q_K.
\tag{5.1}
\]

## 6. Upgrade from relative link proper proximality

Assume \(M\) has no amenable direct summand and is not properly proximal. By the DKEP normal-bidual obstruction criterion, there is an \(M\)-central state \(\Omega\) on the absolute compactification \(\widetilde S(M)\subset N\), normal on \(M\), such that

\[
\Omega|_M=\tau,\qquad
\Omega(q_K)=0.
\tag{6.1}
\]

The vanishing on \(q_KNq_K=(K(H)^\sharp_J)^*\) is obtained by excluding the weakly compact/hypertrace alternative using the assumption that there is no amenable summand. This is the normal-bidual form of DKEP Lemma 8.6. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

Now suppose \(M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X_U\). I claim that every absolute obstruction state \(\Omega\) as above satisfies

\[
\Omega(q_U)=1.
\tag{6.2}
\]

Indeed, if \(r=1-q_U\) and \(\Omega(r)>0\), define for \(T\in\widetilde S_U(M)\)

\[
\Phi(T)=\frac{\Omega(rTr)}{\Omega(r)}.
\]

Because \(q_U\) commutes with \(M\) and \(JMJ\), for \(a\in JMJ\),

\[
[rTr,a]=r[T,a]r=0,
\]

so \(rTr\in\widetilde S(M)\). Thus \(\Phi\) is well-defined. It is \(M\)-central because \(\Omega\) is \(M\)-central and \(r\) commutes with \(M\). Moreover \(\Phi|_M\) is normal: for \(x\in M_+\),

\[
0\le \Phi(x)=\frac{\Omega(rxr)}{\Omega(r)}
\le
\frac{\Omega(x)}{\Omega(r)}
=
\frac{\tau(x)}{\Omega(r)}.
\]

Hence \(\Phi\) is an \(M\)-central state on \(\widetilde S_U(M)\), normal on \(M\), contradicting proper proximality relative to \(X_U\). Therefore \(\Omega(q_U)=1\).

Apply this with \(U=\operatorname{Link}(v)\) for every vertex \(v\). Then

\[
\Omega(q_{\operatorname{Link}(v)})=1
\qquad\forall v\in V\Gamma.
\]

By the product formula (4.4),

\[
\prod_{v\in V\Gamma} q_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}
=
q_{\bigcap_{v\in V\Gamma}\operatorname{Link}(v)}.
\]

Because the graph has no loops, no vertex belongs to its own link, so

\[
\bigcap_{v\in V\Gamma}\operatorname{Link}(v)=\varnothing.
\]

Thus

\[
\prod_{v\in V\Gamma} q_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}
=
q_\varnothing
=
q_K.
\]

The projections commute, and a state that is \(1\) on each commuting projection is \(1\) on their product. Hence \(\Omega(q_K)=1\), contradicting (6.1). Therefore \(M\) is properly proximal.

## 7. What was wrong in the t2 draft

Two points needed repair.

1. The inclusion \(X_UX_W\subset X_{U\cap W}\) is false because \(X_U\) contains the large corner \(e_UB(H)e_U\). The correct statement is a triple support statement: finite \(U\)-approximate-unit pieces, then \(W\)-pieces, then \(U\)-pieces are forced through \(U\cap W\) after passing to \(K_{U\cap W}^{\infty,1}\).

2. For the upgrade step, the obstruction state \(\Omega\) is not normal on all of \(N\). Thus one cannot use only \(q_U\wedge q_W=q_{U\cap W}\) unless one also knows commutation/product. The repaired proof proves the stronger formula \(q_Uq_W=q_{U\cap W}\), so the finite product argument is legitimate for arbitrary states.

## Bottom line

**t1 is essentially correct** (assuming the standard “irreducible = not a nontrivial join over a partition into two nonempty vertex sets” convention). I do not see a substantive flaw in the AFP ping-pong argument, including the \(M_v=\mathbb C^2\) cases and the unique non-neighbor case.

**The final assembly via t2 is not complete as written.** The main issues are not in t1 but in t2: the “nonamenability” paragraph fails for finite-dimensional vertices, the support-intersection formula \(q_U\wedge q_W=q_{U\cap W}\) is only sketched and has a real approximate-unit/state issue, and the normal-bidual obstruction/compression step needs to be quoted or proved precisely. These are repairable, but they are genuine gaps.

## 1. Verification of t1

Fix \(v\), and set
\[
B=M_{\operatorname{Link}(v)},\qquad
A=M_{\operatorname{Star}(v)},\qquad
C=M_{V\setminus\{v\}}.
\]

### 1.1 AFP decomposition

This part is correct. Since \(v\) commutes exactly with the link algebra,
\[
A=M_{\{v\}}\bar\otimes B.
\]
The standard graph-product normal form gives
\[
M=A *_B C
\]
with the trace-preserving expectations. The intersection \(A\cap C\) is \(B\), and reduced graph-product words alternating between \(A\ominus B\) and \(C\ominus B\) are precisely the AFP reduced words.

### 1.2 Sector projections in \(S_{X_B}(M)\)

Let \(R_A\) be the projection onto
\[
H_A^+=L^2(B)\oplus\{\text{AFP words whose first letter is from } A\ominus B\},
\]
and define \(R_C\) similarly. Then
\[
P_A=R_A-e_B,\qquad P_C=R_C-e_B,\qquad 1=e_B+P_A+P_C.
\]

For \(D\in\{A,C\}\), \(x\in D\ominus B\), and \(\rho(x)=Jx^*J\), the AFP right-multiplication calculation gives
\[
[R_D,\rho(x)]=0,
\]
and, writing \(\bar D\) for the other algebra,
\[
[R_{\bar D},\rho(x)]=e_B\rho(x)-\rho(x)e_B
\]
up to the harmless sign convention for commutators. This is correct: right multiplication changes only the last AFP letter, so the first-letter sector changes only when the vector is in \(L^2(B)\) or when a one-letter \(D\)-word partially collapses into \(B\).

The operators \(e_B\rho(x)\) and \(\rho(x)e_B\) factor through \(L^2(B)\), hence lie in the DKEP compact ideal associated to \(X_B\). Since \(A\) and \(C\) generate \(M\), this gives
\[
R_A,R_C\in S_{X_B}(M).
\]
Also \(e_B\in S_{X_B}(M)\), so \(P_A,P_C\in S_{X_B}(M)\). This part is sound, modulo the standard DKEP generator criterion.

### 1.3 Graph-combinatorial unitary construction

The combinatorics are correct under the standard join definition.

- If \(v\) had no non-neighbor, then \(\Gamma\) would split as
  \[
  \{v\}*\Gamma_{V\setminus\{v\}},
  \]
  contradicting irreducibility.

- If \(v\) has a unique non-neighbor \(w\), then \(\operatorname{Link}(v)\neq\varnothing\). If \(w\) were adjacent to every \(z\in\operatorname{Link}(v)\), then
  \[
  \Gamma=\Gamma_{\{v,w\}}*\Gamma_{\operatorname{Link}(v)},
  \]
  again a nontrivial join. Hence there is \(z\in\operatorname{Link}(v)\) not adjacent to \(w\).

The two cases for \(c_1,c_2\in C\) are valid.

#### Case 1: two non-neighbors

Take distinct non-neighbors \(w_1,w_2\notin\operatorname{Star}(v)\), and trace-zero unitaries \(u_{w_i}\in M_{w_i}\). Then
\[
c_i=u_{w_i}
\]
satisfies
\[
E_B(c_i)=0,\qquad E_B(c_i^*c_j)=\delta_{ij}1.
\]
Even if \(w_1,w_2\) are adjacent, the expectation onto \(B\) kills \(u_{w_1}^*u_{w_2}\), because both syllables are centered and outside \(B\).

#### Case 2: unique non-neighbor

Choose \(z\in\operatorname{Link}(v)\) not adjacent to \(w\), and take trace-zero unitaries \(u_w\in M_w\), \(u_z\in M_z\subset B\). Put
\[
c_1=u_w,\qquad c_2=u_z u_w.
\]
Then \(c_1,c_2\in C\) are unitaries, \(E_B(c_i)=0\), and
\[
E_B(c_i^*c_j)=\delta_{ij}1.
\]
The cross term
\[
c_1^*c_2=u_w^*u_z u_w
\]
is a graph-reduced word of type \(w,z,w\), because \(w\) and \(z\) are not adjacent. Thus it cannot collapse to \(B\). This remains true for \(M_w\simeq\mathbb C^2\): even if \(u_w=u_w^*=u_w^{-1}\), the two \(w\)-letters cannot be shuffled together across the noncommuting \(z\)-letter.

### 1.4 Projection inequalities

If \(d\in C\) is unitary and \(E_B(d)=0\), then \(d\in L^2(C)\ominus L^2(B)\) as an AFP \(C\)-letter. Hence
\[
dH_A^+\subset P_C L^2(M).
\]
Indeed, \(dB\subset C\ominus B\), and \(d\) followed by an \(A\)-letter is an AFP reduced word beginning with \(C\). Therefore
\[
dR_A d^*\le P_C.
\]

For \(d=c_1^*c_2\), one also has \(d\in C\), \(E_B(d)=0\), so
\[
(c_1^*c_2)H_A^+\subset P_C L^2(M)\perp H_A^+.
\]
Thus the ranges \(c_1R_Ac_1^*\) and \(c_2R_Ac_2^*\) are orthogonal, and
\[
c_1R_Ac_1^*+c_2R_Ac_2^*\le P_C.
\]

Similarly, for the trace-zero unitary \(a=u_v\in A\ominus B\),
\[
aH_C^+\subset P_A L^2(M),
\]
so
\[
aR_Ca^*\le P_A.
\]

No hidden \(e_B\)-error appears.

### 1.5 State contradiction

For an \(M\)-central state \(\varphi\) on \(S_{X_B}(M)\), centrality gives
\[
2\varphi(R_A)\le \varphi(P_C),\qquad
\varphi(R_C)\le \varphi(P_A).
\]
Writing
\[
\alpha=\varphi(e_B),\qquad \beta=\varphi(P_A),\qquad \gamma=\varphi(P_C),
\]
we get
\[
\alpha+\beta+\gamma=1,
\]
and
\[
2(\alpha+\beta)\le \gamma,\qquad \alpha+\gamma\le \beta.
\]
Combining forces \(\alpha=\beta=\gamma=0\), contradiction. Thus t1 proves no \(M\)-central state on \(S_{X_B}(M)\), hence relative proper proximality with respect to the link boundary piece.

---

## 2. Problems in t2 / final assembly

### 2.1 The nonamenability paragraph is flawed

t2 says that irreducibility gives nonadjacent \(v,w\), and then one can choose “free Haar unitaries” giving an expected \(L\mathbb F_2\). This is false under the theorem’s hypotheses: vertices may be finite-dimensional, e.g. \(M_v=M_w=\mathbb C^2\). Two trace-zero order-two unitaries generate \(L(\mathbb Z_2*\mathbb Z_2)\), which is amenable.

**Repair:** Use t1 instead. If \(M\) had a nonzero amenable direct summand \(zM\), then amenability of \(zM\) gives a \(zM\)-central hypertrace on \(B(L^2(zM))\). For any fixed \(v\), define
\[
\Phi(T)=\psi(zTz),\qquad T\in S_{X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}}(M).
\]
This is an \(M\)-central state on the relative compactification, contradicting t1. Hence t1 itself implies \(M\) has no amenable direct summand. This repair works also for \(\mathbb C^2\) vertices.

### 2.2 The normal-bidual obstruction step needs a precise DKEP lemma

The assembly needs the following result:

> If \(M\) has no amenable direct summand and is not properly proximal, then there is an \(M\)-central state \(\Omega\) on the absolute normal-bidual compactification, normal on \(M\), with \(\Omega(q_K)=0\).

This is plausible and standard in the DKEP framework, but t2 should quote it precisely. Also, in the nonfactor case, one should not assert unnecessarily that \(\Omega|_M=\tau\). What is needed is only that \(\Omega|_M\) is normal.

### 2.3 The compression argument is conceptually right but must be formalized

Given relative proper proximality for \(X\), the argument that an absolute obstruction state must satisfy
\[
\Omega(q_X)=1
\]
is correct provided:

1. \(q_X\) commutes with the left and right \(M\)-actions;
2. \(S_X(M)\) embeds into the normal-bidual system \(\widetilde S_X(M)\);
3. if \(r=1-q_X\), then \(rTr\in \widetilde S_K(M)\) for \(T\in\widetilde S_X(M)\);
4. the compressed state
   \[
   T\mapsto \frac{\Omega(rTr)}{\Omega(r)}
   \]
   restricts to an \(M\)-central state on the ordinary \(S_X(M)\), normal on \(M\).

These are DKEP formalism points. They are believable, but they should be listed as a lemma rather than treated informally.

### 2.4 The support-intersection formula is the main remaining gap

t2 needs
\[
q_U\wedge q_W=q_{U\cap W}
\]
for graph-product subalgebras \(M_U,M_W\). This is the most delicate step.

The reduced-word finite-propagation claim is plausible, but as written it only handles algebraic coefficients. It does not by itself prove the needed statement for the full hereditary DKEP cores, especially because \(X_U\) contains large corners like \(e_U B(L^2M)e_U\).

There is also a real technical error in the approximate-unit argument: t2 uses arbitrary states and says that if \(\varphi(q_U)=1\), then one may choose approximate-unit elements \(a\in K_U\) with \(\varphi(a)\approx1\). That is false for nonnormal states. The standard counterexample is a singular state on \(\ell^\infty\) which gives value \(1\) to the support projection of \(c_0\) but vanishes on every element of \(c_0\).

**Repair:** prove the projection inequality using normal states on the ambient von Neumann algebra \(N\), since normal states separate projections and do see ultraweak convergence of approximate units. Then the approximate-unit argument can be salvaged, assuming the product inclusion
\[
K_UK_WK_U\subset K_{U\cap W}^{\infty,1}
\]
has been rigorously proved.

---

## 3. Direct absolute ping-pong?

A direct absolute proof works only in special cases, e.g. if some vertex has empty link, because then \(B=\mathbb C\), \(e_B\) is rank one, and the t1 proof is already absolute.

In general, the fixed-vertex sector projections are only in \(S_{X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}}(M)\), not necessarily in the absolute \(S_K(M)\). Thus an absolute obstruction state need not be defined on them. Any direct proof would have to show that the link-relative errors become compact after combining all vertices; that is essentially the same content as the support-intersection formula in t2. So I do not see a genuinely simpler direct absolute ping-pong proof beyond repackaging t2.

---

## 4. Final proof skeleton, with needed lemmas

A clean final proof should be organized as follows.

1. **Fixed-vertex AFP lemma.**  
   For every vertex \(v\), with
   \[
   B=M_{\operatorname{Link}(v)},\quad
   A=M_{\operatorname{Star}(v)},\quad
   C=M_{V\setminus\{v\}},
   \]
   one has \(M=A*_B C\). The sector projections \(R_A,R_C,P_A,P_C\) lie in \(S_{X_B}(M)\), and the ping-pong unitaries \(a,c_1,c_2\) force a contradiction for any \(M\)-central state. Hence \(M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X_B\).

2. **No amenable direct summand.**  
   If \(M\) had an amenable direct summand, its hypertrace would restrict to an \(M\)-central state on every \(S_{X_B}(M)\), contradicting step 1.

3. **DKEP absolute obstruction lemma.**  
   If \(M\) is not properly proximal and has no amenable direct summand, then there exists an absolute obstruction state \(\Omega\), normal on \(M\), with \(\Omega(q_K)=0\).

4. **Compression lemma.**  
   If \(M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X\), then every absolute obstruction state \(\Omega\) satisfies \(\Omega(q_X)=1\). Otherwise compression by \(1-q_X\) gives a forbidden relative central state.

5. **Graph-product support intersection lemma.**  
   For induced \(U_1,\dots,U_n\subset V\),
   \[
   q_{U_1}\wedge\cdots\wedge q_{U_n}
   =
   q_{U_1\cap\cdots\cap U_n}.
   \]
   Also \(q_\varnothing=q_K\). This is the main lemma still needing a rigorous proof.

6. **Finite cover contradiction.**  
   For each vertex \(v\), step 1 and step 4 give
   \[
   \Omega(q_{\operatorname{Link}(v)})=1.
   \]
   Hence
   \[
   \Omega\left(\bigwedge_{v\in V}q_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}\right)=1.
   \]
   But
   \[
   \bigcap_{v\in V}\operatorname{Link}(v)=\varnothing
   \]
   because no vertex lies in its own link. Therefore the meet is \(q_\varnothing=q_K\), contradicting \(\Omega(q_K)=0\).

So: **t1 is solid; the theorem is proved once the DKEP obstruction/compression formalism and the graph-product support-intersection lemma are supplied rigorously.**

writeup_tasks.statement: Let Gamma be a finite simplicial graph with at least three vertices, irreducible in the nontrivial-join sense, and let (M_v, tau_v) be separable tracial von Neumann algebras such that every M_v contains a unitary u_v with tau_v(u_v)=0. Then the tracial graph product von Neumann algebra M_Gamma is properly proximal.
writeup_tasks.writeup_agent_prompt: Produce a fully self-contained mathematical proof of the following theorem. State the theorem exactly at the start, define all notation, and do not refer to any prior conversation or task history. The theorem: Let Gamma be a finite simplicial graph with at least three vertices. Assume Gamma is irreducible, meaning it is not a nontrivial join of two nonempty induced subgraphs. For each vertex v, let (M_v, tau_v) be a separable tracial von Neumann algebra containing a unitary u_v with tau_v(u_v)=0. Let M = graph product over Gamma of the (M_v, tau_v). Prove that M is properly proximal in the sense of Ding, Kunnawalkam Elayavalli, and Peterson. The proof should be organized as follows. First, introduce graph-product subalgebras M_U for induced vertex sets U, the projections e_U from L2(M) onto L2(M_U), and the DKEP boundary piece X_U associated with M_U. State the DKEP facts you use: the operator system S_X(M), relative proper proximality as nonexistence of an M-central state normal on M, the normal-bidual support projection q_X, the obstruction-state criterion, and the compression principle. Second, prove the fixed-vertex link-relative result: for a vertex v set B = M_Link(v), A = M_Star(v), C = M_{V minus {v}} and use M = A *_B C. Define the first-letter sector projections R_A and R_C, with P_A = R_A - e_B and P_C = R_C - e_B. Prove these projections belong to S_{X_B}(M) by the AFP right-multiplication commutator calculation. Construct a trace-zero unitary a in A with E_B(a)=0 and unitaries c_1,c_2 in C with E_B(c_i)=0 and E_B(c_i^* c_j)=delta_ij 1. Treat separately the case of two non-neighbors of v and the case of a unique non-neighbor, using irreducibility to find z in Link(v) not adjacent to that non-neighbor. Prove the projection inequalities c_1 R_A c_1^* + c_2 R_A c_2^* <= P_C and a R_C a^* <= P_A. Deduce that an M-central state on S_{X_B}(M) cannot exist, hence M is properly proximal relative to X_B for every vertex link. Third, prove the finite link-cover upgrade. State and prove the support-product lemma: for all induced U,W subset V, q_U q_W = q_W q_U = q_{U cap W}, and q_emptyset equals the compact support q_K. Include the reduced-word finite-propagation lemma and the approximate-unit argument carefully; explicitly address that X_U contains e_U B(L2M) e_U and explain why the chosen approximate units from finite algebraic conjugates of e_U suffice. Fourth, use the known graph-product factoriality/nonamenability theorem under the present hypotheses, or prove enough to exclude the weakly compact alternative, so that if M were not properly proximal the DKEP obstruction criterion gives an M-central normal-bidual state Omega with Omega(q_K)=0. Since M is relatively properly proximal for every link, the compression argument gives Omega(q_Link(v))=1 for all vertices v. The support-product lemma gives product over v of q_Link(v) = q_intersection_v Link(v) = q_emptyset = q_K, because no vertex lies in its own link. Since the projections commute, Omega(q_K)=1, contradiction. Write the proof in polished referee style, with all symbols defined inline and no dependence on external context except clearly stated standard theorems.

plan.reasoning: The proof is now reduced to making the support argument referee-grade. Task t5 gives a plausible proof of the strong product formula, but the final argument only needs the weaker sandwich inequality q_U q_W q_U ≤ q_{U∩W}. This is strategically safer: it avoids proving commutation of support projections and avoids relying on an arbitrary obstruction state being normal on the ambient bidual. I will therefore ask one solver to produce a fully rigorous proof or disproof of the support sandwich, and another solver to rebuild the final proof using only that weaker lemma.
plan.strategic_note: The main simplification is that full equality q_U q_W = q_{U∩W} is overkill. Prove q_U q_W q_U ≤ q_{U∩W}, then use the GNS observation that a state with φ(p)=1 is supported on p. This should make the finite link-cover upgrade more robust.

new_proven_results.statement: For the final obstruction-state argument, the full commuting support-product formula is not necessary; the sandwich inequality q_U q_W q_U ≤ q_{U∩W} suffices.
new_proven_results.sketch: If Ω is a state and Ω(p)=1 for a projection p, then in the GNS representation π(p)ξ=ξ, so Ω(a)=Ω(pap) for all a. Hence if Ω(q_U)=Ω(q_W)=1, then Ω(q_U q_W q_U)=1. If q_U q_W q_U ≤ q_{U∩W}, positivity forces Ω(q_{U∩W})=1. Iterating over the finite family of vertex links gives Ω(q_empty)=1.

new_proven_results.statement: The final proof can be organized around a weaker finite-cover lemma: relative proper proximality for all vertex-link boundary pieces plus the support sandwich inequality implies absolute proper proximality.
new_proven_results.sketch: An absolute DKEP obstruction state Ω has Ω(q_K)=0. Relative proper proximality for each link forces Ω(q_Link(v))=1. Repeated use of the sandwich inequality gives Ω(q_intersection of all links)=1. Since the graph has no loops, the intersection of all links is empty, so q_empty=q_K, contradiction.

new_failed_attempts.approach: Insist on proving the full commuting product formula q_U q_W=q_{U∩W} before finishing the original theorem.
new_failed_attempts.reason: This is stronger than needed and forces extra normal-bidual multiplicativity and commutation issues; the sandwich inequality already yields the contradiction for arbitrary states.

Check that all uses of DKEP normal-bidual multiplicativity are stated in exactly the form available in the published framework.

task_assignments.solver_prompt:
Your goal is to produce a referee-grade proof, or find a fatal flaw, for the following support sandwich lemma.

Setting: Γ is a finite simplicial graph, M is a tracial graph product von Neumann algebra, and for each U⊂V(Γ), M_U is the canonical induced-subgraph subalgebra. Let e_U be the orthogonal projection L2(M)→L2(M_U). Let X_U be the DKEP boundary piece generated by the M and JMJ translates of e_U, and let q_U be its support projection in the DKEP normal-bidual compactification. For U,W⊂V(Γ), put I=U∩W. Prove, if true, that q_U q_W q_U ≤ q_I.

Read Task t5 carefully and red-team every technical step. You should not merely repeat the sketch. Required deliverables:

1. State exactly which DKEP support facts you use. In particular, specify what it means for a positive contractive approximate unit for the relevant ∥·∥∞,1-closed boundary ideal to converge to q_U, and what multiplicativity or order-continuity is available in the normal bidual.

2. Prove a precise finite-propagation lemma. Either prove an operator identity of the form e_U L_a R_b e_W factors through finitely many M/JMJ translates of e_I for algebraic graph-product words a,b, or prove directly the weaker statement needed for triple products. The proof must use the graph-product reduced-word/conditional-expectation calculus, not just an intuitive cancellation-pattern description.

3. Address the large-corner issue explicitly. X_U contains e_U B(L2(M)) e_U, so explain why the approximate units you use really converge to q_U in the DKEP support sense, or replace the approximate-unit construction with one that genuinely works for the full boundary piece.

4. Prove the passage from algebraic triple products to q_U q_W q_U ≤ q_I. A satisfactory proof should define nets h_λ^U and h_μ^W, show h_λ^U h_μ^W h_λ^U lies in the I-boundary ideal or its ∥·∥∞,1 closure, and then pass to the support projections without assuming unproved global multiplicativity of the bidual embedding.

5. If any step of Task t5 is false or unsupported, say exactly where it fails and propose the weakest corrected support statement that is still enough for the link-cover argument. Remember that the final theorem only needs the sandwich inequality, not q_U q_W=q_I.

# Referenced Prior Solver Outputs
Below are the full outputs of the prior tasks the orchestrator asked you to read. Each section starts with a metadata line that tells you whether the artifact has been verified by an independent verifier+refiner: a `verified ✓` block is the canonical, refined text and its claim/relaxation flags are authoritative; an `unverified self-report` block is a raw draft whose claim flags are the agent's own self-declaration and have NOT been checked. Examine the actual arguments — do not rely solely on summaries elsewhere in this prompt.

## Task t5 — Red-team and repair the DKEP support-product formula
[unverified self-report — verification not run or not yet complete | self_declared_is_relaxation: False | self_declared_problem_solved (the agent's own claim, NOT verified): Prove the following statement:

Let $\Gamma$ be a finite simplicial graph with $|\VV(\GG)|\geq 3$, and $(M_v,\varphi_v)$ be a separable tracial von Neumann algebra for each $v\in \VV$. If $\Gamma$ is irreducible (in the sense that there does not exists subgraphs $\GG_1,\GG_2$ such that $ \VV(\GG_1)\cup \VV(\GG_2)=\VV(\GG) $ and every $(v,u)\in \EE(\GG) $ for all $v\in\VV(\GG_1),u\in \VV(\GG_2)$), and each $M_v$ has a trace zero unitary, then the graph product von Neumann algebra $M$ is properly proximal.]
Source: task_outputs.jsonl — raw agent output. The values in the metadata line above are self-declared and have not been checked. Treat the proof as a draft, not as established.
## Verdict

I do **not** see a counterexample to the product formula. I do see two places where t3’s proof is too quick:

1. **Algebraic conjugates do not give a norm approximate unit for the full boundary piece \(X_U\)** unless one indexes over all relevant \(M\)-unitaries, not just a small algebraic core. This matters because \(X_U\) contains the large corner \(e_U B(L^2M)e_U\).  
2. The claim \(Y_U^0Y_W^0Y_U^0\subset X_{U\cap W}\) needs a more careful multiplier argument; one cannot just insert one \(e_{U\cap W}\) and then multiply freely by arbitrary \(e_U\)-blocks.

After repairing these points, the formula
\[
q_Uq_W=q_Wq_U=q_{U\cap W}
\]
is provable. I describe the corrected proof.

I use the DKEP convention that the boundary piece associated with a subalgebra \(N\subset M\) is the hereditary \(C^*\)-subalgebra generated by \(xJyJ e_N\), and the DKEP normal-bidual support calculus in which approximate units converging in the relevant \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-core converge weak* to the support projection. ([ar5iv.org](https://ar5iv.org/pdf/2211.05298)) DKEP’s bidual embedding is not globally multiplicative, but it is multiplicative on the algebra generated by \(M\), \(JMJ\), and the Jones projections \(e_N\), because \(e_N\) commutes with the normal support projection. ([ar5iv.org](https://ar5iv.org/pdf/2211.05298))

---

## 1. Approximate units: the correct statement

Let \(H=L^2(M)\), \(e_U\) be the projection onto \(L^2(M_U)\), and
\[
L_aR_b=aJb^*J,\qquad L_aR_b(\widehat z)=\widehat{azb}.
\]

Let \(\mathcal A\subset M\) be a bounded \(\|\cdot\|_2\)-dense algebraic graph-product \(*\)-subalgebra. Define the algebraic core
\[
\mathcal S_U^0=\{\,L_aR_b e_U: a,b\in\mathcal A\,\}.
\]
Let \(D_U\) be the hereditary \(C^*\)-subalgebra of \(B(H)\) generated by \(\mathcal S_U^0\). For finite \(F\subset\mathcal S_U^0\) and \(\varepsilon>0\), put
\[
A_{F,U}=\sum_{s\in F} ss^*,\qquad
h_{F,\varepsilon}^U=A_{F,U}(A_{F,U}+\varepsilon)^{-1}.
\]
Then \(h_{F,\varepsilon}^U\) is a positive contraction lying in the \(C^*\)-algebra generated by finitely many algebraic translates of \(e_U\), and the net \((h_{F,\varepsilon}^U)\) is a contractive approximate unit for \(D_U\).

**Important correction.** This net should not be advertised as a norm approximate unit for the full \(X_U\) if the coefficients are restricted to a small algebraic core. The full \(X_U\) contains, for example,
\[
e_U B(H)e_U,
\]
and also translates by arbitrary \(M\)-unitaries. What is true and sufficient is that \(D_U\) is \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-dense in the DKEP boundary ideal associated with \(X_U\). Indeed, for bounded \(x,y,a,b\in M\),
\[
\|L_xR_y e_U-L_aR_b e_U\|_{\infty,1}
\lesssim \|x-a\|_2+\|y-b\|_2,
\]
with constants depending only on uniform operator-norm bounds. Thus algebraic coefficients approximate arbitrary coefficients in the topology relevant for the normal-bidual support. DKEP’s approximate-unit lemma then gives
\[
\iota(h_{F,\varepsilon}^U)\xrightarrow[\;F,\varepsilon\;]{\sigma\text{-weak}} q_U.
\]

The large corner causes no obstruction: since \(e_U\in\mathcal S_U^0\), once \(e_U\in F\), the positive operator \(A_{F,U}\) dominates \(e_U\), so \(h_{F,\varepsilon}^U\) is uniformly close to the identity on \(e_UH\) as \(\varepsilon\downarrow0\). Thus arbitrary elements of \(e_U B(H)e_U\) are absorbed by the approximate unit; they do **not** need to be controlled by reduced-word propagation.

---

## 2. Reduced-word finite propagation

Let \(I=U\cap W\). For \(a,b\in\mathcal A\), one has a finite expansion
\[
e_U L_aR_b e_W
=
\sum_{k=1}^n
L_{\alpha_k}R_{\beta_k}\, e_I\, L_{\gamma_k}R_{\delta_k} e_W,
\tag{2.1}
\]
with \(\alpha_k,\beta_k,\gamma_k,\delta_k\in\mathcal A\). Equivalently, for every algebraic \(z\in M_W\),
\[
E_U(azb)
=
\sum_{k=1}^n
\alpha_k\,E_I(\gamma_k z\delta_k)\,\beta_k.
\tag{2.2}
\]

Here is the reduced-word argument. Write \(a,b,z\) as finite linear combinations of reduced graph-product words. Multiplication \(azb\) is computed by the standard graph-product Wick reduction: shuffle commuting letters, multiply adjacent equal-vertex letters, and decompose each vertex product into scalar plus trace-zero part.

Fix reduced words \(a,b\). In any reduction term contributing to \(E_U(azb)\), every surviving letter must have vertex in \(U\). Since \(z\in M_W\), any surviving letter from \(z\) must therefore have vertex in \(U\cap W=I\). Hence every letter of \(z\) with vertex in \(W\setminus U\) must be removed by scalar contractions during the reduction. Such removals can occur only through finitely many cancellation patterns involving the fixed boundary words \(a\) and \(b\). The untouched part of \(z\) is precisely its \(I\)-part after inserting finitely many fixed algebraic words on the left and right. Each cancellation pattern therefore contributes a term of the form
\[
z\mapsto \alpha\,E_I(\gamma z\delta)\,\beta.
\]
There are only finitely many patterns because \(a\) and \(b\) have finite length. Summing over those patterns gives (2.2), hence (2.1) on the dense algebraic subspace of \(L^2(M_W)\), and therefore as an operator identity.

Taking adjoints also gives the right-handed version
\[
e_W L_aR_b e_U
=
\sum_{\ell=1}^m
e_W L_{\eta_\ell}R_{\theta_\ell}\, e_I\, L_{\rho_\ell}R_{\sigma_\ell}.
\tag{2.3}
\]

---

## 3. Algebraic triple products land in the \(I\)-piece

Let \(Y_U^0\) be the \(*\)-algebra generated by algebraic positive translates
\[
L_aR_b e_U R_b^*L_a^*,\qquad a,b\in\mathcal A,
\]
or, if \(a,b\) are chosen unitary, by the corresponding algebraic conjugate projections.

The key corrected statement is:
\[
Y_U^0\,Y_W^0\,Y_U^0\subset X_I.
\tag{3.1}
\]

The proof has two parts.

First, if \(I\subset S\), then \(Y_S^0\) acts by multipliers on \(X_I\). Indeed, applying (2.1) with \((U,W)=(S,I)\) gives
\[
e_S L_aR_b e_I
\in \operatorname{span}\bigl\{L_\alpha R_\beta e_I L_\gamma R_\delta e_I\bigr\}\subset X_I,
\]
and the adjoint formula gives the corresponding right-multiplier statement. Thus
\[
Y_S^0X_I\subset X_I,\qquad X_IY_S^0\subset X_I
\]
for \(S=U,W\).

Second, isolate one \(U\)-to-\(W\) transition and one \(W\)-to-\(U\) transition. For algebraic multipliers \(A,B\) and any bounded middle \(W\)-block \(T\), consider
\[
e_U A e_W\, T\, e_W B e_U.
\]
Using (2.1) on the first transition and the adjoint form (2.3) on the second,
\[
e_U A e_W
=
\sum_i A_i e_I B_i e_W,
\qquad
e_W B e_U
=
\sum_j e_W C_j e_I D_j.
\]
Therefore
\[
e_U A e_W\,T\,e_W B e_U
=
\sum_{i,j}
A_i\,e_I\,(B_i e_W T e_W C_j)\,e_I\,D_j.
\]
The middle term \(e_I(\cdots)e_I\) lies in the large corner \(e_I B(H)e_I\subset X_I\), and the outside factors are \(M\)- and \(JMJ\)-multipliers. Hence the whole expression lies in \(X_I\).

Combining this with the multiplier absorption from the first paragraph proves (3.1). Consequently, if \(h^U\) and \(h^W\) are chosen from finite functional calculus as above, then by polynomial approximation with zero constant term,
\[
h^U h^W h^U\in X_I\subset K_I^{\infty,1}.
\tag{3.2}
\]

This is the rigorous replacement for the false shortcut \(X_UX_W\subset X_I\). The correct phenomenon is a **sandwich** phenomenon: \(U\)-piece, then \(W\)-piece, then \(U\)-piece forces passage through \(I=U\cap W\).

---

## 4. Passing to the normal bidual

The DKEP embedding \(\iota:B(H)\to N=(B(H)_J^\sharp)^*\) is not globally multiplicative. But it is multiplicative on the finite \(C^*\)-algebras generated here, because those algebras are generated by \(M\), \(JMJ\), and finitely many projections \(e_S\), and DKEP’s Lemma 3.8 gives that each \(e_S\) commutes with the normal support projection used in the compression defining \(\iota\). ([ar5iv.org](https://ar5iv.org/pdf/2211.05298))

Thus
\[
\iota(h^U h^W h^U)
=
\iota(h^U)\,\iota(h^W)\,\iota(h^U).
\]
Since \(h^Uh^Wh^U\in K_I^{\infty,1}\), its image lies in the corner \(q_INq_I\). Because it is a positive contraction,
\[
0\le
\iota(h^U)\,\iota(h^W)\,\iota(h^U)
\le q_I.
\tag{4.1}
\]

Now take the approximate-unit limits. Write
\[
a_\lambda=\iota(h_\lambda^U),\qquad b_\mu=\iota(h_\mu^W).
\]
For fixed \(\lambda\), \(b_\mu\to q_W\) \(\sigma\)-weakly, so
\[
a_\lambda b_\mu a_\lambda\to a_\lambda q_W a_\lambda
\]
\(\sigma\)-weakly, and hence
\[
0\le a_\lambda q_W a_\lambda\le q_I.
\]
Next \(a_\lambda\to q_U\) \(\sigma\)-weakly. Since \(0\le a_\lambda\le q_U\) and the limit is the projection \(q_U\), the convergence is strong. Therefore
\[
a_\lambda q_W a_\lambda\to q_Uq_Wq_U
\]
\(\sigma\)-weakly, and we obtain
\[
q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_I.
\tag{4.2}
\]

---

## 5. Projection algebra gives the product formula

Because \(I\subset U,W\), the boundary piece \(X_I\) is contained in both \(X_U\) and \(X_W\). Hence
\[
q_I\le q_U,\qquad q_I\le q_W.
\]
Set \(p=q_U\), \(q=q_W\), \(r=q_I\). We have \(r\le p,q\) and \(pqp\le r\). Then
\[
0\le p(q-r)p=pqp-r\le0,
\]
so \(p(q-r)p=0\). Hence \((q-r)p=0\), and therefore
\[
qp=rp=r.
\]
Taking adjoints gives \(pq=r\). Thus
\[
q_Uq_W=q_Wq_U=q_I=q_{U\cap W}.
\]

By induction,
\[
q_{U_1}\cdots q_{U_n}=q_{U_1\cap\cdots\cap U_n}.
\]

For \(U=\varnothing\), \(e_\varnothing\) is the rank-one projection onto \(\mathbb C\widehat1\), and the hereditary \(M\)-\(JMJ\) hull is \(K(L^2M)\), so \(q_\varnothing=q_K\). Therefore the finite link-cover upgrade uses commuting projections and is valid: if an obstruction state is \(1\) on every \(q_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}\), then it is \(1\) on their product, which is \(q_\varnothing=q_K\), contradicting the absolute obstruction condition \(\Omega(q_K)=0\).

---

## Direct answers to the six requested points

1. **Approximate units.**  
   Finite functional calculus of algebraic translates gives a p.c.a.i. for an \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-dense core and hence converges to \(q_U\). It should not be claimed to be a norm p.c.a.i. for the full \(X_U\) if only a small algebraic coefficient set is used.

2. **Finite propagation.**  
   The reduced-word Wick reduction proves
   \[
   e_U L_aR_b e_W
   =
   \sum L_\alpha R_\beta e_{U\cap W}L_\gamma R_\delta e_W
   \]
   for algebraic \(a,b\).

3. **Large corners.**  
   Arbitrary \(e_U B(H)e_U\) elements need not be reduced word by word. They are absorbed by the approximate unit because \(e_U\) itself is among the generators.

4. **Triple products.**  
   The valid statement is not \(X_UX_W\subset X_I\), but
   \[
   Y_U^0Y_W^0Y_U^0\subset X_I,
   \]
   and hence \(h^Uh^Wh^U\in K_I^{\infty,1}\).

5. **Normal-bidual products.**  
   Multiplicativity holds on the finite algebras used because \(p_{\rm nor}\) commutes with \(M\), \(JMJ\), and the relevant \(e_S\). The weak* limits give
   \[
   q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_{U\cap W}.
   \]

6. **Projection algebra.**  
   Since \(q_{U\cap W}\le q_U,q_W\), the inequality \(q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_{U\cap W}\) forces
   \[
   q_Uq_W=q_Wq_U=q_{U\cap W}.
   \]

Your goal is to produce a referee-grade proof, or find a fatal flaw, for the following support sandwich lemma.

Setting: Γ is a finite simplicial graph, M is a tracial graph product von Neumann algebra, and for each U⊂V(Γ), M_U is the canonical induced-subgraph subalgebra. Let e_U be the orthogonal projection L2(M)→L2(M_U). Let X_U be the DKEP boundary piece generated by the M and JMJ translates of e_U, and let q_U be its support projection in the DKEP normal-bidual compactification. For U,W⊂V(Γ), put I=U∩W. Prove, if true, that q_U q_W q_U ≤ q_I.

Read Task t5 carefully and red-team every technical step. You should not merely repeat the sketch. Required deliverables:

1. State exactly which DKEP support facts you use. In particular, specify what it means for a positive contractive approximate unit for the relevant ∥·∥∞,1-closed boundary ideal to converge to q_U, and what multiplicativity or order-continuity is available in the normal bidual.

2. Prove a precise finite-propagation lemma. Either prove an operator identity of the form e_U L_a R_b e_W factors through finitely many M/JMJ translates of e_I for algebraic graph-product words a,b, or prove directly the weaker statement needed for triple products. The proof must use the graph-product reduced-word/conditional-expectation calculus, not just an intuitive cancellation-pattern description.

3. Address the large-corner issue explicitly. X_U contains e_U B(L2(M)) e_U, so explain why the approximate units you use really converge to q_U in the DKEP support sense, or replace the approximate-unit construction with one that genuinely works for the full boundary piece.

4. Prove the passage from algebraic triple products to q_U q_W q_U ≤ q_I. A satisfactory proof should define nets h_λ^U and h_μ^W, show h_λ^U h_μ^W h_λ^U lies in the I-boundary ideal or its ∥·∥∞,1 closure, and then pass to the support projections without assuming unproved global multiplicativity of the bidual embedding.

5. If any step of Task t5 is false or unsupported, say exactly where it fails and propose the weakest corrected support statement that is still enough for the link-cover argument. Remember that the final theorem only needs the sandwich inequality, not q_U q_W=q_I.

1. Write the final finite-cover upgrade carefully. Prove the elementary GNS fact: if φ is any state on a C*-algebra or von Neumann algebra and p is a projection with φ(p)=1, then φ(a)=φ(pap) for all a. Deduce that if φ(q_U)=φ(q_W)=1 and q_U q_W q_U ≤ q_{U∩W}, then φ(q_{U∩W})=1. Iterate this over the finite family U_v=Link(v) to get φ(q_empty)=1.

2. Check the DKEP obstruction/compression step. Starting from non-proper proximality and no amenable direct summand, state precisely the obstruction state Ω and why relative proper proximality to X_U forces Ω(q_U)=1. Make sure this does not use normality of Ω on the whole bidual.

3. Recheck the fixed-vertex AFP ping-pong lemma from Task t6. Verify the graph-combinatorial construction of a∈A⊖B and c_1,c_2∈C⊖B with E_B(c_i* c_j)=δ_ij 1, including the unique non-neighbor and order-two unitary cases. 4. Recheck the no-amenable-direct-summand step. If zM is amenable for a nonzero central projection z, show carefully that a hypertrace on B(zL2(M)) gives an M-central state on S_{X_Link(v)} normal on M, contradicting the relative ping-pong result.

## Verdict

I do **not** see a counterexample to the product formula. I do see two places where t3’s proof is too quick:

1. **Algebraic conjugates do not give a norm approximate unit for the full boundary piece \(X_U\)** unless one indexes over all relevant \(M\)-unitaries, not just a small algebraic core. This matters because \(X_U\) contains the large corner \(e_U B(L^2M)e_U\).  
2. The claim \(Y_U^0Y_W^0Y_U^0\subset X_{U\cap W}\) needs a more careful multiplier argument; one cannot just insert one \(e_{U\cap W}\) and then multiply freely by arbitrary \(e_U\)-blocks.

After repairing these points, the formula
\[
q_Uq_W=q_Wq_U=q_{U\cap W}
\]
is provable. I describe the corrected proof.

I use the DKEP convention that the boundary piece associated with a subalgebra \(N\subset M\) is the hereditary \(C^*\)-subalgebra generated by \(xJyJ e_N\), and the DKEP normal-bidual support calculus in which approximate units converging in the relevant \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-core converge weak* to the support projection. ([ar5iv.org](https://ar5iv.org/pdf/2211.05298)) DKEP’s bidual embedding is not globally multiplicative, but it is multiplicative on the algebra generated by \(M\), \(JMJ\), and the Jones projections \(e_N\), because \(e_N\) commutes with the normal support projection. ([ar5iv.org](https://ar5iv.org/pdf/2211.05298))

---

## 1. Approximate units: the correct statement

Let \(H=L^2(M)\), \(e_U\) be the projection onto \(L^2(M_U)\), and
\[
L_aR_b=aJb^*J,\qquad L_aR_b(\widehat z)=\widehat{azb}.
\]

Let \(\mathcal A\subset M\) be a bounded \(\|\cdot\|_2\)-dense algebraic graph-product \(*\)-subalgebra. Define the algebraic core
\[
\mathcal S_U^0=\{\,L_aR_b e_U: a,b\in\mathcal A\,\}.
\]
Let \(D_U\) be the hereditary \(C^*\)-subalgebra of \(B(H)\) generated by \(\mathcal S_U^0\). For finite \(F\subset\mathcal S_U^0\) and \(\varepsilon>0\), put
\[
A_{F,U}=\sum_{s\in F} ss^*,\qquad
h_{F,\varepsilon}^U=A_{F,U}(A_{F,U}+\varepsilon)^{-1}.
\]
Then \(h_{F,\varepsilon}^U\) is a positive contraction lying in the \(C^*\)-algebra generated by finitely many algebraic translates of \(e_U\), and the net \((h_{F,\varepsilon}^U)\) is a contractive approximate unit for \(D_U\).

**Important correction.** This net should not be advertised as a norm approximate unit for the full \(X_U\) if the coefficients are restricted to a small algebraic core. The full \(X_U\) contains, for example,
\[
e_U B(H)e_U,
\]
and also translates by arbitrary \(M\)-unitaries. What is true and sufficient is that \(D_U\) is \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-dense in the DKEP boundary ideal associated with \(X_U\). Indeed, for bounded \(x,y,a,b\in M\),
\[
\|L_xR_y e_U-L_aR_b e_U\|_{\infty,1}
\lesssim \|x-a\|_2+\|y-b\|_2,
\]
with constants depending only on uniform operator-norm bounds. Thus algebraic coefficients approximate arbitrary coefficients in the topology relevant for the normal-bidual support. DKEP’s approximate-unit lemma then gives
\[
\iota(h_{F,\varepsilon}^U)\xrightarrow[\;F,\varepsilon\;]{\sigma\text{-weak}} q_U.
\]

The large corner causes no obstruction: since \(e_U\in\mathcal S_U^0\), once \(e_U\in F\), the positive operator \(A_{F,U}\) dominates \(e_U\), so \(h_{F,\varepsilon}^U\) is uniformly close to the identity on \(e_UH\) as \(\varepsilon\downarrow0\). Thus arbitrary elements of \(e_U B(H)e_U\) are absorbed by the approximate unit; they do **not** need to be controlled by reduced-word propagation.

---

## 2. Reduced-word finite propagation

Let \(I=U\cap W\). For \(a,b\in\mathcal A\), one has a finite expansion
\[
e_U L_aR_b e_W
=
\sum_{k=1}^n
L_{\alpha_k}R_{\beta_k}\, e_I\, L_{\gamma_k}R_{\delta_k} e_W,
\tag{2.1}
\]
with \(\alpha_k,\beta_k,\gamma_k,\delta_k\in\mathcal A\). Equivalently, for every algebraic \(z\in M_W\),
\[
E_U(azb)
=
\sum_{k=1}^n
\alpha_k\,E_I(\gamma_k z\delta_k)\,\beta_k.
\tag{2.2}
\]

Here is the reduced-word argument. Write \(a,b,z\) as finite linear combinations of reduced graph-product words. Multiplication \(azb\) is computed by the standard graph-product Wick reduction: shuffle commuting letters, multiply adjacent equal-vertex letters, and decompose each vertex product into scalar plus trace-zero part.

Fix reduced words \(a,b\). In any reduction term contributing to \(E_U(azb)\), every surviving letter must have vertex in \(U\). Since \(z\in M_W\), any surviving letter from \(z\) must therefore have vertex in \(U\cap W=I\). Hence every letter of \(z\) with vertex in \(W\setminus U\) must be removed by scalar contractions during the reduction. Such removals can occur only through finitely many cancellation patterns involving the fixed boundary words \(a\) and \(b\). The untouched part of \(z\) is precisely its \(I\)-part after inserting finitely many fixed algebraic words on the left and right. Each cancellation pattern therefore contributes a term of the form
\[
z\mapsto \alpha\,E_I(\gamma z\delta)\,\beta.
\]
There are only finitely many patterns because \(a\) and \(b\) have finite length. Summing over those patterns gives (2.2), hence (2.1) on the dense algebraic subspace of \(L^2(M_W)\), and therefore as an operator identity.

Taking adjoints also gives the right-handed version
\[
e_W L_aR_b e_U
=
\sum_{\ell=1}^m
e_W L_{\eta_\ell}R_{\theta_\ell}\, e_I\, L_{\rho_\ell}R_{\sigma_\ell}.
\tag{2.3}
\]

---

## 3. Algebraic triple products land in the \(I\)-piece

Let \(Y_U^0\) be the \(*\)-algebra generated by algebraic positive translates
\[
L_aR_b e_U R_b^*L_a^*,\qquad a,b\in\mathcal A,
\]
or, if \(a,b\) are chosen unitary, by the corresponding algebraic conjugate projections.

The key corrected statement is:
\[
Y_U^0\,Y_W^0\,Y_U^0\subset X_I.
\tag{3.1}
\]

The proof has two parts.

First, if \(I\subset S\), then \(Y_S^0\) acts by multipliers on \(X_I\). Indeed, applying (2.1) with \((U,W)=(S,I)\) gives
\[
e_S L_aR_b e_I
\in \operatorname{span}\bigl\{L_\alpha R_\beta e_I L_\gamma R_\delta e_I\bigr\}\subset X_I,
\]
and the adjoint formula gives the corresponding right-multiplier statement. Thus
\[
Y_S^0X_I\subset X_I,\qquad X_IY_S^0\subset X_I
\]
for \(S=U,W\).

Second, isolate one \(U\)-to-\(W\) transition and one \(W\)-to-\(U\) transition. For algebraic multipliers \(A,B\) and any bounded middle \(W\)-block \(T\), consider
\[
e_U A e_W\, T\, e_W B e_U.
\]
Using (2.1) on the first transition and the adjoint form (2.3) on the second,
\[
e_U A e_W
=
\sum_i A_i e_I B_i e_W,
\qquad
e_W B e_U
=
\sum_j e_W C_j e_I D_j.
\]
Therefore
\[
e_U A e_W\,T\,e_W B e_U
=
\sum_{i,j}
A_i\,e_I\,(B_i e_W T e_W C_j)\,e_I\,D_j.
\]
The middle term \(e_I(\cdots)e_I\) lies in the large corner \(e_I B(H)e_I\subset X_I\), and the outside factors are \(M\)- and \(JMJ\)-multipliers. Hence the whole expression lies in \(X_I\).

Combining this with the multiplier absorption from the first paragraph proves (3.1). Consequently, if \(h^U\) and \(h^W\) are chosen from finite functional calculus as above, then by polynomial approximation with zero constant term,
\[
h^U h^W h^U\in X_I\subset K_I^{\infty,1}.
\tag{3.2}
\]

This is the rigorous replacement for the false shortcut \(X_UX_W\subset X_I\). The correct phenomenon is a **sandwich** phenomenon: \(U\)-piece, then \(W\)-piece, then \(U\)-piece forces passage through \(I=U\cap W\).

---

## 4. Passing to the normal bidual

The DKEP embedding \(\iota:B(H)\to N=(B(H)_J^\sharp)^*\) is not globally multiplicative. But it is multiplicative on the finite \(C^*\)-algebras generated here, because those algebras are generated by \(M\), \(JMJ\), and finitely many projections \(e_S\), and DKEP’s Lemma 3.8 gives that each \(e_S\) commutes with the normal support projection used in the compression defining \(\iota\). ([ar5iv.org](https://ar5iv.org/pdf/2211.05298))

Thus
\[
\iota(h^U h^W h^U)
=
\iota(h^U)\,\iota(h^W)\,\iota(h^U).
\]
Since \(h^Uh^Wh^U\in K_I^{\infty,1}\), its image lies in the corner \(q_INq_I\). Because it is a positive contraction,
\[
0\le
\iota(h^U)\,\iota(h^W)\,\iota(h^U)
\le q_I.
\tag{4.1}
\]

Now take the approximate-unit limits. Write
\[
a_\lambda=\iota(h_\lambda^U),\qquad b_\mu=\iota(h_\mu^W).
\]
For fixed \(\lambda\), \(b_\mu\to q_W\) \(\sigma\)-weakly, so
\[
a_\lambda b_\mu a_\lambda\to a_\lambda q_W a_\lambda
\]
\(\sigma\)-weakly, and hence
\[
0\le a_\lambda q_W a_\lambda\le q_I.
\]
Next \(a_\lambda\to q_U\) \(\sigma\)-weakly. Since \(0\le a_\lambda\le q_U\) and the limit is the projection \(q_U\), the convergence is strong. Therefore
\[
a_\lambda q_W a_\lambda\to q_Uq_Wq_U
\]
\(\sigma\)-weakly, and we obtain
\[
q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_I.
\tag{4.2}
\]

---

## 5. Projection algebra gives the product formula

Because \(I\subset U,W\), the boundary piece \(X_I\) is contained in both \(X_U\) and \(X_W\). Hence
\[
q_I\le q_U,\qquad q_I\le q_W.
\]
Set \(p=q_U\), \(q=q_W\), \(r=q_I\). We have \(r\le p,q\) and \(pqp\le r\). Then
\[
0\le p(q-r)p=pqp-r\le0,
\]
so \(p(q-r)p=0\). Hence \((q-r)p=0\), and therefore
\[
qp=rp=r.
\]
Taking adjoints gives \(pq=r\). Thus
\[
q_Uq_W=q_Wq_U=q_I=q_{U\cap W}.
\]

By induction,
\[
q_{U_1}\cdots q_{U_n}=q_{U_1\cap\cdots\cap U_n}.
\]

For \(U=\varnothing\), \(e_\varnothing\) is the rank-one projection onto \(\mathbb C\widehat1\), and the hereditary \(M\)-\(JMJ\) hull is \(K(L^2M)\), so \(q_\varnothing=q_K\). Therefore the finite link-cover upgrade uses commuting projections and is valid: if an obstruction state is \(1\) on every \(q_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}\), then it is \(1\) on their product, which is \(q_\varnothing=q_K\), contradicting the absolute obstruction condition \(\Omega(q_K)=0\).

---

## Direct answers to the six requested points

1. **Approximate units.**  
   Finite functional calculus of algebraic translates gives a p.c.a.i. for an \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-dense core and hence converges to \(q_U\). It should not be claimed to be a norm p.c.a.i. for the full \(X_U\) if only a small algebraic coefficient set is used.

2. **Finite propagation.**  
   The reduced-word Wick reduction proves
   \[
   e_U L_aR_b e_W
   =
   \sum L_\alpha R_\beta e_{U\cap W}L_\gamma R_\delta e_W
   \]
   for algebraic \(a,b\).

3. **Large corners.**  
   Arbitrary \(e_U B(H)e_U\) elements need not be reduced word by word. They are absorbed by the approximate unit because \(e_U\) itself is among the generators.

4. **Triple products.**  
   The valid statement is not \(X_UX_W\subset X_I\), but
   \[
   Y_U^0Y_W^0Y_U^0\subset X_I,
   \]
   and hence \(h^Uh^Wh^U\in K_I^{\infty,1}\).

5. **Normal-bidual products.**  
   Multiplicativity holds on the finite algebras used because \(p_{\rm nor}\) commutes with \(M\), \(JMJ\), and the relevant \(e_S\). The weak* limits give
   \[
   q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_{U\cap W}.
   \]

6. **Projection algebra.**  
   Since \(q_{U\cap W}\le q_U,q_W\), the inequality \(q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_{U\cap W}\) forces
   \[
   q_Uq_W=q_Wq_U=q_{U\cap W}.
   \]

Source: task_outputs.jsonl — raw agent output. The values in the metadata line above are self-declared and have not been checked. Treat the proof as a draft, not as established.
Below is a complete proof skeleton, with the **support-product lemma**
\[
q_Uq_W=q_Wq_U=q_{U\cap W}
\]
taken as the allowed black box. I also flag the hidden assumptions needed for the DKEP normal-bidual formulation.

---

## 0. Conventions and external DKEP inputs

Interpret “irreducible” in the standard graph-product sense: there is **no nontrivial join decomposition**
\[
V=V_1\sqcup V_2,\qquad V_i\neq\varnothing,
\]
such that every vertex of \(V_1\) is adjacent to every vertex of \(V_2\). If empty subgraphs were allowed in the definition, no graph would satisfy the condition, so the nonempty/nontrivial convention is necessary.

For \(U\subset V\), write \(M_U\) for the graph-product subalgebra, \(e_U:L^2(M)\to L^2(M_U)\) for the Jones projection, \(X_U\) for the DKEP boundary piece generated by \(e_U\), and \(q_U\) for its normal-bidual support projection.

We use these standard DKEP facts:

1. \(S_{X_U}(M)\) consists of operators whose commutators with the right \(M\)-action are \(X_U\)-compact; the generator criterion is valid.
2. Relative proper proximality to \(X_U\) means: no \(M\)-central state on \(S_{X_U}(M)\) whose restriction to \(M\) is normal.
3. If \(M\) has no amenable direct summand and is not properly proximal, there is an absolute obstruction state \(\Omega\) in the normal-bidual compactification, normal on \(M\), with
   \[
   \Omega(q_K)=0,
   \]
   where \(q_K\) is the compact support.
4. The support projections \(q_U\) commute with the left and right \(M\)-actions.
5. **Allowed black box:** for all \(U,W\subset V\),
   \[
   q_Uq_W=q_Wq_U=q_{U\cap W}.
   \]

---

## 1. Graph-combinatorial consequences of irreducibility

Fix \(v\in V\). Let
\[
L=\operatorname{Link}(v),\qquad \operatorname{Star}(v)=L\cup\{v\},\qquad N(v)=V\setminus \operatorname{Star}(v).
\]

### Star is not all of \(V\)

If \(\operatorname{Star}(v)=V\), then \(v\) is adjacent to every other vertex, so
\[
V=\{v\}\sqcup (V\setminus\{v\})
\]
is a nontrivial join decomposition. Since \(|V|\ge 3\), both sides are nonempty. Thus irreducibility forces
\[
N(v)\neq\varnothing.
\]

### Unique non-neighbor case

If \(N(v)=\{w\}\), then
\[
V=\{v,w\}\sqcup L.
\]
Because \(|V|\ge 3\), \(L\neq\varnothing\). If \(w\) were adjacent to every \(z\in L\), then every vertex in \(\{v,w\}\) would be adjacent to every vertex in \(L\), giving a join decomposition
\[
\Gamma=\Gamma_{\{v,w\}}*\Gamma_L,
\]
contradiction. Hence in the unique non-neighbor case there exists
\[
z\in L
\]
such that \(z\) is not adjacent to \(w\).

These are the only graph-combinatorial facts needed later.

---

## 2. AFP decomposition

Set
\[
B=M_L,\qquad A=M_{\operatorname{Star}(v)},\qquad C=M_{V\setminus\{v\}}.
\]
Since \(v\) is adjacent to every vertex in \(L\),
\[
A\cong M_v\bar\otimes B,
\]
and the expectation \(E_B:A\to B\) is
\[
E_B=\tau_v\otimes \operatorname{id}_B.
\]

The standard graph-product normal form gives
\[
M=A*_B C.
\]
Indeed, \(A\) is generated by \(B\) and \(M_v\), while \(C\) is generated by \(B\) and the vertex algebras \(M_w\) for \(w\neq v\). Since no vertex in \(V\setminus\operatorname{Star}(v)\) is adjacent to \(v\), alternating \(B\)-centered words from \(A\ominus B\) and \(C\ominus B\) remain orthogonal to \(L^2(B)\). Thus the \(B\)-valued mixed moments vanish, which is precisely amalgamated freeness over \(B\).

---

## 3. Sector projections lie in \(S_{X_L}(M)\)

Let
\[
K_A=L^2(A)\ominus L^2(B),\qquad K_C=L^2(C)\ominus L^2(B).
\]
The AFP decomposition is
\[
L^2(M)=L^2(B)\oplus
\bigoplus_{\epsilon_1\ne\cdots\ne\epsilon_n}
K_{\epsilon_1}\otimes_B\cdots\otimes_B K_{\epsilon_n},
\]
where each \(\epsilon_i\in\{A,C\}\).

Let \(R_A\) be the projection onto \(L^2(B)\) plus the summands whose first AFP letter is \(A\). Define \(R_C\) similarly, and put
\[
P_A=R_A-e_B,\qquad P_C=R_C-e_B.
\]
Then
\[
1=e_B+P_A+P_C.
\]

Let \(\rho(x)=Jx^*J\) denote right multiplication. For \(D\in\{A,C\}\), \(x\in D^\circ:=\ker E_B|_D\), right multiplication by \(x\) only changes the last AFP letter. Hence it does not change whether the first letter is \(D\), except for the harmless \(L^2(B)\)-boundary already included in \(R_D\). Therefore
\[
[R_D,\rho(x)]=0.
\]
Since
\[
R_{\bar D}=1-R_D+e_B,
\]
we get
\[
[R_{\bar D},\rho(x)]=[e_B,\rho(x)]
=e_B\rho(x)-\rho(x)e_B.
\]
Both terms factor through \(L^2(B)\), hence are \(X_L\)-compact. For \(b\in B\), right multiplication by \(b\) preserves every AFP sector, so the commutators vanish.

Every vertex algebra lies in \(A\) or \(C\), and these vertex algebras generate \(M\). Thus the DKEP generator criterion gives
\[
R_A,R_C\in S_{X_L}(M).
\]
Since \(e_B\in X_L\subset S_{X_L}(M)\), also
\[
P_A,P_C\in S_{X_L}(M).
\]

No hidden \(e_B\)-error appears here: the only sector changes caused by right multiplication are exactly the two terms \(e_B\rho(x)\) and \(\rho(x)e_B\), both \(X_L\)-compact.

---

## 4. Ping-pong unitaries

Choose a trace-zero unitary \(u_v\in M_v\). Since
\[
A\cong M_v\bar\otimes B,
\]
put
\[
a=u_v\otimes 1_B\in A.
\]
Then
\[
E_B(a)=\tau_v(u_v)1_B=0.
\]

Now construct \(c_1,c_2\in C\).

### Case 1: at least two non-neighbors

If \(|N(v)|\ge 2\), choose distinct \(w_1,w_2\in N(v)\). Let \(u_{w_i}\in M_{w_i}\) be trace-zero unitaries and set
\[
c_i=u_{w_i}.
\]
Then \(c_i\in C\), \(E_B(c_i)=0\), and
\[
E_B(c_i^*c_j)=\delta_{ij}1.
\]
For \(i\neq j\), the product \(u_{w_i}^*u_{w_j}\) has support outside \(L\), so \(E_B\) kills it. This remains true whether or not \(w_1,w_2\) are adjacent.

### Case 2: unique non-neighbor

Let \(N(v)=\{w\}\). By the graph argument above, choose \(z\in L\) not adjacent to \(w\). Pick trace-zero unitaries
\[
u_w\in M_w,\qquad u_z\in M_z\subset B,
\]
and define
\[
c_1=u_w,\qquad c_2=u_z u_w.
\]
Then \(c_1,c_2\in C\) are unitaries, and
\[
E_B(c_1)=0,\qquad E_B(c_2)=u_zE_B(u_w)=0.
\]
Also \(E_B(c_i^*c_i)=1\). For the cross term,
\[
c_1^*c_2=u_w^*u_z u_w.
\]
Because \(z\) and \(w\) are not adjacent, the word \(w,z,w\) is graph-reduced; the two \(w\)-letters cannot be shuffled together. Hence the word contains a non-link vertex and is killed by \(E_B\):
\[
E_B(c_1^*c_2)=0.
\]
Taking adjoints gives \(E_B(c_2^*c_1)=0\).

This also covers order-two trace-zero unitaries, e.g. \(M_w\simeq\mathbb C^2\). Even if \(u_w=u_w^*=u_w^{-1}\), the intervening noncommuting \(u_z\) prevents cancellation.

Thus in all cases we have unitaries
\[
a\in A,\qquad c_1,c_2\in C
\]
such that
\[
E_B(a)=0,\qquad E_B(c_i)=0,\qquad E_B(c_i^*c_j)=\delta_{ij}1.
\]

---

## 5. Projection inequalities

Let
\[
H_A^+=R_A L^2(M),\qquad H_C^+=R_C L^2(M).
\]

If \(d\in C\) satisfies \(E_B(d)=0\), then left multiplication by \(d\) sends \(H_A^+\) into \(P_C L^2(M)\). Indeed:

- \(dL^2(B)\subset L^2(C)\ominus L^2(B)\);
- if a reduced AFP word begins with an \(A\)-letter, then multiplying on the left by \(d\in C^\circ\) produces a reduced word beginning with \(C\).

Hence
\[
dH_A^+\subset P_C L^2(M).
\]
Applying this to \(d=c_i\),
\[
c_iR_Ac_i^*\le P_C.
\]

The two ranges are orthogonal. Since
\[
E_B(c_1^*c_2)=0,
\]
we also have
\[
(c_1^*c_2)H_A^+\subset P_C L^2(M).
\]
But
\[
P_C L^2(M)\perp H_A^+.
\]
Thus
\[
c_1R_Ac_1^*\perp c_2R_Ac_2^*,
\]
and therefore
\[
c_1R_Ac_1^*+c_2R_Ac_2^*\le P_C.
\]

Similarly, since \(a\in A\) and \(E_B(a)=0\),
\[
aH_C^+\subset P_A L^2(M),
\]
so
\[
aR_Ca^*\le P_A.
\]

Again, there is no \(e_B\)-error: centered \(A\)- or \(C\)-letters send \(L^2(B)\) into \(A\ominus B\) or \(C\ominus B\), not back into \(L^2(B)\).

---

## 6. Relative central-state contradiction

Let \(\varphi\) be an \(M\)-central state on \(S_{X_L}(M)\). Since \(a,c_1,c_2\in M\) are unitaries,
\[
\varphi(uTu^*)=\varphi(T)
\]
for \(u\in\{a,c_1,c_2\}\).

From
\[
c_1R_Ac_1^*+c_2R_Ac_2^*\le P_C,
\]
we get
\[
2\varphi(R_A)\le \varphi(P_C).
\]
From
\[
aR_Ca^*\le P_A,
\]
we get
\[
\varphi(R_C)\le \varphi(P_A).
\]

Set
\[
\alpha=\varphi(e_B),\qquad \beta=\varphi(P_A),\qquad \gamma=\varphi(P_C).
\]
Then
\[
\alpha,\beta,\gamma\ge 0,\qquad \alpha+\beta+\gamma=1,
\]
and
\[
\varphi(R_A)=\alpha+\beta,\qquad \varphi(R_C)=\alpha+\gamma.
\]
Thus
\[
2(\alpha+\beta)\le \gamma,\qquad \alpha+\gamma\le \beta.
\]
Combining,
\[
\beta\ge \alpha+\gamma\ge \alpha+2(\alpha+\beta)=3\alpha+2\beta,
\]
so
\[
0\ge 3\alpha+\beta.
\]
Hence \(\alpha=\beta=0\). Then \(\alpha+\gamma\le\beta\) gives \(\gamma=0\), contradicting
\[
\alpha+\beta+\gamma=1.
\]

Therefore there is no \(M\)-central state on \(S_{X_L}(M)\). In particular, \(M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}\) for every vertex \(v\).

---

## 7. No amenable direct summand

This step is important because one cannot prove nonamenability merely by taking two nonadjacent trace-zero unitaries: if both vertex algebras are \(\mathbb C^2\), two order-two unitaries generate \(L(\mathbb Z_2*\mathbb Z_2)\), which is amenable.

Instead, use the relative ping-pong result.

Suppose \(z\in Z(M)\) is nonzero and \(zM\) is amenable. Let \(H_z=zL^2(M)\cong L^2(zM)\). Amenability of \(zM\) gives a \(zM\)-central hypertrace \(\psi\) on \(B(H_z)\). For fixed \(v\), define
\[
\Phi(T)=\psi(zTz|_{H_z}),\qquad T\in S_{X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}}(M).
\]
Then \(\Phi\) is a state. For \(x\in M\),
\[
\Phi(xT)=\psi((zx)(zTz))
=\psi((zTz)(zx))
=\Phi(Tx),
\]
because \(zx\in zM\) and \(\psi\) is \(zM\)-central. Thus \(\Phi\) is an \(M\)-central state on \(S_{X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}}(M)\), contradicting Step 6.

Therefore \(M\) has no amenable direct summand.

---

## 8. Absolute obstruction state and compression

Assume for contradiction that \(M\) is not properly proximal. Since \(M\) has no amenable direct summand, the DKEP obstruction theorem gives an absolute obstruction state \(\Omega\), normal on \(M\), such that
\[
\Omega(q_K)=0.
\]

Now fix \(U\subset V\), and suppose \(M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X_U\). We claim
\[
\Omega(q_U)=1.
\]

Let
\[
r=1-q_U.
\]
If \(\Omega(r)>0\), define for \(T\in S_{X_U}(M)\)
\[
\Phi(T)=\frac{\Omega(rTr)}{\Omega(r)}.
\]
This is well-defined in the normal-bidual formulation because \(q_U\) supports the \(X_U\)-compact ideal. Since \(r\) commutes with the left and right \(M\)-actions,
\[
[rTr,\rho(x)]
=
r[T,\rho(x)]r
=
0
\]
for \(x\in M\). Hence \(rTr\) belongs to the absolute compactification on which \(\Omega\) is defined.

The functional \(\Phi\) is a state. It is \(M\)-central because \(r\) commutes with \(M\) and \(\Omega\) is \(M\)-central:
\[
\Phi(xT)
=
\frac{\Omega(x\,rTr)}{\Omega(r)}
=
\frac{\Omega(rTr\,x)}{\Omega(r)}
=
\Phi(Tx).
\]
Moreover, for \(x\in M_+\),
\[
0\le \Phi(x)
=
\frac{\Omega(rxr)}{\Omega(r)}
\le
\frac{\Omega(x)}{\Omega(r)}.
\]
Since \(\Omega|_M\) is normal, \(\Phi|_M\) is normal. This contradicts relative proper proximality to \(X_U\). Therefore
\[
\Omega(1-q_U)=0,
\]
so
\[
\Omega(q_U)=1.
\]

Applying this to \(U=\operatorname{Link}(v)\) for every \(v\in V\), Step 6 gives
\[
\Omega(q_{\operatorname{Link}(v)})=1
\qquad\forall v\in V.
\]

Hidden assumption repaired here: \(\Omega\) must be taken in the DKEP normal-bidual setting, not merely as an abstract state on a smaller \(C^*\)-algebra, so that the projections \(q_U\) and the compressions \(rTr\) are in its domain.

---

## 9. Final finite product contradiction

By the allowed support-product lemma,
\[
q_Uq_W=q_{U\cap W}.
\]
Thus the projections \(q_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}\) commute, and
\[
\prod_{v\in V} q_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}
=
q_{\bigcap_{v\in V}\operatorname{Link}(v)}.
\]

Because the graph is simplicial and loopless, no vertex belongs to its own link. Hence
\[
\bigcap_{v\in V}\operatorname{Link}(v)=\varnothing.
\]
Therefore
\[
\prod_{v\in V} q_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}
=
q_\varnothing.
\]

For \(U=\varnothing\), \(M_\varnothing=\mathbb C1\), and \(e_\varnothing\) is the rank-one projection onto \(\mathbb C\widehat 1\). The hereditary \(M\)-\(JMJ\) hull of \(e_\varnothing\) contains the finite-rank operators
\[
x e_\varnothing J y^*J,
\]
whose span is norm-dense in \(K(L^2(M))\). Hence
\[
q_\varnothing=q_K.
\]

Since \(\Omega(q_{\operatorname{Link}(v)})=1\) for every \(v\), and these projections commute, positivity gives
\[
\Omega\left(\prod_{v\in V} q_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}\right)=1.
\]
Indeed,
\[
1-\prod_v q_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}
\le
\sum_v \left(1-q_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}\right),
\]
so applying \(\Omega\) gives zero on the left.

Thus
\[
\Omega(q_K)=1,
\]
contradicting the obstruction-state property
\[
\Omega(q_K)=0.
\]

Therefore \(M\) is properly proximal.

---

## Hidden assumptions and repairs

1. **Irreducibility must mean no nontrivial join.**  
   Empty join pieces must be excluded.

2. **The graph product normal form is required.**  
   The AFP decomposition relies on the standard reduced-word theorem for graph products.

3. **The DKEP normal-bidual formulation is required.**  
   The compression argument needs \(\Omega\) to be defined on the support projections \(q_U\) and on \(rTr\).

4. **The support-product lemma must give products, not only meets.**  
   Since \(\Omega\) need not be normal on the whole normal bidual, knowing only a meet formula is insufficient. The commuting product formula fixes this.

5. **No free-Haar or diffuseness assumption is available.**  
   The proof only uses trace-zero unitaries and works for finite-dimensional vertices, including order-two unitaries.

With these conventions and the allowed support-product lemma, the proof is complete.

Your goal is to assemble a complete proof of the original theorem, but using only the weaker support sandwich q_U q_W q_U ≤ q_{U∩W}, not the full commuting product formula. Also red-team every non-support step from Task t6.

Assume as a lemma, only for this task, that for graph-product parabolic boundary supports q_U one has q_U q_W q_U ≤ q_{U∩W}. Do not assume q_U and q_W commute and do not assume q_U q_W=q_{U∩W}.

Required deliverables:

1. Write the final finite-cover upgrade carefully. Prove the elementary GNS fact: if φ is any state on a C*-algebra or von Neumann algebra and p is a projection with φ(p)=1, then φ(a)=φ(pap) for all a. Deduce that if φ(q_U)=φ(q_W)=1 and q_U q_W q_U ≤ q_{U∩W}, then φ(q_{U∩W})=1. Iterate this over the finite family U_v=Link(v) to get φ(q_empty)=1.

2. Check the DKEP obstruction/compression step. Starting from non-proper proximality and no amenable direct summand, state precisely the obstruction state Ω and why relative proper proximality to X_U forces Ω(q_U)=1. Make sure this does not use normality of Ω on the whole bidual.

3. Recheck the fixed-vertex AFP ping-pong lemma from Task t6. Verify the graph-combinatorial construction of a∈A⊖B and c_1,c_2∈C⊖B with E_B(c_i* c_j)=δ_ij 1, including the unique non-neighbor and order-two unitary cases. Verify the sector projections R_A,R_C,P_A,P_C lie in S_{X_Link(v)} and the projection inequalities have no hidden e_B error.

4. Recheck the no-amenable-direct-summand step. If zM is amenable for a nonzero central projection z, show carefully that a hypertrace on B(zL2(M)) gives an M-central state on S_{X_Link(v)} normal on M, contradicting the relative ping-pong result.

5. Conclude the original theorem under the support sandwich lemma. If you find any gap outside the support sandwich itself, isolate it precisely and propose a repair.

writeup_tasks.statement: Finite-cover upgrade from link-relative proper proximality using only a DKEP support sandwich inequality
writeup_tasks.writeup_agent_prompt:
Produce a fully self-contained write-up of the following abstract finite-cover lemma, independent of the solver-history context.

Claim to prove: Let (M,τ) be a separable tracial von Neumann algebra in the DKEP properly proximal framework. Suppose there is a finite collection of DKEP boundary pieces indexed by sets U_1,...,U_n, with support projections q_U in the normal-bidual compactification for every finite intersection U of the U_i. Assume q_empty is the compact support q_K. Assume also the support sandwich inequality q_U q_W q_U ≤ q_{U∩W} for all such finite intersections U,W. If M has no amenable direct summand, and M is properly proximal relative to each boundary piece X_{U_i}, then M is properly proximal.

Your proof must define every object it uses: DKEP boundary piece, support projection q_U at the level needed for the argument, compact support q_K, relative proper proximality, and the obstruction-state criterion. You may state the DKEP obstruction theorem as a named black box: if M has no amenable direct summand and is not properly proximal, there exists an M-central obstruction state Ω in the normal-bidual compactification, normal on M, with Ω(q_K)=0; and if M is properly proximal relative to X_U, then any such Ω satisfies Ω(q_U)=1.

The key point is to avoid assuming q_U and q_W commute. Include a proof of the elementary state-support fact: if φ is a state and p is a projection with φ(p)=1, then φ(a)=φ(pap) for all a. Then show that φ(q_U)=φ(q_W)=1 and q_U q_W q_U≤q_{U∩W} imply φ(q_{U∩W})=1. Iterate this to obtain Ω(q_empty)=1, contradicting Ω(q_K)=0.

This write-up is only for the finite-cover upgrade lemma, not the full graph-product theorem.

I will run one focused red-team on the support sandwich and one broader final-proof audit, while also commissioning a full self-contained write-up based on t7 and t8.
