Below is a **dependency-checked proof blueprint**, not polished final prose. The theorem-level assembly is sound once the **t9 support sandwich**
\[
q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_{U\cap W}
\]
and the stated DKEP normal-bidual obstruction package are imported.

---

## A. Conventions and objects

### A1. Irreducibility convention

Use the following precise convention:

> A finite simplicial graph \(\Gamma=(V,E)\) is **irreducible** if there is no partition
> \[
> V=V_1\sqcup V_2,\qquad V_1,V_2\ne\varnothing,
> \]
> such that every vertex of \(V_1\) is adjacent to every vertex of \(V_2\).

Equivalently, \(\Gamma\) has no **nontrivial join decomposition**. If one phrases this using subgraphs \(\Gamma_1,\Gamma_2\), the vertex sets must be **nonempty join factors**; otherwise an empty factor makes the condition vacuous. Since the graph is simplicial, it has no loops, and \(\operatorname{Link}(v)\) excludes \(v\). Caspers–Fima use the same loopless simplicial convention and define \(\operatorname{Star}(v)=\operatorname{Link}(v)\cup\{v\}\). ([webusers.imj-prg.fr](https://webusers.imj-prg.fr/~pierre.fima/Documents/GraphProducts.pdf))

Consequences needed later:

1. **\(\operatorname{Star}(v)\ne V\) for every \(v\).**  
   If \(\operatorname{Star}(v)=V\), then
   \[
   V=\{v\}\sqcup (V\setminus\{v\})
   \]
   is a nontrivial join.

2. Hence
   \[
   N(v):=V\setminus\operatorname{Star}(v)\ne\varnothing.
   \]

3. If \(N(v)=\{w\}\), then \(\operatorname{Link}(v)\ne\varnothing\) because \(|V|\ge3\). Moreover, there exists \(z\in\operatorname{Link}(v)\) not adjacent to \(w\); otherwise
   \[
   V=\{v,w\}\sqcup \operatorname{Link}(v)
   \]
   would be a nontrivial join.

---

## B. External dependency list

### B1. Graph-product facts required

Let \(M=\star_\Gamma(M_v,\tau_v)\) be the tracial graph product.

Needed graph-product inputs:

1. **Reduced-word dense algebra and parabolic expectations.**  
   The linear span of \(1\) and reduced graph-product words is weakly dense in \(M\). For every induced subgraph \(U\subset V\), there is a trace-preserving conditional expectation
   \[
   E_U:M\to M_U
   \]
   killing every reduced word containing a vertex outside \(U\). ([webusers.imj-prg.fr](https://webusers.imj-prg.fr/~pierre.fima/Documents/GraphProducts.pdf))

2. **Star-link AFP decomposition.**  
   For each \(v\in V\), with
   \[
   L=\operatorname{Link}(v),\quad B=M_L,\quad A=M_{\operatorname{Star}(v)},\quad C=M_{V\setminus\{v\}},
   \]
   one has
   \[
   A\cong M_v\bar\otimes B,\qquad E_B|_A=\tau_v\otimes\operatorname{id}_B,
   \]
   and
   \[
   M=A*_B C.
   \]
   This is exactly the star-link amalgamated free product decomposition. ([webusers.imj-prg.fr](https://webusers.imj-prg.fr/~pierre.fima/Documents/GraphProducts.pdf))

3. **Relative reduced-word conditional expectation.**  
   For the support-sandwich proof, one needs the conditional relative Wick formula: if \(x,\tilde x\) are graph-reduced relative to \((V_1,V_2)\), then
   \[
   E_{V_2}(\tilde x^*yx)=\tau(\tilde x^*x)E_U(y),\qquad y\in M_{V_1},
   \]
   where \(U\subset V_1\cap V_2\) is the set of vertices adjacent to the relevant middle word; if the relative words are not equivalent, the scalar is \(0\). This is Lemma 3.17 in the graph-product structure paper. ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743))

4. **\(q_\varnothing=q_K\).**  
   This is elementary from DKEP’s boundary-piece definition: for \(U=\varnothing\), \(M_\varnothing=\mathbb C1\), and \(e_\varnothing\) is the rank-one projection onto \(\mathbb C\widehat1\). The hereditary \(M\)-\(JMJ\) hull of \(e_\varnothing\) is exactly \(K(L^2M)\), since
   \[
   L_a e_\varnothing L_b^*=\theta_{\widehat a,\widehat b}
   \]
   and \(M\widehat1\) is dense in \(L^2M\). DKEP define subalgebra boundary pieces as the hereditary algebra generated by \(xJyJe_B\)-type operators. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

---

### B2. DKEP / DE facts required

Use the following DKEP package.

1. **Boundary pieces and \(S_X(M)\).**  
   For an \(M\)-boundary piece \(X\subset B(L^2M)\), define
   \[
   S_X(M)=\{T\in B(L^2M):[T,JxJ]\in K_X^{\infty,1}(M)\ \forall x\in M\}.
   \]
   DKEP prove \(K_X^{\infty,1}(M)=\overline{K_X(M)}^{\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}}\) and that it is a strong \(M\)-\(M\) and \(JMJ\)-\(JMJ\) bimodule, so the commutator condition can be checked on a von Neumann generating set. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

2. **Relative proper proximality.**  
   For the inclusion \(M\subset M\), \(M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X\) iff there is no \(M\)-central state on \(S_X(M)\) whose restriction to \(M\) is normal. DKEP formulate relative proper proximality in this way. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

3. **Normal-bidual formulation.**  
   Let
   \[
   \widetilde S_X(M)\subset (B(L^2M)^\sharp_J)^*
   \]
   be the normal-bidual compactification. DE/DKEP give the characterization:
   \[
   M\text{ properly proximal relative to }X
   \iff
   \widetilde S_X(M)\text{ has no }M\text{-central state normal on }M.
   \]
   ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2211.05298))

4. **Support projections.**  
   For each boundary piece \(X\), let \(q_X\) be the identity/support projection of the normal-bidual compact corner \((K_X(M)^\sharp_J)^*\). It commutes with both \(M\) and \(JMJ\). DKEP explicitly record this commutation. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

5. **Compression inclusion.**  
   DE record the useful normal-bidual compression fact
   \[
   q_X^\perp\,\widetilde S_X(M)\,q_X^\perp
   \subset
   q_K^\perp\,\widetilde S(M),
   \]
   using that \(q_X\) commutes with \(JMJ\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2211.05298))

6. **Obstruction state.**  
   Required final external input:
   > If \(M\) is separable tracial, has no amenable direct summand, and is not properly proximal, then there exists an \(M\)-central state
   > \[
   > \Omega:\widetilde S(M)\to\mathbb C
   > \]
   > normal on \(M\), such that
   > \[
   > \Omega(q_K)=0.
   > \]
   
   This is the normal-bidual version of the DKEP compact-corner obstruction; it is the only major DKEP theorem-level input beyond definitions.

7. **Support-sandwich lemma from t9.**  
   For all \(U,W\subset V\),
   \[
   q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_{U\cap W}.
   \]
   Internally, t9 uses DE’s approximate-unit lemma and \(e_N\)-commutation with \(p_{\mathrm{nor}}\), plus the relative graph-product expectation formula. DE’s Lemmas 3.7 and 3.8 give the approximate-unit convergence and \(e_N\)-commutation inputs. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2211.05298))

---

## C. Proof blueprint

### Step 1. Setup and separability

Because \(V\) is finite and each \(M_v\) is separable tracial, the graph product \(M\) is separable tracial: take countable \(\|\cdot\|_2\)-dense subsets in each \(M_v\), form countably many reduced words, and use weak/\(\|\cdot\|_2\)-density.

Thus the DKEP separability hypotheses apply.

---

### Step 2. Fixed-vertex AFP decomposition

Fix \(v\in V\). Set
\[
L=\operatorname{Link}(v),\quad B=M_L,\quad A=M_{\operatorname{Star}(v)},\quad C=M_{V\setminus\{v\}}.
\]
Then
\[
M=A*_B C,\qquad A\cong M_v\bar\otimes B.
\]

Choose a trace-zero unitary \(u_v\in M_v\), and set
\[
a=u_v\otimes1_B\in A.
\]
Then \(a\) is unitary and \(E_B(a)=0\), so \(a\in A^\circ:=\ker E_B|_A\).

---

### Step 3. Construct two \(B\)-orthogonal \(C^\circ\)-unitaries

Need unitaries \(c_1,c_2\in C\) such that
\[
E_B(c_i)=0,\qquad E_B(c_i^*c_j)=\delta_{ij}1.
\]

#### Case 1: \(|N(v)|\ge2\)

Pick distinct \(w_1,w_2\in N(v)\), and trace-zero unitaries \(u_{w_i}\in M_{w_i}\). Set
\[
c_i=u_{w_i}.
\]
Then \(c_i\in C^\circ\), \(E_B(c_i^*c_i)=1\), and \(E_B(c_1^*c_2)=0\) because the reduced word contains vertices outside \(L\).

#### Case 2: \(N(v)=\{w\}\)

By irreducibility, choose \(z\in L\) not adjacent to \(w\). Pick trace-zero unitaries \(u_w\in M_w\) and \(u_z\in M_z\subset B\). Set
\[
c_1=u_w,\qquad c_2=u_z u_w.
\]
Then \(c_1,c_2\in C^\circ\), and
\[
c_1^*c_2=u_w^*u_z u_w
\]
is a graph-reduced word of type \(w,z,w\), because \(w\) and \(z\) are not adjacent. Therefore its \(B\)-expectation is zero. This also handles order-two unitaries: the middle noncommuting \(u_z\) prevents cancellation.

---

### Step 4. AFP sector projections lie in \(S_{X_L}(M)\)

Use the AFP \(L^2\)-decomposition
\[
L^2(M)=L^2(B)\oplus
\bigoplus_{\epsilon_1\ne\cdots\ne\epsilon_n}
K_{\epsilon_1}\otimes_B\cdots\otimes_B K_{\epsilon_n},
\]
where
\[
K_A=L^2(A)\ominus L^2(B),\qquad K_C=L^2(C)\ominus L^2(B).
\]

Let:

- \(R_A\): projection onto \(L^2(B)\) plus words whose first AFP letter is \(A\);
- \(R_C\): projection onto \(L^2(B)\) plus words whose first AFP letter is \(C\);
- \(P_A=R_A-e_B\);
- \(P_C=R_C-e_B\).

Then
\[
1=e_B+P_A+P_C.
\]

For \(d\in A^\circ\), right multiplication by \(d\) preserves \(R_A L^2(M)\) and its orthogonal complement, so
\[
[R_A,\rho(d)]=0,\qquad \rho(d)=Jd^*J.
\]
Similarly, for \(d\in C^\circ\),
\[
[R_C,\rho(d)]=0.
\]
Right multiplication by \(B\) preserves every AFP sector. Since
\[
R_C=1-R_A+e_B,\qquad R_A=1-R_C+e_B,
\]
the only commutator errors are \([e_B,\rho(d)]\), which lie in \(K_{X_L}^{\infty,1}(M)\) because \(e_B\in X_L\) and \(JMJ\) acts by multipliers.

By the DKEP generator criterion:
\[
R_A,R_C,P_A,P_C\in S_{X_L}(M).
\]

---

### Step 5. Ping-pong projection inequalities

Let
\[
H_A^+=R_A L^2(M),\qquad H_C^+=R_C L^2(M).
\]

If \(d\in C^\circ\), then left multiplication by \(d\) sends \(H_A^+\) into \(P_C L^2(M)\). Hence
\[
c_iR_Ac_i^*\le P_C.
\]
Because \(E_B(c_1^*c_2)=0\), the same argument applied to \(d=c_1^*c_2\in C^\circ\) gives
\[
c_1H_A^+\perp c_2H_A^+.
\]
Thus
\[
c_1R_Ac_1^*+c_2R_Ac_2^*\le P_C.
\]

Similarly, since \(a\in A^\circ\),
\[
aR_Ca^*\le P_A.
\]

---

### Step 6. No central state on \(S_{X_L}(M)\)

Let \(\varphi\) be an \(M\)-central state on \(S_{X_L}(M)\). Since \(a,c_1,c_2\in M\) are unitaries,
\[
\varphi(uTu^*)=\varphi(T).
\]

From the inequalities:
\[
2\varphi(R_A)\le \varphi(P_C),
\qquad
\varphi(R_C)\le \varphi(P_A).
\]
Set
\[
\alpha=\varphi(e_B),\quad \beta=\varphi(P_A),\quad \gamma=\varphi(P_C).
\]
Then
\[
\alpha+\beta+\gamma=1,
\]
and
\[
2(\alpha+\beta)\le \gamma,\qquad \alpha+\gamma\le \beta.
\]
Combining:
\[
\beta\ge \alpha+\gamma\ge \alpha+2(\alpha+\beta)=3\alpha+2\beta,
\]
so \(\alpha=\beta=0\), and then \(\gamma=0\), contradiction.

Therefore \(S_{X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}}(M)\) has no \(M\)-central state. In particular,
\[
M\text{ is properly proximal relative to }X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}
\]
for every \(v\in V\).

---

### Step 7. No amenable direct summand

Assume \(0\ne z\in Z(M)\) and \(zM\) is amenable. Let
\[
H_z=zL^2(M)\cong L^2(zM).
\]
Amenability gives a \(zM\)-central hypertrace \(\psi\) on \(B(H_z)\) extending the normalized trace on \(zM\).

Fix \(v\), and set \(L=\operatorname{Link}(v)\). For \(T\in S_{X_L}(M)\), define
\[
\Phi(T)=\psi(zTz|_{H_z}).
\]
Then \(\Phi\) is a state. For \(x\in M\),
\[
\Phi(xT)=\psi((zx)(zTz))=\psi((zTz)(zx))=\Phi(Tx),
\]
so \(\Phi\) is \(M\)-central. Also
\[
\Phi(x)=\psi(zxz)=\tau_z(zx),
\]
so \(\Phi|_M\) is normal.

This contradicts Step 6. Hence \(M\) has no amenable direct summand.

---

### Step 8. DKEP obstruction and compression to link supports

Assume, for contradiction, that \(M\) is not properly proximal. Since \(M\) has no amenable direct summand, DKEP gives an \(M\)-central state
\[
\Omega:\widetilde S(M)\to\mathbb C
\]
normal on \(M\), with
\[
\Omega(q_K)=0.
\]

Now prove the compression claim:

> If \(M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X\), then \(\Omega(q_X)=1\).

Let \(r=1-q_X\). If \(\Omega(r)>0\), define for \(T\in\widetilde S_X(M)\)
\[
\Phi(T)=\frac{\Omega(rTr)}{\Omega(r)}.
\]
This is well-defined because
\[
r\widetilde S_X(M)r\subset \widetilde S(M).
\]
It is \(M\)-central since \(r\) commutes with \(M\). For \(x\in M_+\),
\[
0\le r x r\le x,
\]
so
\[
0\le \Phi(x)\le \frac{\Omega(x)}{\Omega(r)}.
\]
Thus \(\Phi|_M\) is normal by domination by a normal positive functional.

This contradicts relative proper proximality to \(X\). Hence \(\Omega(q_X)=1\).

Applying this to \(X=X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}\):
\[
\Omega(q_{\operatorname{Link}(v)})=1
\qquad\forall v\in V.
\]

---

### Step 9. Finite-cover step using only the one-sided sandwich

Let \(\phi\) be any state on the ambient normal-bidual \(C^*\)-algebra or von Neumann algebra containing the \(q_U\)’s. If \(\phi\) is initially only a state on the operator system \(\widetilde S(M)\), extend it to the ambient \(C^*\)-algebra first.

If \(\phi(q_U)=1\), then in the GNS representation,
\[
\pi(q_U)\xi=\xi.
\]
Therefore, if also \(\phi(q_W)=1\),
\[
1=\phi(q_W)=\phi(q_Uq_Wq_U).
\]
By the support sandwich,
\[
0\le q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_{U\cap W}.
\]
Hence
\[
1\le \phi(q_{U\cap W})\le1,
\]
so
\[
\phi(q_{U\cap W})=1.
\]

Iterating over the finite family
\[
U_v=\operatorname{Link}(v),\qquad v\in V,
\]
gives
\[
\Omega\left(q_{\bigcap_{v\in V}\operatorname{Link}(v)}\right)=1.
\]
Since the graph has no loops,
\[
\bigcap_{v\in V}\operatorname{Link}(v)=\varnothing.
\]
Therefore
\[
\Omega(q_\varnothing)=1.
\]
But \(q_\varnothing=q_K\), so
\[
1=\Omega(q_\varnothing)=\Omega(q_K),
\]
contradicting \(\Omega(q_K)=0\).

Thus \(M\) is properly proximal.

---

## D. Remaining phrasing pitfalls for the final write-up

1. **Do not allow empty join factors.**  
   State irreducibility as “no nontrivial join partition into two nonempty parts.”

2. **Do not claim support projections commute.**  
   The finite-cover step uses only
   \[
   q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_{U\cap W}.
   \]

3. **Do not claim \(X_UX_W\subset X_{U\cap W}\).**  
   t9 only proves the special triple-product support sandwich.

4. **Do not use global multiplicativity of \(\iota_{\mathrm{nor}}\).**  
   In t9, multiplicativity is only used on \(C^*(M,JMJ,e_U,e_W)\), where \(p_{\mathrm{nor}}\) commutes with the generators.

5. **Keep concrete \(S_X(M)\) and normal-bidual \(\widetilde S_X(M)\) separate.**  
   Ping-pong is concrete; the obstruction/compression step is normal-bidual.

6. **In the finite-cover step, extend operator-system states before using GNS.**

7. **In the amenable-summand step, normalize the trace on \(zM\).**  
   The hypertrace lives on \(B(zL^2M)\), and compression by \(z\) makes the resulting state \(M\)-central.

8. **In the unique non-neighbor case, explicitly choose \(z\in\operatorname{Link}(v)\) not adjacent to \(w\).**  
   This is what prevents cancellation in \(u_w^*u_zu_w\).

9. **Prove \(q_\varnothing=q_K\), not merely \(q_\varnothing\ge q_K\).**  
   The boundary piece for \(M_\varnothing=\mathbb C\) is exactly \(K(L^2M)\).

10. **State the DKEP compact-corner obstruction explicitly.**  
    The contradiction requires an obstruction state satisfying \(\Omega(q_K)=0\), not just an arbitrary central state.

---

First, clarify the irreducibility convention in the problem: no nontrivial join decomposition into two nonempty vertex sets with all cross edges. Prove Star(v) is not all of V for every vertex, prove the unique non-neighbor lemma used to construct c_2, and check edge cases when |V|=3. Second, verify that the tracial graph product M is a nonamenable II1 factor, or at least has no amenable direct summand and is not weakly compact for the DKEP obstruction. You may cite the Rigid Graph Products theorem if it exactly applies, but state its hypotheses precisely and use web search if necessary. writeup_tasks.statement: Let Gamma be a finite simplicial graph with at least three vertices, irreducible in the standard nontrivial-join sense, and let (M_v, tau_v) be separable tracial von Neumann algebras such that each M_v contains a unitary u_v with tau_v(u_v)=0. Then the tracial graph product von Neumann algebra M_Gamma is properly proximal.
writeup_tasks.writeup_agent_prompt: Produce a fully self-contained proof of the stated theorem. The artifact must stand alone: define the standard nontrivial-join meaning of irreducible, define Link(v), Star(v), parabolic subalgebras M_U, the tracial graph product notation, and the DKEP boundary pieces X_U associated with M_U. State proper proximality in the DKEP operator-system sense. You may cite standard external facts, but state them precisely before using them: graph-product reduced words and expectations, the star-link amalgamated free product decomposition M = M_Star(v) *_M_Link(v) M_{V minus {v}}, the relative graph-product conditional expectation formula needed for finite propagation, and the DKEP normal-bidual obstruction theorem. The proof must contain the following sections. Section 1: graph consequences of irreducibility, including Star(v) != V and the unique non-neighbor lemma. Section 2: fixed-vertex link-relative proper proximality. For B = M_Link(v), A = M_Star(v), C = M_{V minus {v}}, use the AFP decomposition, define the first-letter sector projections R_A, R_C and P_A, P_C, show they belong to S_{X_B}(M), construct a in A^circ and B-orthogonal C^circ unitaries c_1,c_2 including the unique non-neighbor case c_1 = u_w, c_2 = u_z u_w, prove the projection inequalities c_1 R_A c_1^* + c_2 R_A c_2^* <= P_C and a R_C a^* <= P_A, and derive the contradiction for any M-central state. Section 3: no amenable direct summand and the exact DKEP obstruction setup; if you cite a graph-product factor theorem, state why its hypotheses apply, and still explain why weak compactness is excluded. Section 4: prove the support-sandwich lemma q_U q_W q_U <= q_{U cap W} for parabolic boundary supports. Use the repaired t11 proof: algebraic hereditary cores, finite functional-calculus approximate units, restricted multiplicativity only on C*(M, JMJ, e_U, e_W), finite propagation e_S C e_T through e_J with J subset S cap T, the stable algebraic corner class Z_I, and the weak-star order-limit argument. Do not claim global multiplicativity of the normal-bidual map and do not claim X_U X_W subset X_{U cap W}. Section 5: finite-cover contradiction. From a non-properly-proximal obstruction state Omega with Omega(q_K)=0, use relative proper proximality to show Omega(q_Link(v))=1 for every vertex v by compression with 1 - q_Link(v). Then use the GNS support-one argument plus q_U q_W q_U <= q_{U cap W} to iterate over all links. Since the graph has no loops, the intersection of all links is empty. Prove q_empty = q_K because M_empty = C and the hereditary hull of the vacuum projection is K(L2 M). Conclude Omega(q_K)=1, contradiction. The write-up must not refer to prior tasks, the KB, or solver history. Avoid stronger unsupported claims such as q_U q_W = q_{U cap W} or commuting supports.

plan.reasoning: Tasks t13 and t14 close the main final-proof dependency gaps. We now know the graph irreducibility convention gives Star(v) != V, handles the unique non-neighbor construction, and gives no amenable direct summand, either via the known graph-product factor theorem or directly from relative ping-pong. Task t13 also repaired the DKEP obstruction step: avoid a bare trivial-action use of Lemma 8.6, use the normal-bidual characterization plus no amenable direct summand, prove Omega(q_X)=1 by compression, extend states before GNS, and note q_empty=q_K. The only component still worth another solver pass is the support-sandwich proof q_U q_W q_U <= q_{U cap W}, because it contains the delicate normal-bidual multiplicativity and finite-propagation arguments. In parallel, a final assembly audit can check that no old overclaims, such as global multiplicativity or q_U q_W equality, remain. A self-contained final write-up should now be attempted.

new_proven_results.statement: The graph-theoretic prerequisites for the AFP ping-pong construction are settled under the standard nontrivial-join interpretation of irreducibility.
new_proven_results.sketch: Irreducibility implies Star(v) is never all of V. If v has a unique non-neighbor w, then there is z in Link(v) not adjacent to w, so c1=u_w and c2=u_z u_w are B-orthogonal C-unitaries even in order-two and |V|=3 cases.

new_proven_results.statement: The proof may assume M has no amenable direct summand.
new_proven_results.sketch: This follows either from the known rigid graph-product theorem giving a nonamenable II1 factor under the hypotheses, or directly because an amenable central summand would yield an M-central state on any link-relative boundary operator system, contradicting the link ping-pong lemma.

new_proven_results.statement: The final DKEP obstruction step must use the general normal-bidual characterization, with an obstruction state Omega satisfying Omega(q_K)=0.
new_proven_results.sketch: Non-proper-proximality plus no amenable direct summand yields an M-central normal-on-M state on the absolute normal-bidual system vanishing on the compact support. Relative proper proximality to X then forces Omega(q_X)=1 by compressing with 1-q_X; positive mass there would contradict relative proper proximality.

new_proven_results.statement: The finite-cover GNS argument is valid after extending the obstruction state to the ambient C*-algebra containing the support projections.
new_proven_results.sketch: If a state has value one on projections q_U and q_W, then its GNS vector is supported by both, so it has value one on q_U q_W q_U. The sandwich inequality then forces value one on q_{U cap W}; iterating over all links gives value one on q_empty=q_K.

new_proven_results.statement: The empty parabolic boundary piece is the compact boundary.
new_proven_results.sketch: For U=empty, e_U is the rank-one projection onto C hat{1}; its left-right translates span finite-rank operators on a dense subspace of L2(M), hence X_empty=K(L2M) and q_empty=q_K.

new_failed_attempts.approach: Apply DKEP Lemma 8.6 directly to the trivial action without checking center-ergodicity or factoriality.
new_failed_attempts.reason: The action version inherits a center-ergodicity hypothesis, so the safer route is the general normal-bidual characterization plus no amenable direct summand.

new_failed_attempts.approach: Run the final GNS support-one argument on an operator-system state without extension.
new_failed_attempts.reason: Products of the support projections live in an ambient C*- or von Neumann algebra, so the state must first be extended there.

new_failed_attempts.approach: Read irreducibility with empty join factors allowed.
new_failed_attempts.reason: That would make the condition vacuous or impossible; the theorem uses the standard no nontrivial join partition convention.

new_bottlenecks: The support-sandwich proof must be stated in a referee-grade way, especially the finite-propagation lemma, the algebraic approximate-unit support convergence, and the restricted multiplicativity of the normal-bidual embedding.
The final write-up must not conflate concrete S_X(M) with the normal-bidual tilde S_X(M), and must not claim global multiplicativity or q_U q_W=q_{U cap W}.

task_assignments.solver_prompt: You are a hostile referee and repair agent for the support-sandwich lemma. The attached outputs t11, t9, and t13 contain the current proof attempt and the normal-bidual cautions. Your task is to produce a referee-grade statement and proof of the following lemma, or identify a precise remaining gap and the weakest extra hypothesis needed to close it: Let M be a finite tracial graph product over a finite graph, and for each vertex subset U let X_U be the DKEP boundary piece associated with the parabolic subalgebra M_U and q_U the support projection of its normal-bidual compact corner. For U,W subset V and I=U cap W, prove q_U q_W q_U <= q_I. You must avoid claiming global multiplicativity of the normal-bidual map and avoid claiming q_U q_W=q_I. You should explicitly state the DE/DKEP inputs you use: support projections as weak-star limits of suitable algebraic approximate units, commutation of Jones projections with the normal support projection, and multiplicativity only on the C*-algebra generated by M, JMJ, e_U, e_W when justified by the reducing projection. Give a detailed finite-propagation argument: for algebraic left-right multipliers C, explain why e_S C e_T factors through finitely many e_J with J subset S cap T, using graph-product reduced-word or conditional-expectation normal forms. Then explain how this implies h_U h_W h_U lies in the I-boundary compact corner for the chosen approximate units, and how weak-star order limits give q_U q_W q_U <= q_I. End with a SOLVER_REPORT listing proven results, failed attempts, and any caveats.

## Task t11 — Hostile referee audit of the support-sandwich proof
[unverified self-report — verification not run or not yet complete | self_declared_is_relaxation: False | self_declared_problem_solved (the agent's own claim, NOT verified): Prove the following statement:

Let $\Gamma$ be a finite simplicial graph with $|\VV(\GG)|\geq 3$, and $(M_v,\varphi_v)$ be a separable tracial von Neumann algebra for each $v\in \VV$. If $\Gamma$ is irreducible (in the sense that there does not exists subgraphs $\GG_1,\GG_2$ such that $ \VV(\GG_1)\cup \VV(\GG_2)=\VV(\GG) $ and every $(v,u)\in \EE(\GG) $ for all $v\in\VV(\GG_1),u\in \VV(\GG_2)$), and each $M_v$ has a trace zero unitary, then the graph product von Neumann algebra $M$ is properly proximal.]
Source: task_outputs.jsonl — raw agent output. The values in the metadata line above are self-declared and have not been checked. Treat the proof as a draft, not as established.
## Verdict

The support-sandwich lemma is **sound**, but I would **not accept t9 verbatim**. The dangerous part is the triple-product paragraph: it informally claims that the algebraic \(U\)- and \(W\)-corners multiply all of \(X_{U\cap W}\) into itself. That is stronger than what was proved and is not needed. The proof is repairable by working with a smaller algebraic corner class \(\mathcal Z_{U\cap W}\subset X_{U\cap W}\) that is stable under the specific algebraic words used by the approximate units.

Below is the corrected referee-level proof.

---

## 1. Restricted multiplicativity of \(\iota_{\rm nor}\)

Let \(H=L^2(M)\), \(\iota:B(H)\to B(H)^{**}\) be the universal representation, and
\[
\iota_{\rm nor}(T)=p_{\rm nor}\iota(T)p_{\rm nor}.
\]
The exact hypothesis needed is:

\[
p_{\rm nor}\in \iota\!\left(C^*(M,JMJ,e_U,e_W)\right)'.
\]

Under this hypothesis, \(\iota_{\rm nor}\) is a \(*\)-homomorphism on \(C^*(M,JMJ,e_U,e_W)\), since
\[
p\iota(xy)p=p\iota(x)\iota(y)p=p\iota(x)p\iota(y)p.
\]

It is **not** enough to use vague multiplicativity of the normal bidual map; DKEP explicitly note that the canonical embedding into the normal envelope is generally not multiplicative because \(p_{\rm nor}\) need not be central. However, in the present situation the needed commutation is available: \(p_{\rm nor}\) commutes with the universal images of \(M\) and \(JMJ\), and DE Lemma 3.8 gives commutation with \(\iota(e_N)\) for every von Neumann subalgebra \(N\subset M\), hence with \(\iota(e_U)\) and \(\iota(e_W)\). DE also formulate \(\iota_{\rm nor}=\operatorname{Ad}(p_{\rm nor})\circ\iota\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2211.05298))

So the restricted multiplicativity step is valid, provided it is stated exactly as above.

---

## 2. Algebraic hereditary core and convergence to \(q_U\)

Let \(\mathcal A\subset M\) be the algebraic graph-product \(*\)-algebra spanned by reduced Wick words. For \(S\subset V\), set
\[
\mathscr S_S=\{L_aR_b e_S:a,b\in\mathcal A\},
\]
and let \(D_S\) be the hereditary \(C^*\)-subalgebra generated by \(\mathscr S_S\).

DKEP’s subalgebra boundary piece \(X_S\) is the hereditary \(C^*\)-subalgebra generated by \(xJyJe_S\), equivalently by left-right translates of the large corner \(e_SB(H)e_S\). They define
\[
K_{X_S}^{\infty,1}(M)=\overline{X_S}^{\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}},
\]
and prove that this agrees with the \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-closure of \(K_{X_S}(M)\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

The algebraic core \(D_S\) is \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-dense in \(K_{X_S}^{\infty,1}(M)\). Indeed, for bounded algebraic approximants \(a\to x\), \(b\to y\) in \(\|\cdot\|_2\),
\[
\|L_xR_y e_S-L_aR_b e_S\|_{\infty,2}
\le
\|y\|_\infty\|x-a\|_2+\|a\|_\infty\|y-b\|_2,
\]
and the same estimate holds for adjoints. Hence finite sums
\[
(L_xR_y e_S)\,T\,(L_{x'}R_{y'}e_S)^*
\]
are \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-approximated by the corresponding algebraic sums. The large corner \(e_SB(H)e_S\) causes no problem, because \(e_S=L_1R_1e_S\in\mathscr S_S\), so \(e_STe_S\in D_S\) exactly.

For finite \(F\subset \mathscr S_S\) and \(\varepsilon>0\), put
\[
A_{F,S}=\sum_{s\in F}ss^*,\qquad
h^S_{F,\varepsilon}=A_{F,S}(A_{F,S}+\varepsilon)^{-1}.
\]
This is a positive contractive approximate unit for \(D_S\). Each \(h^S_{F,\varepsilon}\in C^*(M,JMJ,e_S)\), so its universal image commutes with \(p_{\rm nor}\). DE Lemma 3.7 then applies and gives
\[
\iota_{\rm nor}(h^S_{F,\varepsilon})\xrightarrow{\ \sigma\text{-weak}\ }q_S,
\]
where \(q_S\) is the identity/support projection of \((K_{X_S}(M)^\sharp_J)^*\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2211.05298))

Thus the support convergence part is correct.

---

## 3. Finite propagation

A slightly sharper finite-propagation statement is the right one.

Let
\[
\mathcal C_S=\operatorname{span}\{L_aR_b:a,b\in\mathcal A_S\},
\qquad
\mathcal C=\mathcal C_V.
\]
Then for all \(S,T\subset V\),
\[
e_S\mathcal C e_T
\subset
\operatorname{span}\{\mathcal C_S e_J\mathcal C_T:J\subset S\cap T\}.
\tag{FP}
\]

Proof for one reduced Wick term \(L_aR_b\): write relative normal forms
\[
a=a_Sa_0a_T,\qquad b=b_Tb_0b_S,
\]
where \(a_0\) is reduced relative to \((S,T)\), and \(b_0\) is reduced relative to \((T,S)\). For \(z\in\mathcal A_T\),
\[
E_S(azb)=a_S\,E_S\bigl(a_0(a_Tzb_T)b_0\bigr)\,b_S.
\]
Apply the relative conditional expectation formula with \(V_1=T\), \(V_2=S\), \(x=b_0\), \(\tilde x=a_0^*\), and \(y=a_Tzb_T\in M_T\). If the middle words are inequivalent, the scalar is \(0\). If they are equivalent,
\[
E_S\bigl(a_0(a_Tzb_T)b_0\bigr)
=
\tau(a_0b_0)E_J(a_Tzb_T),
\]
where \(J\subset S\cap T\) consists of the vertices adjacent to all letters of the middle word. The relative normal form and the conditional expectation formula are exactly Lemmas 3.12 and 3.17 in the graph-product structure paper. ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743))

Therefore
\[
e_SL_aR_be_T
=
\tau(a_0b_0)\,
L_{a_S}R_{b_S}\,e_J\,L_{a_T}R_{b_T}.
\]
The terminal \(e_T\) may be omitted because \(J\subset T\) and \(a_T,b_T\in M_T\), so
\[
e_JL_{a_T}R_{b_T}=e_JL_{a_T}R_{b_T}e_T.
\]
Linearity gives (FP).

---

## 4. Triple products: repaired argument

This is the only genuinely underwritten part of t9. The statement
\[
\mathcal Y_U^0\mathcal Y_W^0\mathcal Y_U^0\subset X_I
\]
is correct after replacing the informal multiplier argument by the following algebraic-corner argument. Let \(I=U\cap W\).

Define
\[
\mathcal P_S
=
\operatorname{span}\{A_0e_SA_1e_S\cdots e_SA_n:n\ge1,\ A_i\in\mathcal C\}.
\]
The approximate-unit elements \(h^S_{F,\varepsilon}\) lie in the norm closure of \(\mathcal P_S\), because \(A_{F,S}=\sum ss^*\in\mathcal P_S\), \(t(t+\varepsilon)^{-1}\) is uniformly approximable by polynomials with zero constant term, and \(\mathcal P_S\) is an algebra.

Now define the algebraic \(I\)-corner class
\[
\mathcal Z_I
=
\operatorname{span}\{A e_J T e_K B:
A,B\in\mathcal C,\ J,K\subset I,\ T\in B(H)\}.
\]
Since \(e_J,e_K\le e_I\), we have
\[
e_JTe_K\in e_IB(H)e_I\subset X_I,
\]
and \(M,JMJ\) multiply \(X_I\). Hence
\[
\mathcal Z_I\subset X_I.
\]

### Stability of \(\mathcal Z_I\)

For \(S=U\) or \(S=W\),
\[
\mathcal P_S\mathcal Z_I\subset\mathcal Z_I,
\qquad
\mathcal Z_I\mathcal P_S\subset\mathcal Z_I.
\tag{4.1}
\]
Indeed, if \(J\subset I\subset S\), finite propagation gives
\[
e_S\mathcal C e_J\subset
\operatorname{span}\{\mathcal C e_L\mathcal C:L\subset J\},
\]
and similarly
\[
e_J\mathcal C e_S\subset
\operatorname{span}\{\mathcal C e_L\mathcal C:L\subset J\}.
\]
Multiplying a generator \(Ae_JTe_KB\) of \(\mathcal Z_I\) on either side by one algebraic \(S\)-block therefore keeps all intermediate projections inside subsets of \(I\). Iterating over the word gives (4.1).

### The central \(U\)-\(W\)-\(U\) crossing

For \(A,B\in\mathcal C\) and \(T\in B(H)\),
\[
e_UAe_W\,T\,e_WBe_U\in\mathcal Z_I.
\tag{4.2}
\]
Indeed, finite propagation gives
\[
e_UAe_W=\sum_r A_r e_{J_r}B_r,
\qquad
e_WBe_U=\sum_s C_s e_{K_s}D_s,
\]
with \(J_r,K_s\subset I\). Hence
\[
e_UAe_W\,T\,e_WBe_U
=
\sum_{r,s}
A_r e_{J_r}\bigl(B_rT C_s\bigr)e_{K_s}D_s\in\mathcal Z_I.
\]

Now take words \(P\in\mathcal P_U\), \(Q\in\mathcal P_W\), \(R\in\mathcal P_U\). Isolate the last \(e_U\) in \(P\), the first and last \(e_W\) in \(Q\), and the first \(e_U\) in \(R\). The middle has the form covered by (4.2), hence lies in \(\mathcal Z_I\). The remaining prefixes and suffixes preserve \(\mathcal Z_I\) by (4.1). Therefore
\[
\mathcal P_U\mathcal P_W\mathcal P_U\subset \mathcal Z_I\subset X_I.
\tag{4.3}
\]

Since each \(h^S_{F,\varepsilon}\) lies in the norm closure of \(\mathcal P_S\), (4.3) gives
\[
h^U_\lambda h^W_\mu h^U_\lambda\in X_I
\]
for all algebraic approximate-unit indices \(\lambda,\mu\).

This repair avoids any false use of \(X_UX_W\subset X_I\).

---

## 5. Weak-* order-limit passage

Let
\[
a_\lambda=\iota_{\rm nor}(h^U_\lambda),
\qquad
b_\mu=\iota_{\rm nor}(h^W_\mu).
\]
By the support convergence above,
\[
a_\lambda\to q_U,\qquad b_\mu\to q_W
\]
ultraweakly.

Restricted multiplicativity gives
\[
a_\lambda b_\mu a_\lambda
=
\iota_{\rm nor}(h^U_\lambda h^W_\mu h^U_\lambda).
\]
The product inside belongs to \(X_I\subset K_{X_I}(M)\) and is a positive contraction. Therefore its image is a positive contraction in the \(q_I\)-corner:
\[
0\le a_\lambda b_\mu a_\lambda\le q_I.
\]
DKEP identify \((K_X(M)^\sharp_J)^*\) as a corner of the ambient normal bidual with support projection commuting with \(M\) and \(JMJ\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

Fix \(\lambda\) and let \(\mu\to\infty\). Separate ultraweak continuity of multiplication in the von Neumann algebra gives
\[
0\le a_\lambda q_W a_\lambda\le q_I.
\]
Now let \(\lambda\to\infty\). Since \(0\le a_\lambda\le q_U\) and \(a_\lambda\to q_U\) ultraweakly, we have \(a_\lambda\to q_U\) strongly: for every normal positive functional \(\omega\),
\[
\omega((q_U-a_\lambda)^2)\le \omega(q_U-a_\lambda)\to0.
\]
Hence
\[
a_\lambda q_W a_\lambda\to q_Uq_Wq_U
\]
ultraweakly. The order interval \([0,q_I]\) is ultraweakly closed, so
\[
\boxed{q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_I=q_{U\cap W}.}
\]

---

## Referee conclusion

The lemma is correct, but t9 needs the following repairs:

1. State restricted multiplicativity using the exact condition \(p_{\rm nor}\in\iota(C^*(M,JMJ,e_U,e_W))'\).
2. Use DE Lemma 3.7 for support convergence; do not claim ordinary norm density of the full boundary piece.
3. Use the sharper finite-propagation statement
   \[
   e_S\mathcal C e_T\subset \operatorname{span}\{\mathcal C_Se_J\mathcal C_T:J\subset S\cap T\}.
   \]
4. Replace the broad claim that \(\mathcal P_U\) and \(\mathcal P_W\) multiply all of \(X_I\) into itself by the precise stable algebraic-corner class \(\mathcal Z_I\).
5. Then the weak-* order-limit passage is valid.

## Task t9 — Red-team the DKEP support sandwich proof
[unverified self-report — verification not run or not yet complete | self_declared_is_relaxation: False | self_declared_problem_solved (the agent's own claim, NOT verified): Prove the following statement:

Let $\Gamma$ be a finite simplicial graph with $|\VV(\GG)|\geq 3$, and $(M_v,\varphi_v)$ be a separable tracial von Neumann algebra for each $v\in \VV$. If $\Gamma$ is irreducible (in the sense that there does not exists subgraphs $\GG_1,\GG_2$ such that $ \VV(\GG_1)\cup \VV(\GG_2)=\VV(\GG) $ and every $(v,u)\in \EE(\GG) $ for all $v\in\VV(\GG_1),u\in \VV(\GG_2)$), and each $M_v$ has a trace zero unitary, then the graph product von Neumann algebra $M$ is properly proximal.]
Source: task_outputs.jsonl — raw agent output. The values in the metadata line above are self-declared and have not been checked. Treat the proof as a draft, not as established.
## Verdict

The support sandwich lemma is **correct**:

\[
\boxed{q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_{U\cap W}}
\]

for graph-product parabolic boundary pieces in the DKEP normal-bidual compactification. The candidate proof in t7 is essentially right, but it must be stated with the following repairs:

- use multiplicativity of \(\iota_{\rm nor}\) **only** on \(C^*(M,JMJ,e_U,e_W)\);
- use the DKEP/DE approximate-unit lemma, not norm density of the full boundary piece;
- state finite propagation through \(e_J\)’s with \(J\subset U\cap W\), not necessarily through \(e_{U\cap W}\) literally;
- prove only the **triple** inclusion \(h_Uh_Wh_U\in X_{U\cap W}\), not \(X_UX_W\subset X_{U\cap W}\).

Below is a corrected proof.

---

# Proof

Let \(H=L^2(M)\), and write

\[
L_aR_b(\widehat x)=\widehat{axb},\qquad R_b=Jb^*J.
\]

For \(S\subset V(\Gamma)\), let \(M_S\subset M\) be the parabolic graph-product subalgebra, \(e_S\) the projection \(L^2(M)\to L^2(M_S)\), \(X_S\) the DKEP boundary piece associated with \(M_S\), and \(q_S\) the identity/support projection of \((K_{X_S}(M)^\sharp_J)^*\) inside the ambient normal bidual \(\mathcal N=(B(H)^\sharp_J)^*\). DKEP define subalgebra boundary pieces via the hereditary algebra generated by \(xJyJe_B\), and define \(K_X^{\infty,1}(M)\) as the \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-closure of \(X\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

## 1. Normal-bidual multiplicativity where needed

Let \(\iota:B(H)\to B(H)^{**}\) be the universal representation and

\[
\iota_{\rm nor}(T)=p_{\rm nor}\,\iota(T)\,p_{\rm nor}
\]

the canonical normal-bidual u.c.p. map. This map is **not** globally multiplicative; DKEP explicitly note that the canonical embedding into the normal envelope is generally not a \(*\)-homomorphism because \(p_{\rm nor}\) need not be central. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) DE record the same map as \(\operatorname{Ad}(p_{\rm nor})\circ\iota\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2211.05298))

However, \(p_{\rm nor}\) commutes with the universal images of \(M\) and \(JMJ\): equivalently, \(p_{\rm nor}\) is the intersection of the normal summands for the commuting von Neumann algebras \(M\) and \(JMJ\). Moreover, for every von Neumann subalgebra \(N\subset M\), DE Lemma 3.8 proves that \(\iota(e_N)\) commutes with \(p_{\rm nor}\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2211.05298)) Therefore

\[
p_{\rm nor}\ \text{commutes with}\ \iota(C^*(M,JMJ,e_U,e_W)).
\]

Consequently, \(\iota_{\rm nor}\) is a \(*\)-homomorphism on \(C^*(M,JMJ,e_U,e_W)\). In particular, if \(h_U\in C^*(M,JMJ,e_U)\) and \(h_W\in C^*(M,JMJ,e_W)\), then

\[
\iota_{\rm nor}(h_Uh_Wh_U)
=
\iota_{\rm nor}(h_U)\iota_{\rm nor}(h_W)\iota_{\rm nor}(h_U).
\tag{1.1}
\]

This is the only multiplicativity used.

---

## 2. Algebraic hereditary core and support convergence

Choose a unital algebraic graph-product \(*\)-subalgebra \(\mathcal A\subset M\), spanned by reduced Wick words, which is Kaplansky \(\|\cdot\|_2\)-dense in \(M\). For \(S\subset V\), define

\[
\mathscr S_S=\{L_aR_b e_S:a,b\in\mathcal A\},
\]

and let \(D_S\) be the hereditary \(C^*\)-subalgebra generated by \(\mathscr S_S\), equivalently the norm closure of finite sums

\[
s\,T\,t^*,\qquad s,t\in \mathscr S_S,\ T\in B(H).
\]

Since \(e_S=L_1R_1e_S\in\mathscr S_S\), \(D_S\) contains the full large corner \(e_SB(H)e_S\).

### Density in \(K_{X_S}^{\infty,1}(M)\)

The boundary piece \(X_S\) is the hereditary algebra generated by \(L_xR_y e_S\) with \(x,y\in M\). It is enough to approximate those coefficients by algebraic ones in \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\).

For \(x,y,a,b\in M\), with \(a,b\) bounded algebraic approximants to \(x,y\),

\[
\|L_xR_y e_S-L_aR_b e_S\|_{\infty,2}
\le
\|y\|_\infty\|x-a\|_2+\|a\|_\infty\|y-b\|_2,
\]

and the same estimate holds for adjoints. Hence for

\[
s=L_xR_y e_S,\qquad t=L_{x'}R_{y'}e_S,
\]

and algebraic approximants \(s',t'\),

\[
\|sTt^*-s'T(t')^*\|_{\infty,1}
\le
\|T\|
\bigl(
\|(s-s')^*\|_{\infty,2}\|t\|
+
\|s'\|\|(t-t')^*\|_{\infty,2}
\bigr).
\]

Thus \(D_S\) is \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-dense in \(X_S^{\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}}=K_{X_S}^{\infty,1}(M)\). DKEP prove \(K_X^{\infty,1}(M)=K_X(M)^{\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}}\), so this is exactly the support-dense core needed. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

### Approximate units

For finite \(F\subset\mathscr S_S\) and \(\varepsilon>0\), set

\[
A_{F,S}=\sum_{s\in F}ss^*,\qquad
h^S_{F,\varepsilon}=A_{F,S}(A_{F,S}+\varepsilon)^{-1}.
\]

The net \(h^S_{F,\varepsilon}\) is a positive contractive approximate unit for \(D_S\). Each \(h^S_{F,\varepsilon}\) lies in \(C^*(M,JMJ,e_S)\), so \(\iota(h^S_{F,\varepsilon})\) commutes with \(p_{\rm nor}\). By the DE approximate-unit lemma, any such approximate unit of a \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-dense core converges weak-* to the identity of \((K_{X_S}(M)^\sharp_J)^*\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2211.05298)) Hence

\[
\iota_{\rm nor}(h^S_{F,\varepsilon})\xrightarrow{\ \sigma\text{-weak}\ } q_S.
\tag{2.1}
\]

---

## 3. Finite propagation

Let \(I=U\cap W\). We need:

\[
e_U L_aR_b e_W
\in
\operatorname{span}
\{L_\alpha R_\beta e_J L_\gamma R_\delta e_W:
\alpha,\beta,\gamma,\delta\in\mathcal A,\ J\subset I\}.
\tag{3.1}
\]

It suffices to prove this for reduced Wick words \(a,b\).

Use the standard graph-product relative normal form. A reduced vertex word is reduced relative to \((V_1,V_2)\) if it remains reduced after adding any letter from \(V_1\) on the left and any letter from \(V_2\) on the right. The relative normal form decomposes any reduced word into a \(V_1\)-part, a middle part reduced relative to \((V_1,V_2)\), and a \(V_2\)-part. ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743))

Thus write, after admissible commutations,

\[
a=a_Ua_0a_W,
\qquad
b=b_Wb_0b_U,
\]

where

\[
a_U,b_U\in\mathcal A_U,\qquad
a_W,b_W\in\mathcal A_W,
\]

\(a_0\) is reduced relative to \((U,W)\), and \(b_0\) is reduced relative to \((W,U)\). Then \(a_0^*\) is reduced relative to \((W,U)\).

The conditional relative Wick formula says: if \(x,\tilde x\) have reduced words relative to \((W,U)\), then for \(y\in M_W\),

\[
E_U(\tilde x^* y x)
=
\tau(\tilde x^*x)\,E_J(y),
\]

where \(J\subset U\cap W\) is the set of vertices in \(U\cap W\) adjacent to all letters of the word of \(x\); if the two relative words are not equivalent, the scalar is \(0\). This is exactly Lemma 3.17 in the graph-product structure paper, specialized with \(V_1=W,V_2=U\). ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743))

Apply it with \(\tilde x=a_0^*\), \(x=b_0\), and \(y=a_Wzb_W\in M_W\). For \(z\in\mathcal A_W\),

\[
\begin{aligned}
E_U(azb)
&=
a_U\,E_U\bigl(a_0(a_Wzb_W)b_0\bigr)\,b_U\\
&=
\tau(a_0b_0)\,
a_U\,E_J(a_Wzb_W)\,b_U.
\end{aligned}
\]

Therefore, as operators \(L^2(M_W)\to L^2(M_U)\),

\[
e_U L_aR_b e_W
=
\tau(a_0b_0)\,
L_{a_U}R_{b_U}\,e_J\,L_{a_W}R_{b_W}e_W,
\]

with \(J\subset I\). Linearity gives (3.1). Taking adjoints gives the symmetric formula for \(e_WL_aR_b e_U\).

---

## 4. Triple products land in \(X_I\)

Let \(\mathcal Y_U^0\) be the \(*\)-algebra generated by positive algebraic \(U\)-blocks

\[
L_aR_b e_U R_b^*L_a^*,\qquad a,b\in\mathcal A.
\]

Equivalently, \(\mathcal Y_U^0\) consists of finite sums of words

\[
A_0e_UA_1e_U\cdots e_UA_n,
\]

where the \(A_i\)’s are algebraic \(M\)-\(JMJ\) multipliers. Define \(\mathcal Y_W^0\) similarly.

First, by finite propagation with \(W=I\),

\[
e_U A e_I\in X_I,\qquad e_I A e_U\in X_I
\]

for every algebraic multiplier \(A\), because all intermediate projections are \(e_J\) with \(J\subset I\), and

\[
e_JB(H)e_K\subset e_IB(H)e_I\subset X_I.
\]

Thus elements of \(\mathcal Y_U^0\) and \(\mathcal Y_W^0\) multiply \(X_I\) into itself on both sides.

Second, for algebraic multipliers \(A,B\) and arbitrary \(T\in B(H)\), finite propagation gives

\[
e_UAe_W=\sum_i A_i e_{J_i}B_i e_W,\qquad
e_WBe_U=\sum_j e_W C_j e_{K_j}D_j,
\]

with \(J_i,K_j\subset I\). Hence

\[
e_UAe_W\,T\,e_WBe_U
=
\sum_{i,j}
A_i e_{J_i}
\bigl(B_i e_W T e_W C_j\bigr)
e_{K_j}D_j
\in X_I.
\tag{4.1}
\]

Combining the previous two observations,

\[
\mathcal Y_U^0\,\mathcal Y_W^0\,\mathcal Y_U^0\subset X_I.
\tag{4.2}
\]

Each \(h^U_{F,\varepsilon}\) is a norm limit of polynomials with zero constant term in finite sums of positive \(U\)-blocks, hence lies in the norm closure of \(\mathcal Y_U^0\). Similarly for \(h^W_{G,\delta}\). Since \(X_I\) is norm closed, (4.2) gives

\[
h^U_{F,\varepsilon}\,h^W_{G,\delta}\,h^U_{F,\varepsilon}\in X_I\subset K_{X_I}(M).
\tag{4.3}
\]

This is where the large corners are handled: the proof does **not** claim \(X_UX_W\subset X_I\); it only uses the special algebraic approximate units built from positive blocks.

---

## 5. Passing to the support projections

Let

\[
a_\lambda=\iota_{\rm nor}(h^U_\lambda),\qquad
b_\mu=\iota_{\rm nor}(h^W_\mu).
\]

By (2.1),

\[
a_\lambda\to q_U,\qquad b_\mu\to q_W
\]

\(\sigma\)-weakly. By multiplicativity on \(C^*(M,JMJ,e_U,e_W)\), (1.1) gives

\[
a_\lambda b_\mu a_\lambda
=
\iota_{\rm nor}(h^U_\lambda h^W_\mu h^U_\lambda).
\]

By (4.3), the operator \(h^U_\lambda h^W_\mu h^U_\lambda\) lies in \(X_I\subset K_{X_I}(M)\). It is a positive contraction, so its image lies under the identity \(q_I\) of the \(K_{X_I}\)-corner:

\[
0\le a_\lambda b_\mu a_\lambda\le q_I.
\tag{5.1}
\]

Fix \(\lambda\) and let \(\mu\to\infty\). Multiplication by the fixed \(a_\lambda\) is normal, so

\[
0\le a_\lambda q_W a_\lambda\le q_I.
\tag{5.2}
\]

Now let \(\lambda\to\infty\). Since \(0\le a_\lambda\le q_U\) and \(a_\lambda\to q_U\) \(\sigma\)-weakly, we also have \(a_\lambda\to q_U\) \(\sigma\)-strongly: for every normal positive functional \(\omega\),

\[
\omega((q_U-a_\lambda)^2)
\le
\omega(q_U-a_\lambda)\to0.
\]

Therefore

\[
a_\lambda q_W a_\lambda\to q_Uq_Wq_U
\]

\(\sigma\)-weakly. The order interval \([0,q_I]\) is \(\sigma\)-weakly closed, so (5.2) yields

\[
\boxed{q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_I}.
\]

---

# Risk audit

1. **Non-multiplicativity of \(\iota_{\rm nor}\).**  
   No global multiplicativity is used. The proof only uses multiplicativity on \(C^*(M,JMJ,e_U,e_W)\), where \(p_{\rm nor}\) commutes with the generators.

2. **Density of the algebraic hereditary core.**  
   The algebraic core \(D_U\) is not asserted to be norm dense in the full boundary piece. It is \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-dense in \(K_{X_U}^{\infty,1}(M)\), which is exactly what the DKEP/DE support approximate-unit lemma requires.

3. **Finite propagation.**  
   The correct statement factors through \(e_J\) for \(J\subset U\cap W\). This follows from the relative graph-product conditional expectation formula. No stronger literal \(e_{U\cap W}\)-factorization is needed.

4. **Large corners.**  
   The false inclusion \(X_UX_W\subset X_{U\cap W}\) is not used. Only the special triple products \(h_Uh_Wh_U\) are shown to lie in \(X_{U\cap W}\).

5. **Order-limit passage.**  
   The passage from finite approximate units to \(q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_I\) is valid because \(0\le a_\lambda\le q_U\), weak convergence to \(q_U\) improves to strong convergence, and \([0,q_I]\) is weak-* closed.

## Task t13 — Hostile audit of the final DKEP obstruction and normal-bidual hypotheses

Let $\Gamma$ be a finite simplicial graph with $|\VV(\GG)|\geq 3$, and $(M_v,\varphi_v)$ be a separable tracial von Neumann algebra for each $v\in \VV$. If $\Gamma$ is irreducible (in the sense that there does not exists subgraphs $\GG_1,\GG_2$ such that $ \VV(\GG_1)\cup \VV(\GG_2)=\VV(\GG) $ and every $(v,u)\in \EE(\GG) $ for all $v\in\VV(\GG_1),u\in \VV(\GG_2)$), and each $M_v$ has a trace zero unitary, then the graph product von Neumann algebra $M$ is properly proximal.]
Source: task_outputs.jsonl — raw agent output. The values in the metadata line above are self-declared and have not been checked. Treat the proof as a draft, not as established.
## Verdict

**Accept with dependency patches.** The final argument is salvageable and does **not** need a separate proof that the graph product is a nonamenable factor. However, it must **not** directly apply DKEP Lemma 8.6 to the trivial action unless factor/center-ergodicity is first established. The safer and sufficient route is:

1. prove **no amenable direct summand** by the ping-pong argument;
2. use the **general normal-bidual characterization** of relative proper proximality, not DKEP 8.6 in its center-ergodic action form;
3. derive the obstruction state \(\Omega\) with \(\Omega(q_K)=0\) from no amenable direct summand;
4. extend states to an ambient \(C^*\)-algebra before the final GNS finite-cover argument.

DKEP Lemma 8.5 is stated for actions with \(\Gamma\curvearrowright Z(M)\) ergodic, and Lemma 8.6 says “using the same notation,” so it inherits that hypothesis. For the trivial action, that means \(Z(M)=\mathbb C\). Thus, a literal citation of Lemma 8.6 for the graph product would be a gap unless the proof establishes factoriality. But factoriality is unnecessary: the no-amenable-direct-summand argument plus the general DE/DKEP normal-bidual formulation suffices. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

---

## Gaps and patches

### 1. **Trivial-action / center-ergodicity issue**

**Gap if written naively:** “Apply DKEP Lemma 8.6 to the trivial action” is not justified unless \(M\) is a factor. DKEP define properly proximal actions with weak compactness and compact-vanishing alternatives, then Lemma 8.5 assumes \(\Gamma\curvearrowright Z(M)\) is ergodic; Lemma 8.6 is a variant “using the same notation,” so it keeps that hypothesis. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

**Patch:** Do **not** cite Lemma 8.6 directly. Cite the general bidual characterization: for a separable tracial \(M\) and boundary piece \(X\), \(M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X\) iff there is no \(M\)-central state on \(\widetilde S_X(M)\) whose restriction to \(M\) is normal. This is stated as DE Lemma 3.5. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2211.05298))

### 2. **Concrete relative proper proximality \(\Rightarrow\) normal-bidual relative proper proximality**

**Gap:** The ping-pong proof gives no \(M\)-central state on the concrete operator system \(S_{X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}}(M)\). The later compression argument produces a state on \(\widetilde S_X(M)\), not on concrete \(S_X(M)\).

**Patch:** Insert: by DKEP Theorem 6.2, the absence of an \(M\)-central state normal on \(M\) is precisely concrete relative proper proximality; by DE Lemma 3.5, this is equivalent to the absence of such a state on \(\widetilde S_X(M)\). DKEP define \(S_X(M)\) and relative proper proximality in exactly this form. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

### 3. **Obstruction state with \(\Omega(q_K)=0\)**

**Gap:** The final proof needs an obstruction state on \(\widetilde S(M)\) satisfying \(\Omega(q_K)=0\). This is stronger than merely “\(M\) is not properly proximal.”

**Patch:** State and prove/cite the following derived lemma:

> If \(M\) is separable, has no amenable direct summand, and is not properly proximal, then there exists an \(M\)-central state \(\Omega\) on \(\widetilde S(M)\), normal on \(M\), such that \(\Omega(q_K)=0\).

Derivation: non-proper proximality gives \(\Omega\) by DE Lemma 3.5 with \(X=K(L^2M)\). If \(\Omega(q_K)>0\), normalizing the \(q_K\)-corner gives an \(M\)-central state on \(B(L^2M)\) normal on \(M\), hence an amenable direct summand, contradiction. DE use exactly this support argument: if a state has positive mass on \(q_K\), restricting through the compact-corner normal operator system produces amenability. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2211.05298))

### 4. **Compression argument \(\Omega(q_X)=1\)**

**Mostly correct, but must cite the right support facts.**

Required ingredients:

- \(q_X\), the identity/support of \((K_X(M)^\sharp_J)^*\), commutes with \(M\) and \(JMJ\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))
- DE record the compression inclusion
  \[
  q_X^\perp\,\widetilde S_X(M)\,q_X^\perp
  \subset
  q_K^\perp\,\widetilde S(M).
  \]
  ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2211.05298))

Then, if \(r=1-q_X\) and \(\Omega(r)>0\), define
\[
\Phi(T)=\frac{\Omega(rTr)}{\Omega(r)},\qquad T\in\widetilde S_X(M).
\]
This is well-defined because \(r\widetilde S_X(M)r\subset \widetilde S(M)\). It is \(M\)-central because \(r\) commutes with \(M\). Its restriction to \(M\) is normal because for \(x\in M_+\),
\[
0\le r x r\le x
\]
and hence \(\Phi|_M\) is dominated by the normal functional \(\Omega|_M/\Omega(r)\). This contradicts relative proper proximality to \(X\). Therefore \(\Omega(q_X)=1\).

### 5. **Final finite-cover GNS argument**

**Patch needed:** Since \(\widetilde S(M)\) is an operator system, not a \(C^*\)-algebra in the concrete proof, extend \(\Omega\) to a state \(\bar\Omega\) on the ambient \(C^*\)-algebra/von Neumann algebra generated by the support projections before using GNS.

Then the argument is valid:

If \(\bar\Omega(q_U)=\bar\Omega(q_W)=1\), then in the GNS representation,
\[
\pi(q_U)\xi=\xi.
\]
Hence
\[
\bar\Omega(q_Uq_Wq_U)=\bar\Omega(q_W)=1.
\]
Using the support sandwich
\[
0\le q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_{U\cap W},
\]
we get \(\bar\Omega(q_{U\cap W})=1\). Iterate over \(U_v=\operatorname{Link}(v)\). No commutativity of the \(q_U\)’s is needed.

### 6. **\(q_\varnothing=q_K\)**

This is correct but should be explicitly proved.

For \(U=\varnothing\), \(M_\varnothing=\mathbb C1\), and \(e_\varnothing\) is the rank-one projection onto \(\mathbb C\widehat 1\). The DKEP subalgebra boundary piece for \(M_U\) is the hereditary algebra generated by left-right translates of \(e_U\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) For \(U=\varnothing\), those translates are finite-rank operators:
\[
L_a e_\varnothing L_b^*=\theta_{\widehat a,\widehat b}.
\]
Since \(M\widehat 1\) is dense in \(L^2M\), these span a norm-dense subalgebra of \(K(L^2M)\), and conversely all generators are compact. Hence
\[
X_\varnothing=K(L^2M),\qquad q_\varnothing=q_K.
\]

---

## Concise dependency list for insertion into the final proof

Use the following external inputs.

1. **Concrete relative proper proximality.**  
   For an \(M\)-boundary piece \(X\),
   \[
   S_X(M)=\{T\in B(L^2M):[T,JxJ]\in K_X^{\infty,1}(M)\ \forall x\in M\}.
   \]
   The inclusion \(M\subset M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X\) iff there is no \(M\)-central state on \(S_X(M)\) whose restriction to \(M\) is normal. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

2. **Normal-bidual formulation.**  
   For separable tracial \(M\), proper proximality relative to \(X\) is equivalent to the nonexistence of an \(M\)-central state on
   \[
   \widetilde S_X(M)
   =
   \{T\in (B(L^2M)^\sharp_J)^*:[T,a]\in (K_X(M)^\sharp_J)^*\ \forall a\in JMJ\}
   \]
   whose restriction to \(M\) is normal. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2211.05298))

3. **Support projections.**  
   Let \(q_X\) be the identity of \((K_X(M)^\sharp_J)^*\). Then \(q_X\) commutes with \(M\) and \(JMJ\). In particular \(q_X\in\widetilde S(M)\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

4. **Compression inclusion.**  
   For every boundary piece \(X\),
   \[
   q_X^\perp\widetilde S_X(M)q_X^\perp
   \subset
   q_K^\perp\widetilde S(M).
   \]
   This is the key input for proving \(\Omega(q_X)=1\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2211.05298))

5. **Obstruction state without factoriality.**  
   If \(M\) is separable, has no amenable direct summand, and is not properly proximal, then there is an \(M\)-central state \(\Omega\) on \(\widetilde S(M)\), normal on \(M\), with \(\Omega(q_K)=0\). This follows from the normal-bidual characterization plus the compact-corner support argument: positive mass on \(q_K\) would yield an amenable direct summand. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2211.05298))

6. **Relative-to-absolute support forcing.**  
   If \(M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X\) and \(\Omega\) is the obstruction state above, then \(\Omega(q_X)=1\). Proof: if \(r=1-q_X\) has positive \(\Omega\)-mass, the normalized compression \(T\mapsto \Omega(rTr)/\Omega(r)\) gives an \(M\)-central state on \(\widetilde S_X(M)\) normal on \(M\), contradiction.

7. **Support sandwich.**  
   For graph-product parabolic boundary pieces,
   \[
   q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_{U\cap W}.
   \]
   Use only this one-sided inequality; do not assume the \(q_U\)’s commute.

8. **Empty parabolic equals compact boundary.**  
   Since \(M_\varnothing=\mathbb C1\), \(e_\varnothing\) is rank-one and the boundary piece \(X_\varnothing\) is exactly \(K(L^2M)\). Thus \(q_\varnothing=q_K\).

9. **Final state extension.**  
   Before applying GNS to products \(q_Uq_Wq_U\), extend \(\Omega\) from \(\widetilde S(M)\) to a state on the ambient \(C^*\)-algebra generated by the support projections. Then the support-one GNS argument is legitimate.

With these insertions, the final proof’s dependency chain is sound, and the trivial-action issue is neutralized without proving that the graph product is a factor.

## 1. The sector projections lie in \(S_{X_B}(M)\)

Let
\[
K_A=L^2(A)\ominus L^2(B),\qquad K_C=L^2(C)\ominus L^2(B).
\]
The AFP decomposition gives
\[
L^2(M)=L^2(B)\oplus \bigoplus_{\epsilon_1\ne\cdots\ne\epsilon_n,\ \epsilon_i\in\{A,C\}}
K_{\epsilon_1}\otimes_B\cdots\otimes_B K_{\epsilon_n}.
\]
Let \(R_A\) project onto \(L^2(B)\) plus the summands with first letter \(A\), and \(R_C\) similarly. Then
\[
P_A=R_A-e_B,\qquad P_C=R_C-e_B,\qquad 1=e_B+P_A+P_C.
\]

For \(D\in\{A,C\}\), let \(\bar D\) denote the other algebra and \(D^0=\ker(E_B|_D)\). On algebraic reduced tensors, right multiplication by \(x\in D^0\) has the usual AFP form: it modifies only the **last** tensor letter. Therefore the first AFP letter is unchanged except in two cases: the vector lies in \(L^2(B)\), or a one-letter \(D\)-word collapses partly into \(L^2(B)\). Consequently,
\[
[R_D,\rho(x)]=0,
\]
and
\[
[R_{\bar D},\rho(x)]=e_B\rho(x)-\rho(x)e_B. \tag{1}
\]
Indeed, \(-\rho(x)e_B\) accounts for \(L^2(B)\ni b\mapsto bx\in K_D\), and \(e_B\rho(x)\) accounts for the \(B\)-part of \(\eta x\), \(\eta\in K_D\).

For general \(x\in D\), write \(x=E_B(x)+x^0\). Right multiplication by \(E_B(x)\in B\) preserves every AFP sector, so only \(x^0\) contributes. Hence the commutators of \(R_A,R_C\) with right multiplication by elements of \(A\) or \(C\) are either zero or of the form
\[
e_B\rho(y)-\rho(y)e_B.
\]
Both summands factor through \(L^2(B)\), hence lie in \(\mathcal K^{\infty,1}_{X_B}\), since \(X_B\) is generated by \(e_B\).

Every vertex algebra lies in \(A\) or \(C\) — link vertices lie in \(B\subset A\cap C\). Thus the DKEP generator criterion gives
\[
R_A,R_C\in S_{X_B}(M).
\]
Since \(e_B\in X_B\subset S_{X_B}(M)\), it follows that
\[
P_A=R_A-e_B,\qquad P_C=R_C-e_B
\]
also belong to \(S_{X_B}(M)\).

## 2. Ping-pong unitaries

Because \(A=M_v\bar\otimes B\), if \(u_v\in M_v\) is a trace-zero unitary, then
\[
a:=u_v\in A,\qquad E_B(a)=\tau_v(u_v)1=0.
\]

Now construct \(c_1,c_2\in C\).

First, join irreducibility implies \(v\) has at least one non-neighbour; otherwise \(\Gamma=\{v\}*\Gamma_{V\setminus\{v\}}\) would be a nontrivial join.

### Case 1: two distinct non-neighbours

Let \(w_1,w_2\notin \operatorname{Star}(v)\), \(w_1\ne w_2\). Choose trace-zero unitaries \(u_{w_i}\in M_{w_i}\), and put
\[
c_i=u_{w_i}.
\]
Then \(c_i\in C\), \(E_B(c_i)=0\), and by the reduced-word expectation rule,
\[
E_B(c_i^*c_j)=\delta_{ij}1.
\]
For \(i\ne j\), the word \(u_{w_i}^*u_{w_j}\) contains vertices outside \(\operatorname{Link}(v)\), hence has zero \(B\)-expectation.

### Case 2: unique non-neighbour

Let \(w\) be the unique non-neighbour of \(v\). Since \(|V|\ge 3\), \(\operatorname{Link}(v)\neq\varnothing\). If \(w\) were adjacent to every \(z\in\operatorname{Link}(v)\), then \(\Gamma\) would be the join
\[
\Gamma_{\{v,w\}}*\Gamma_{\operatorname{Link}(v)},
\]
contradicting irreducibility. Thus choose \(z\in\operatorname{Link}(v)\) not adjacent to \(w\).

Pick trace-zero unitaries \(u_w\in M_w\), \(u_z\in M_z\subset B\), and set
\[
c_1=u_w,\qquad c_2=u_z u_w.
\]
Then \(c_1,c_2\in C\) are unitaries. Also
\[
E_B(c_1)=0,\qquad E_B(c_2)=E_B(u_z u_w)=u_zE_B(u_w)=0.
\]
Moreover,
\[
E_B(c_1^*c_1)=E_B(c_2^*c_2)=1.
\]
For the cross term,
\[
c_1^*c_2=u_w^*u_z u_w.
\]
Because \(z\) is not adjacent to \(w\), the word \(w,z,w\) is graph-reduced; the two \(w\)-syllables cannot be shuffled together and cancelled. It contains the vertex \(w\notin \operatorname{Link}(v)\), so
\[
E_B(u_w^*u_z u_w)=0.
\]
Thus
\[
E_B(c_i^*c_j)=\delta_{ij}1.
\]
This remains true in the \(M_w\simeq\mathbb C^2\) case: even if \(u_w=u_w^*=u_w^{-1}\), the word \(u_wu_zu_w\) does not reduce to \(u_z\), because \(u_z\) is a noncommuting intervening syllable.

## 3. Exact sector containments

Let \(d\in C\) be unitary with \(E_B(d)=0\). Then left multiplication by \(d\) sends \(H_A^+\) into \(P_CL^2(M)\): for \(b\in L^2(B)\),
\[
db\in L^2(C)\ominus L^2(B),
\]
and if \(\xi\) is a reduced word beginning with an \(A\)-letter, then \(d\xi\) is reduced and begins with a \(C\)-letter. Hence
\[
dH_A^+\subset P_CL^2(M).
\]
Applying this to \(d=c_1,c_2\),
\[
c_iR_Ac_i^*\le P_C.
\]

The ranges are orthogonal. Indeed, \(c_1^*c_2\in C\) and \(E_B(c_1^*c_2)=0\), so
\[
(c_1^*c_2)H_A^+\subset P_CL^2(M),
\]
which is orthogonal to \(H_A^+=L^2(B)\oplus P_AL^2(M)\). Thus
\[
c_1R_Ac_1^* \perp c_2R_Ac_2^*,
\]
and therefore
\[
c_1R_Ac_1^*+c_2R_Ac_2^*\le P_C. \tag{2}
\]

Similarly, since \(a\in A\) and \(E_B(a)=0\), left multiplication by \(a\) sends \(H_C^+\) into \(P_AL^2(M)\). Hence
\[
aR_Ca^*\le P_A. \tag{3}
\]

No \(e_B\)-error occurs: the \(L^2(B)\)-part of \(H_A^+\) is sent by \(c_i\) into \(C\ominus B\), and the \(L^2(B)\)-part of \(H_C^+\) is sent by \(a\) into \(A\ominus B\).

## 4. State contradiction

Let \(\varphi\) be an \(M\)-central state on \(S_{X_B}(M)\). Since \(a,c_1,c_2\in M\) are unitaries, centrality gives
\[
\varphi(uTu^*)=\varphi(T)
\]
for every \(u\in\{a,c_1,c_2\}\) and \(T\in S_{X_B}(M)\).

From (2),
\[
2\varphi(R_A)
=\varphi(c_1R_Ac_1^*)+\varphi(c_2R_Ac_2^*)
\le \varphi(P_C). \tag{4}
\]
From (3),
\[
\varphi(R_C)=\varphi(aR_Ca^*)\le \varphi(P_A). \tag{5}
\]

Set
\[
\alpha=\varphi(e_B),\qquad \beta=\varphi(P_A),\qquad \gamma=\varphi(P_C).
\]
Then \(\alpha,\beta,\gamma\ge 0\) and
\[
\alpha+\beta+\gamma=1.
\]
Also
\[
\varphi(R_A)=\alpha+\beta,\qquad \varphi(R_C)=\alpha+\gamma.
\]
Thus (4) and (5) become
\[
2(\alpha+\beta)\le \gamma,\qquad \alpha+\gamma\le \beta.
\]
Combining,
\[
\beta\ge \alpha+\gamma\ge \alpha+2(\alpha+\beta)=3\alpha+2\beta,
\]
so
\[
0\ge 3\alpha+\beta.
\]
Since \(\alpha,\beta\ge 0\), this forces \(\alpha=\beta=0\). Then \(\alpha+\gamma\le\beta\) gives \(\gamma\le 0\), hence \(\gamma=0\), contradicting
\[
\alpha+\beta+\gamma=1.
\]

Therefore no \(M\)-central state on \(S_{X_B}(M)\) exists, in particular none that is normal on \(M\). Hence \(M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X_B\).

Source: task_outputs.jsonl — raw agent output. The values in the metadata line above are self-declared and have not been checked. Treat the proof as a draft, not as established.
Below I write the proof using only the **one-sided support sandwich**
\[
q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_{U\cap W},
\]
never the commutation/product formula.

I use the standard convention that “irreducible” means **no nontrivial join decomposition**
\[
V=V_1\sqcup V_2,\qquad V_i\ne\varnothing,
\]
with every vertex of \(V_1\) adjacent to every vertex of \(V_2\). If empty join factors were allowed, the hypothesis would be vacuous.

---

## 0. DKEP notation used

For \(U\subset V\), let \(M_U\subset M\) be the parabolic graph-product subalgebra, \(e_U\) the projection \(L^2(M)\to L^2(M_U)\), \(X_U\) the DKEP boundary piece generated by \(e_U\), and \(q_U\) its normal-bidual support projection. DKEP’s boundary piece associated with a subalgebra \(B\subset M\) is generated by \(e_B\)-corner operators and has \(M\) and \(JMJ\) in its multiplier algebra. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

Let \(S_X(M)\) be the relative small-at-infinity operator system
\[
S_X(M)=\{T\in B(L^2M): [T,JxJ]\in K_X^{\infty,1}(M)\ \forall x\in M\}.
\]
DKEP prove the generator criterion for checking the commutator condition on a von Neumann generating set, and define relative proper proximality by absence of \(M\)-central states on \(S_X(M)\) normal on \(M\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) In the normal-bidual formulation, \(\widetilde S_X(M)\) sits in a von Neumann algebra containing \(M\) and \(JMJ\), and the support projection \(q_X\) of the \(X\)-compact ideal commutes with both \(M\) and \(JMJ\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

The only support input assumed here is:

> **Support sandwich lemma.** For all \(U,W\subset V\),
> \[
> q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_{U\cap W}.
> \]

---

## 1. Finite-cover upgrade without commutation

### 1.1 GNS support-one fact

Let \(\phi\) be a state on a unital \(C^*\)-algebra or von Neumann algebra \(A\), and let \(p\in A\) be a projection with \(\phi(p)=1\). Let \((\pi_\phi,H_\phi,\xi_\phi)\) be the GNS representation. Then
\[
\|(1-\pi_\phi(p))\xi_\phi\|^2
=\phi(1-p)=0,
\]
so \(\pi_\phi(p)\xi_\phi=\xi_\phi\). Hence, for every \(a\in A\),
\[
\phi(a)
=\langle \pi_\phi(a)\xi_\phi,\xi_\phi\rangle
=\langle \pi_\phi(pap)\xi_\phi,\xi_\phi\rangle
=\phi(pap).
\]

This uses only positivity of \(\phi\), not normality.

### 1.2 Two-support descent

Suppose \(\phi(q_U)=\phi(q_W)=1\). Applying the GNS fact with \(p=q_U\) and \(a=q_W\),
\[
1=\phi(q_W)=\phi(q_Uq_Wq_U).
\]
By the support sandwich,
\[
0\le q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_{U\cap W}.
\]
Therefore
\[
1=\phi(q_Uq_Wq_U)\le \phi(q_{U\cap W})\le 1,
\]
so
\[
\phi(q_{U\cap W})=1.
\]

No commutation of \(q_U\) and \(q_W\) is used.

### 1.3 Finite iteration

Let \(U_1,\dots,U_n\subset V\) and suppose \(\phi(q_{U_i})=1\) for all \(i\). Define
\[
I_k=U_1\cap\cdots\cap U_k.
\]
By induction, \(\phi(q_{I_k})=1\). The step from \(k\) to \(k+1\) applies the two-support descent to \(I_k\) and \(U_{k+1}\):
\[
\phi(q_{I_k})=\phi(q_{U_{k+1}})=1
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\phi(q_{I_k\cap U_{k+1}})=1.
\]
Thus
\[
\phi(q_{U_1\cap\cdots\cap U_n})=1.
\]

For \(U_v=\operatorname{Link}(v)\), since the graph is loopless, no vertex belongs to its own link; hence
\[
\bigcap_{v\in V}\operatorname{Link}(v)=\varnothing.
\]
Therefore any state with \(\phi(q_{\operatorname{Link}(v)})=1\) for every \(v\) satisfies
\[
\phi(q_\varnothing)=1.
\]

---

## 2. Fixed-vertex AFP ping-pong

Fix \(v\in V\). Put
\[
L=\operatorname{Link}(v),\qquad
B=M_L,\qquad
A=M_{\operatorname{Star}(v)},\qquad
C=M_{V\setminus\{v\}}.
\]
Since \(v\) commutes with every vertex in \(L\),
\[
A\cong M_v\bar\otimes B,
\qquad
E_B|_A=\tau_v\otimes \operatorname{id}_B.
\]
The graph-product normal form gives
\[
M=A*_B C.
\]

### 2.1 Graph combinatorics

Irreducibility implies \(\operatorname{Star}(v)\ne V\). Otherwise
\[
V=\{v\}\sqcup(V\setminus\{v\})
\]
would be a nontrivial join. Hence
\[
N(v):=V\setminus \operatorname{Star}(v)\ne\varnothing.
\]

If \(N(v)=\{w\}\), then \(L\ne\varnothing\) because \(|V|\ge3\). If \(w\) were adjacent to every \(z\in L\), then
\[
V=\{v,w\}\sqcup L
\]
would be a nontrivial join. Hence in the unique non-neighbor case there exists \(z\in L\) not adjacent to \(w\).

### 2.2 Ping-pong unitaries

Choose a trace-zero unitary \(u_v\in M_v\), and set
\[
a=u_v\otimes 1_B\in A.
\]
Then \(a\) is unitary and
\[
E_B(a)=0.
\]

We construct unitaries \(c_1,c_2\in C\) with
\[
E_B(c_i)=0,\qquad E_B(c_i^*c_j)=\delta_{ij}1.
\]

**Case 1: \(|N(v)|\ge2\).**  
Choose distinct \(w_1,w_2\in N(v)\). Let \(u_{w_i}\in M_{w_i}\) be trace-zero unitaries and set
\[
c_i=u_{w_i}.
\]
Then \(E_B(c_i)=0\), because \(w_i\notin L\). Also \(E_B(c_i^*c_i)=1\). For \(i\ne j\), the word \(u_{w_i}^*u_{w_j}\) contains vertices outside \(L\), so its \(B\)-expectation is zero. This remains true whether or not \(w_1,w_2\) are adjacent.

**Case 2: \(N(v)=\{w\}\).**  
Choose \(z\in L\) not adjacent to \(w\). Pick trace-zero unitaries \(u_w\in M_w\) and \(u_z\in M_z\subset B\), and define
\[
c_1=u_w,\qquad c_2=u_zu_w.
\]
Then \(c_1,c_2\in C\) are unitaries,
\[
E_B(c_1)=0,\qquad E_B(c_2)=u_zE_B(u_w)=0.
\]
Moreover,
\[
c_1^*c_2=u_w^*u_zu_w.
\]
Because \(z\) and \(w\) are not adjacent, the word \(w,z,w\) is graph-reduced. Thus \(E_B(c_1^*c_2)=0\). The same argument gives \(E_B(c_2^*c_1)=0\).

This also handles order-two unitaries: even if \(u_w=u_w^*=u_w^{-1}\), the intervening noncommuting \(u_z\) prevents cancellation.

### 2.3 Sector projections lie in \(S_{X_L}(M)\)

Let
\[
K_A=L^2(A)\ominus L^2(B),\qquad K_C=L^2(C)\ominus L^2(B).
\]
The AFP decomposition is
\[
L^2(M)
=
L^2(B)\oplus
\bigoplus_{\epsilon_1\ne\cdots\ne \epsilon_n}
K_{\epsilon_1}\otimes_B\cdots\otimes_B K_{\epsilon_n},
\]
with \(\epsilon_i\in\{A,C\}\).

Let \(R_A\) be the projection onto \(L^2(B)\) plus the summands whose first AFP letter is \(A\). Define \(R_C\) similarly, and put
\[
P_A=R_A-e_B,\qquad P_C=R_C-e_B.
\]
Then
\[
1=e_B+P_A+P_C.
\]

Let \(\rho(x)=Jx^*J\) be right multiplication. For \(D\in\{A,C\}\) and \(x\in D^\circ:=\ker E_B|_D\), right multiplication by \(x\) affects only the last AFP letter. Therefore it preserves both \(R_D L^2(M)\) and its orthogonal complement, so
\[
[R_D,\rho(x)]=0.
\]
Since
\[
R_{\bar D}=1-R_D+e_B,
\]
we get
\[
[R_{\bar D},\rho(x)]=[e_B,\rho(x)].
\]
The terms \(e_B\rho(x)\) and \(\rho(x)e_B\) lie in \(X_L\), because \(e_B\in X_L\) and \(JMJ\) acts by multipliers on \(X_L\). For \(b\in B\), right multiplication by \(b\) preserves every sector, so the commutators vanish.

Since \(A\) and \(C\) generate \(M\), DKEP’s generator criterion gives
\[
R_A,R_C\in S_{X_L}(M).
\]
Also \(e_B\in X_L\subset S_{X_L}(M)\), so
\[
P_A,P_C\in S_{X_L}(M).
\]

### 2.4 Projection inequalities

Let
\[
H_A^+=R_A L^2(M),\qquad H_C^+=R_C L^2(M).
\]

If \(d\in C^\circ\), then
\[
dH_A^+\subset P_C L^2(M).
\]
Indeed, \(dL^2(B)\subset K_C\), and if an AFP word begins with \(A\), left multiplication by the centered \(C\)-letter \(d\) creates a reduced word beginning with \(C\). There is no \(e_B\)-term because \(E_B(d)=0\).

Thus
\[
c_iR_Ac_i^*\le P_C.
\]
Moreover, since \(E_B(c_1^*c_2)=0\), the same argument applied to \(d=c_1^*c_2\in C^\circ\) gives
\[
(c_1^*c_2)H_A^+\subset P_C L^2(M)\perp H_A^+.
\]
Hence \(c_1H_A^+\perp c_2H_A^+\), and
\[
c_1R_Ac_1^*+c_2R_Ac_2^*\le P_C.
\]

Similarly, since \(a\in A^\circ\),
\[
aH_C^+\subset P_A L^2(M),
\]
so
\[
aR_Ca^*\le P_A.
\]

### 2.5 No central state on \(S_{X_L}(M)\)

Let \(\varphi\) be an \(M\)-central state on \(S_{X_L}(M)\). Since \(a,c_1,c_2\in M\) are unitaries and \(S_{X_L}(M)\) is an \(M\)-bimodule,
\[
\varphi(uTu^*)=\varphi(T)
\]
for \(u\in\{a,c_1,c_2\}\).

From
\[
c_1R_Ac_1^*+c_2R_Ac_2^*\le P_C
\]
we get
\[
2\varphi(R_A)\le \varphi(P_C).
\]
From
\[
aR_Ca^*\le P_A
\]
we get
\[
\varphi(R_C)\le \varphi(P_A).
\]

Set
\[
\alpha=\varphi(e_B),\qquad
\beta=\varphi(P_A),\qquad
\gamma=\varphi(P_C).
\]
Then
\[
\alpha,\beta,\gamma\ge0,\qquad
\alpha+\beta+\gamma=1,
\]
and
\[
\varphi(R_A)=\alpha+\beta,\qquad
\varphi(R_C)=\alpha+\gamma.
\]
Thus
\[
2(\alpha+\beta)\le \gamma,\qquad
\alpha+\gamma\le \beta.
\]
Combining,
\[
\beta\ge \alpha+\gamma\ge \alpha+2(\alpha+\beta)=3\alpha+2\beta,
\]
so
\[
0\ge 3\alpha+\beta.
\]
Hence \(\alpha=\beta=0\). Then \(\alpha+\gamma\le\beta\) gives \(\gamma=0\), contradicting \(\alpha+\beta+\gamma=1\).

Therefore there is no \(M\)-central state on \(S_{X_L}(M)\). In particular, \(M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}\) for every \(v\in V\).

---

## 3. No amenable direct summand

Suppose, for contradiction, that \(z\in Z(M)\) is a nonzero central projection and \(zM\) is amenable. Let
\[
H_z=zL^2(M)=L^2(zM).
\]
Amenability gives a \(zM\)-central hypertrace \(\psi\) on \(B(H_z)\) extending the normalized trace \(\tau_z=\tau(\,\cdot\,)/\tau(z)\) on \(zM\).

Fix \(v\in V\), and write \(L=\operatorname{Link}(v)\). For \(T\in S_{X_L}(M)\), define
\[
\Phi(T)=\psi\bigl(zTz|_{H_z}\bigr).
\]
Then \(\Phi\) is a state. For \(x\in M\),
\[
\Phi(xT)=\psi((zx)(zTz))
=\psi((zTz)(zx))
=\Phi(Tx),
\]
because \(zx\in zM\) and \(\psi\) is \(zM\)-central. Also,
\[
\Phi(x)=\tau_z(zx)
\]
for \(x\in M\), so \(\Phi|_M\) is normal.

This contradicts the fixed-vertex ping-pong result, which produced no \(M\)-central state at all on \(S_{X_L}(M)\). Hence \(M\) has no amenable direct summand.

---

## 4. DKEP obstruction/compression step

Assume now, for contradiction, that \(M\) is not properly proximal. Since \(M\) has no amenable direct summand, the DKEP obstruction theorem gives an \(M\)-central state \(\Omega\) on the absolute normal-bidual compactification \(\widetilde S(M)\), normal on \(M\), such that
\[
\Omega(q_K)=0,
\]
where \(q_K\) is the compact support. In DKEP’s operator-system formulation, absence of amenable direct summands lets one choose an obstruction state vanishing on \(K^{\infty,1}\); in the normal-bidual formulation this is exactly the condition \(\Omega(q_K)=0\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

We claim:

> If \(M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X_U\), then
> \[
> \Omega(q_U)=1.
> \]

Let \(r=1-q_U\). Suppose \(\Omega(r)>0\). For \(T\in \widetilde S_{X_U}(M)\), define
\[
\Phi(T)=\frac{\Omega(rTr)}{\Omega(r)}.
\]

This is well-defined because \(q_U\), hence \(r\), commutes with \(M\) and \(JMJ\). If \(a\in JMJ\), then
\[
[rTr,a]=r[T,a]r.
\]
But \([T,a]\) lies in the \(q_U\)-supported compact ideal, so
\[
r[T,a]r=0.
\]
Thus \(rTr\in \widetilde S(M)\), the domain of \(\Omega\).

The functional \(\Phi\) is a state and is \(M\)-central. Indeed, for \(x\in M\),
\[
\Phi(xT)
=
\frac{\Omega(x\,rTr)}{\Omega(r)}
=
\frac{\Omega(rTr\,x)}{\Omega(r)}
=
\Phi(Tx).
\]

Finally, \(\Phi|_M\) is normal. For \(x\in M_+\),
\[
0\le r x r\le x
\]
because \(r\) commutes with \(M\). Hence
\[
0\le \Phi(x)\le \frac{\Omega(x)}{\Omega(r)}.
\]
The right-hand side is a normal positive functional on \(M\). A positive functional dominated by a normal positive functional is normal: if \(x_i\uparrow x\) in \(M_+\), then
\[
0\le \Phi(x)-\Phi(x_i)=\Phi(x-x_i)
\le \frac{\Omega(x-x_i)}{\Omega(r)}\to0.
\]
Thus \(\Phi|_M\) is normal.

This contradicts relative proper proximality to \(X_U\). Therefore \(\Omega(r)=0\), i.e.
\[
\Omega(q_U)=1.
\]

Crucially, this argument uses only that \(\Omega|_M\) is normal. It does **not** use normality of \(\Omega\) on the whole normal-bidual algebra.

Applying this to \(U=\operatorname{Link}(v)\), we get
\[
\Omega(q_{\operatorname{Link}(v)})=1
\qquad\forall v\in V.
\]

---

## 5. Final contradiction using only the sandwich

By the finite-cover upgrade from §1,
\[
\Omega(q_{\bigcap_{v\in V}\operatorname{Link}(v)})=1.
\]
Since the graph is loopless,
\[
\bigcap_{v\in V}\operatorname{Link}(v)=\varnothing,
\]
so
\[
\Omega(q_\varnothing)=1.
\]

For \(U=\varnothing\), \(M_\varnothing=\mathbb C1\), and \(e_\varnothing\) is the rank-one projection onto \(\mathbb C\widehat1\). The hereditary \(M\)-\(JMJ\) hull of \(e_\varnothing\) is exactly \(K(L^2M)\): indeed, operators
\[
L_a e_\varnothing L_b^*
\]
are rank-one operators \(\theta_{\widehat a,\widehat b}\), and \(M\widehat1\) is dense in \(L^2M\). Hence
\[
q_\varnothing=q_K.
\]
Therefore
\[
1=\Omega(q_\varnothing)=\Omega(q_K),
\]
contradicting the obstruction condition \(\Omega(q_K)=0\).

Thus \(M\) is properly proximal.

---

## Gap audit

No non-support gap remains, provided the standard DKEP normal-bidual obstruction theorem is used.

The main points checked are:

1. **Finite cover:** the GNS support-one argument replaces commutation; no product formula is used.
2. **DKEP compression:** only \(\Omega|_M\) needs to be normal; no normality of \(\Omega\) on the ambient bidual is used.
3. **AFP ping-pong:** the unique non-neighbor case works even for order-two trace-zero unitaries because the separating vertex \(z\in L\) is nonadjacent to \(w\), preventing cancellation.
4. **Sector projections:** the only commutator errors are \(e_B\rho(x)\) and \(\rho(x)e_B\), which are \(X_L\)-compact since \(e_B\in X_L\) and \(JMJ\) acts by multipliers.
5. **Amenable summand:** a central amenable summand gives a hypertrace, hence an \(M\)-central state on \(S_{X_L}(M)\) normal on \(M\), contradicting ping-pong.

The only external input left is precisely the assumed support sandwich
\[
q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_{U\cap W}.
\]
