Below is a **dependency-checked proof blueprint**, not polished final prose. The theorem-level assembly is sound once the **t9 support sandwich**
\[
q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_{U\cap W}
\]
and the stated DKEP normal-bidual obstruction package are imported.

---

## A. Conventions and objects

### A1. Irreducibility convention

Use the following precise convention:

> A finite simplicial graph \(\Gamma=(V,E)\) is **irreducible** if there is no partition
> \[
> V=V_1\sqcup V_2,\qquad V_1,V_2\ne\varnothing,
> \]
> such that every vertex of \(V_1\) is adjacent to every vertex of \(V_2\).

Equivalently, \(\Gamma\) has no **nontrivial join decomposition**. If one phrases this using subgraphs \(\Gamma_1,\Gamma_2\), the vertex sets must be **nonempty join factors**; otherwise an empty factor makes the condition vacuous. Since the graph is simplicial, it has no loops, and \(\operatorname{Link}(v)\) excludes \(v\). Caspers–Fima use the same loopless simplicial convention and define \(\operatorname{Star}(v)=\operatorname{Link}(v)\cup\{v\}\). ([webusers.imj-prg.fr](https://webusers.imj-prg.fr/~pierre.fima/Documents/GraphProducts.pdf))

Consequences needed later:

1. **\(\operatorname{Star}(v)\ne V\) for every \(v\).**  
   If \(\operatorname{Star}(v)=V\), then
   \[
   V=\{v\}\sqcup (V\setminus\{v\})
   \]
   is a nontrivial join.

2. Hence
   \[
   N(v):=V\setminus\operatorname{Star}(v)\ne\varnothing.
   \]

3. If \(N(v)=\{w\}\), then \(\operatorname{Link}(v)\ne\varnothing\) because \(|V|\ge3\). Moreover, there exists \(z\in\operatorname{Link}(v)\) not adjacent to \(w\); otherwise
   \[
   V=\{v,w\}\sqcup \operatorname{Link}(v)
   \]
   would be a nontrivial join.

---

## B. External dependency list

### B1. Graph-product facts required

Let \(M=\star_\Gamma(M_v,\tau_v)\) be the tracial graph product.

Needed graph-product inputs:

1. **Reduced-word dense algebra and parabolic expectations.**  
   The linear span of \(1\) and reduced graph-product words is weakly dense in \(M\). For every induced subgraph \(U\subset V\), there is a trace-preserving conditional expectation
   \[
   E_U:M\to M_U
   \]
   killing every reduced word containing a vertex outside \(U\). ([webusers.imj-prg.fr](https://webusers.imj-prg.fr/~pierre.fima/Documents/GraphProducts.pdf))

2. **Star-link AFP decomposition.**  
   For each \(v\in V\), with
   \[
   L=\operatorname{Link}(v),\quad B=M_L,\quad A=M_{\operatorname{Star}(v)},\quad C=M_{V\setminus\{v\}},
   \]
   one has
   \[
   A\cong M_v\bar\otimes B,\qquad E_B|_A=\tau_v\otimes\operatorname{id}_B,
   \]
   and
   \[
   M=A*_B C.
   \]
   This is exactly the star-link amalgamated free product decomposition. ([webusers.imj-prg.fr](https://webusers.imj-prg.fr/~pierre.fima/Documents/GraphProducts.pdf))

3. **Relative reduced-word conditional expectation.**  
   For the support-sandwich proof, one needs the conditional relative Wick formula: if \(x,\tilde x\) are graph-reduced relative to \((V_1,V_2)\), then
   \[
   E_{V_2}(\tilde x^*yx)=\tau(\tilde x^*x)E_U(y),\qquad y\in M_{V_1},
   \]
   where \(U\subset V_1\cap V_2\) is the set of vertices adjacent to the relevant middle word; if the relative words are not equivalent, the scalar is \(0\). This is Lemma 3.17 in the graph-product structure paper. ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743))

4. **\(q_\varnothing=q_K\).**  
   This is elementary from DKEP’s boundary-piece definition: for \(U=\varnothing\), \(M_\varnothing=\mathbb C1\), and \(e_\varnothing\) is the rank-one projection onto \(\mathbb C\widehat1\). The hereditary \(M\)-\(JMJ\) hull of \(e_\varnothing\) is exactly \(K(L^2M)\), since
   \[
   L_a e_\varnothing L_b^*=\theta_{\widehat a,\widehat b}
   \]
   and \(M\widehat1\) is dense in \(L^2M\). DKEP define subalgebra boundary pieces as the hereditary algebra generated by \(xJyJe_B\)-type operators. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

---

### B2. DKEP / DE facts required

Use the following DKEP package.

1. **Boundary pieces and \(S_X(M)\).**  
   For an \(M\)-boundary piece \(X\subset B(L^2M)\), define
   \[
   S_X(M)=\{T\in B(L^2M):[T,JxJ]\in K_X^{\infty,1}(M)\ \forall x\in M\}.
   \]
   DKEP prove \(K_X^{\infty,1}(M)=\overline{K_X(M)}^{\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}}\) and that it is a strong \(M\)-\(M\) and \(JMJ\)-\(JMJ\) bimodule, so the commutator condition can be checked on a von Neumann generating set. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

2. **Relative proper proximality.**  
   For the inclusion \(M\subset M\), \(M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X\) iff there is no \(M\)-central state on \(S_X(M)\) whose restriction to \(M\) is normal. DKEP formulate relative proper proximality in this way. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

3. **Normal-bidual formulation.**  
   Let
   \[
   \widetilde S_X(M)\subset (B(L^2M)^\sharp_J)^*
   \]
   be the normal-bidual compactification. DE/DKEP give the characterization:
   \[
   M\text{ properly proximal relative to }X
   \iff
   \widetilde S_X(M)\text{ has no }M\text{-central state normal on }M.
   \]
   ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2211.05298))

4. **Support projections.**  
   For each boundary piece \(X\), let \(q_X\) be the identity/support projection of the normal-bidual compact corner \((K_X(M)^\sharp_J)^*\). It commutes with both \(M\) and \(JMJ\). DKEP explicitly record this commutation. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

5. **Compression inclusion.**  
   DE record the useful normal-bidual compression fact
   \[
   q_X^\perp\,\widetilde S_X(M)\,q_X^\perp
   \subset
   q_K^\perp\,\widetilde S(M),
   \]
   using that \(q_X\) commutes with \(JMJ\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2211.05298))

6. **Obstruction state.**  
   Required final external input:
   > If \(M\) is separable tracial, has no amenable direct summand, and is not properly proximal, then there exists an \(M\)-central state
   > \[
   > \Omega:\widetilde S(M)\to\mathbb C
   > \]
   > normal on \(M\), such that
   > \[
   > \Omega(q_K)=0.
   > \]
   
   This is the normal-bidual version of the DKEP compact-corner obstruction; it is the only major DKEP theorem-level input beyond definitions.

7. **Support-sandwich lemma from t9.**  
   For all \(U,W\subset V\),
   \[
   q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_{U\cap W}.
   \]
   Internally, t9 uses DE’s approximate-unit lemma and \(e_N\)-commutation with \(p_{\mathrm{nor}}\), plus the relative graph-product expectation formula. DE’s Lemmas 3.7 and 3.8 give the approximate-unit convergence and \(e_N\)-commutation inputs. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2211.05298))

---

## C. Proof blueprint

### Step 1. Setup and separability

Because \(V\) is finite and each \(M_v\) is separable tracial, the graph product \(M\) is separable tracial: take countable \(\|\cdot\|_2\)-dense subsets in each \(M_v\), form countably many reduced words, and use weak/\(\|\cdot\|_2\)-density.

Thus the DKEP separability hypotheses apply.

---

### Step 2. Fixed-vertex AFP decomposition

Fix \(v\in V\). Set
\[
L=\operatorname{Link}(v),\quad B=M_L,\quad A=M_{\operatorname{Star}(v)},\quad C=M_{V\setminus\{v\}}.
\]
Then
\[
M=A*_B C,\qquad A\cong M_v\bar\otimes B.
\]

Choose a trace-zero unitary \(u_v\in M_v\), and set
\[
a=u_v\otimes1_B\in A.
\]
Then \(a\) is unitary and \(E_B(a)=0\), so \(a\in A^\circ:=\ker E_B|_A\).

---

### Step 3. Construct two \(B\)-orthogonal \(C^\circ\)-unitaries

Need unitaries \(c_1,c_2\in C\) such that
\[
E_B(c_i)=0,\qquad E_B(c_i^*c_j)=\delta_{ij}1.
\]

#### Case 1: \(|N(v)|\ge2\)

Pick distinct \(w_1,w_2\in N(v)\), and trace-zero unitaries \(u_{w_i}\in M_{w_i}\). Set
\[
c_i=u_{w_i}.
\]
Then \(c_i\in C^\circ\), \(E_B(c_i^*c_i)=1\), and \(E_B(c_1^*c_2)=0\) because the reduced word contains vertices outside \(L\).

#### Case 2: \(N(v)=\{w\}\)

By irreducibility, choose \(z\in L\) not adjacent to \(w\). Pick trace-zero unitaries \(u_w\in M_w\) and \(u_z\in M_z\subset B\). Set
\[
c_1=u_w,\qquad c_2=u_z u_w.
\]
Then \(c_1,c_2\in C^\circ\), and
\[
c_1^*c_2=u_w^*u_z u_w
\]
is a graph-reduced word of type \(w,z,w\), because \(w\) and \(z\) are not adjacent. Therefore its \(B\)-expectation is zero. This also handles order-two unitaries: the middle noncommuting \(u_z\) prevents cancellation.

---

### Step 4. AFP sector projections lie in \(S_{X_L}(M)\)

Use the AFP \(L^2\)-decomposition
\[
L^2(M)=L^2(B)\oplus
\bigoplus_{\epsilon_1\ne\cdots\ne\epsilon_n}
K_{\epsilon_1}\otimes_B\cdots\otimes_B K_{\epsilon_n},
\]
where
\[
K_A=L^2(A)\ominus L^2(B),\qquad K_C=L^2(C)\ominus L^2(B).
\]

Let:

- \(R_A\): projection onto \(L^2(B)\) plus words whose first AFP letter is \(A\);
- \(R_C\): projection onto \(L^2(B)\) plus words whose first AFP letter is \(C\);
- \(P_A=R_A-e_B\);
- \(P_C=R_C-e_B\).

Then
\[
1=e_B+P_A+P_C.
\]

For \(d\in A^\circ\), right multiplication by \(d\) preserves \(R_A L^2(M)\) and its orthogonal complement, so
\[
[R_A,\rho(d)]=0,\qquad \rho(d)=Jd^*J.
\]
Similarly, for \(d\in C^\circ\),
\[
[R_C,\rho(d)]=0.
\]
Right multiplication by \(B\) preserves every AFP sector. Since
\[
R_C=1-R_A+e_B,\qquad R_A=1-R_C+e_B,
\]
the only commutator errors are \([e_B,\rho(d)]\), which lie in \(K_{X_L}^{\infty,1}(M)\) because \(e_B\in X_L\) and \(JMJ\) acts by multipliers.

By the DKEP generator criterion:
\[
R_A,R_C,P_A,P_C\in S_{X_L}(M).
\]

---

### Step 5. Ping-pong projection inequalities

Let
\[
H_A^+=R_A L^2(M),\qquad H_C^+=R_C L^2(M).
\]

If \(d\in C^\circ\), then left multiplication by \(d\) sends \(H_A^+\) into \(P_C L^2(M)\). Hence
\[
c_iR_Ac_i^*\le P_C.
\]
Because \(E_B(c_1^*c_2)=0\), the same argument applied to \(d=c_1^*c_2\in C^\circ\) gives
\[
c_1H_A^+\perp c_2H_A^+.
\]
Thus
\[
c_1R_Ac_1^*+c_2R_Ac_2^*\le P_C.
\]

Similarly, since \(a\in A^\circ\),
\[
aR_Ca^*\le P_A.
\]

---

### Step 6. No central state on \(S_{X_L}(M)\)

Let \(\varphi\) be an \(M\)-central state on \(S_{X_L}(M)\). Since \(a,c_1,c_2\in M\) are unitaries,
\[
\varphi(uTu^*)=\varphi(T).
\]

From the inequalities:
\[
2\varphi(R_A)\le \varphi(P_C),
\qquad
\varphi(R_C)\le \varphi(P_A).
\]
Set
\[
\alpha=\varphi(e_B),\quad \beta=\varphi(P_A),\quad \gamma=\varphi(P_C).
\]
Then
\[
\alpha+\beta+\gamma=1,
\]
and
\[
2(\alpha+\beta)\le \gamma,\qquad \alpha+\gamma\le \beta.
\]
Combining:
\[
\beta\ge \alpha+\gamma\ge \alpha+2(\alpha+\beta)=3\alpha+2\beta,
\]
so \(\alpha=\beta=0\), and then \(\gamma=0\), contradiction.

Therefore \(S_{X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}}(M)\) has no \(M\)-central state. In particular,
\[
M\text{ is properly proximal relative to }X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}
\]
for every \(v\in V\).

---

### Step 7. No amenable direct summand

Assume \(0\ne z\in Z(M)\) and \(zM\) is amenable. Let
\[
H_z=zL^2(M)\cong L^2(zM).
\]
Amenability gives a \(zM\)-central hypertrace \(\psi\) on \(B(H_z)\) extending the normalized trace on \(zM\).

Fix \(v\), and set \(L=\operatorname{Link}(v)\). For \(T\in S_{X_L}(M)\), define
\[
\Phi(T)=\psi(zTz|_{H_z}).
\]
Then \(\Phi\) is a state. For \(x\in M\),
\[
\Phi(xT)=\psi((zx)(zTz))=\psi((zTz)(zx))=\Phi(Tx),
\]
so \(\Phi\) is \(M\)-central. Also
\[
\Phi(x)=\psi(zxz)=\tau_z(zx),
\]
so \(\Phi|_M\) is normal.

This contradicts Step 6. Hence \(M\) has no amenable direct summand.

---

### Step 8. DKEP obstruction and compression to link supports

Assume, for contradiction, that \(M\) is not properly proximal. Since \(M\) has no amenable direct summand, DKEP gives an \(M\)-central state
\[
\Omega:\widetilde S(M)\to\mathbb C
\]
normal on \(M\), with
\[
\Omega(q_K)=0.
\]

Now prove the compression claim:

> If \(M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X\), then \(\Omega(q_X)=1\).

Let \(r=1-q_X\). If \(\Omega(r)>0\), define for \(T\in\widetilde S_X(M)\)
\[
\Phi(T)=\frac{\Omega(rTr)}{\Omega(r)}.
\]
This is well-defined because
\[
r\widetilde S_X(M)r\subset \widetilde S(M).
\]
It is \(M\)-central since \(r\) commutes with \(M\). For \(x\in M_+\),
\[
0\le r x r\le x,
\]
so
\[
0\le \Phi(x)\le \frac{\Omega(x)}{\Omega(r)}.
\]
Thus \(\Phi|_M\) is normal by domination by a normal positive functional.

This contradicts relative proper proximality to \(X\). Hence \(\Omega(q_X)=1\).

Applying this to \(X=X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}\):
\[
\Omega(q_{\operatorname{Link}(v)})=1
\qquad\forall v\in V.
\]

---

### Step 9. Finite-cover step using only the one-sided sandwich

Let \(\phi\) be any state on the ambient normal-bidual \(C^*\)-algebra or von Neumann algebra containing the \(q_U\)’s. If \(\phi\) is initially only a state on the operator system \(\widetilde S(M)\), extend it to the ambient \(C^*\)-algebra first.

If \(\phi(q_U)=1\), then in the GNS representation,
\[
\pi(q_U)\xi=\xi.
\]
Therefore, if also \(\phi(q_W)=1\),
\[
1=\phi(q_W)=\phi(q_Uq_Wq_U).
\]
By the support sandwich,
\[
0\le q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_{U\cap W}.
\]
Hence
\[
1\le \phi(q_{U\cap W})\le1,
\]
so
\[
\phi(q_{U\cap W})=1.
\]

Iterating over the finite family
\[
U_v=\operatorname{Link}(v),\qquad v\in V,
\]
gives
\[
\Omega\left(q_{\bigcap_{v\in V}\operatorname{Link}(v)}\right)=1.
\]
Since the graph has no loops,
\[
\bigcap_{v\in V}\operatorname{Link}(v)=\varnothing.
\]
Therefore
\[
\Omega(q_\varnothing)=1.
\]
But \(q_\varnothing=q_K\), so
\[
1=\Omega(q_\varnothing)=\Omega(q_K),
\]
contradicting \(\Omega(q_K)=0\).

Thus \(M\) is properly proximal.

---

## D. Remaining phrasing pitfalls for the final write-up

1. **Do not allow empty join factors.**  
   State irreducibility as “no nontrivial join partition into two nonempty parts.”

2. **Do not claim support projections commute.**  
   The finite-cover step uses only
   \[
   q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_{U\cap W}.
   \]

3. **Do not claim \(X_UX_W\subset X_{U\cap W}\).**  
   t9 only proves the special triple-product support sandwich.

4. **Do not use global multiplicativity of \(\iota_{\mathrm{nor}}\).**  
   In t9, multiplicativity is only used on \(C^*(M,JMJ,e_U,e_W)\), where \(p_{\mathrm{nor}}\) commutes with the generators.

5. **Keep concrete \(S_X(M)\) and normal-bidual \(\widetilde S_X(M)\) separate.**  
   Ping-pong is concrete; the obstruction/compression step is normal-bidual.

6. **In the finite-cover step, extend operator-system states before using GNS.**

7. **In the amenable-summand step, normalize the trace on \(zM\).**  
   The hypertrace lives on \(B(zL^2M)\), and compression by \(z\) makes the resulting state \(M\)-central.

8. **In the unique non-neighbor case, explicitly choose \(z\in\operatorname{Link}(v)\) not adjacent to \(w\).**  
   This is what prevents cancellation in \(u_w^*u_zu_w\).

9. **Prove \(q_\varnothing=q_K\), not merely \(q_\varnothing\ge q_K\).**  
   The boundary piece for \(M_\varnothing=\mathbb C\) is exactly \(K(L^2M)\).

10. **State the DKEP compact-corner obstruction explicitly.**  
    The contradiction requires an obstruction state satisfying \(\Omega(q_K)=0\), not just an arbitrary central state.

---

## Task t13 — Hostile audit of the final DKEP obstruction and normal-bidual hypotheses
[unverified self-report — verification not run or not yet complete | self_declared_is_relaxation: False | self_declared_problem_solved (the agent's own claim, NOT verified): Prove the following statement:

Let $\Gamma$ be a finite simplicial graph with $|\VV(\GG)|\geq 3$, and $(M_v,\varphi_v)$ be a separable tracial von Neumann algebra for each $v\in \VV$. If $\Gamma$ is irreducible (in the sense that there does not exists subgraphs $\GG_1,\GG_2$ such that $ \VV(\GG_1)\cup \VV(\GG_2)=\VV(\GG) $ and every $(v,u)\in \EE(\GG) $ for all $v\in\VV(\GG_1),u\in \VV(\GG_2)$), and each $M_v$ has a trace zero unitary, then the graph product von Neumann algebra $M$ is properly proximal.]
Source: task_outputs.jsonl — raw agent output. The values in the metadata line above are self-declared and have not been checked. Treat the proof as a draft, not as established.
## Verdict

**Accept with dependency patches.** The final argument is salvageable and does **not** need a separate proof that the graph product is a nonamenable factor. However, it must **not** directly apply DKEP Lemma 8.6 to the trivial action unless factor/center-ergodicity is first established. The safer and sufficient route is:

1. prove **no amenable direct summand** by the ping-pong argument;
2. use the **general normal-bidual characterization** of relative proper proximality, not DKEP 8.6 in its center-ergodic action form;
3. derive the obstruction state \(\Omega\) with \(\Omega(q_K)=0\) from no amenable direct summand;
4. extend states to an ambient \(C^*\)-algebra before the final GNS finite-cover argument.

DKEP Lemma 8.5 is stated for actions with \(\Gamma\curvearrowright Z(M)\) ergodic, and Lemma 8.6 says “using the same notation,” so it inherits that hypothesis. For the trivial action, that means \(Z(M)=\mathbb C\). Thus, a literal citation of Lemma 8.6 for the graph product would be a gap unless the proof establishes factoriality. But factoriality is unnecessary: the no-amenable-direct-summand argument plus the general DE/DKEP normal-bidual formulation suffices. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

---

## Gaps and patches

### 1. **Trivial-action / center-ergodicity issue**

**Gap if written naively:** “Apply DKEP Lemma 8.6 to the trivial action” is not justified unless \(M\) is a factor. DKEP define properly proximal actions with weak compactness and compact-vanishing alternatives, then Lemma 8.5 assumes \(\Gamma\curvearrowright Z(M)\) is ergodic; Lemma 8.6 is a variant “using the same notation,” so it keeps that hypothesis. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

**Patch:** Do **not** cite Lemma 8.6 directly. Cite the general bidual characterization: for a separable tracial \(M\) and boundary piece \(X\), \(M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X\) iff there is no \(M\)-central state on \(\widetilde S_X(M)\) whose restriction to \(M\) is normal. This is stated as DE Lemma 3.5. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2211.05298))

### 2. **Concrete relative proper proximality \(\Rightarrow\) normal-bidual relative proper proximality**

**Gap:** The ping-pong proof gives no \(M\)-central state on the concrete operator system \(S_{X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}}(M)\). The later compression argument produces a state on \(\widetilde S_X(M)\), not on concrete \(S_X(M)\).

**Patch:** Insert: by DKEP Theorem 6.2, the absence of an \(M\)-central state normal on \(M\) is precisely concrete relative proper proximality; by DE Lemma 3.5, this is equivalent to the absence of such a state on \(\widetilde S_X(M)\). DKEP define \(S_X(M)\) and relative proper proximality in exactly this form. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

### 3. **Obstruction state with \(\Omega(q_K)=0\)**

**Gap:** The final proof needs an obstruction state on \(\widetilde S(M)\) satisfying \(\Omega(q_K)=0\). This is stronger than merely “\(M\) is not properly proximal.”

**Patch:** State and prove/cite the following derived lemma:

> If \(M\) is separable, has no amenable direct summand, and is not properly proximal, then there exists an \(M\)-central state \(\Omega\) on \(\widetilde S(M)\), normal on \(M\), such that \(\Omega(q_K)=0\).

Derivation: non-proper proximality gives \(\Omega\) by DE Lemma 3.5 with \(X=K(L^2M)\). If \(\Omega(q_K)>0\), normalizing the \(q_K\)-corner gives an \(M\)-central state on \(B(L^2M)\) normal on \(M\), hence an amenable direct summand, contradiction. DE use exactly this support argument: if a state has positive mass on \(q_K\), restricting through the compact-corner normal operator system produces amenability. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2211.05298))

### 4. **Compression argument \(\Omega(q_X)=1\)**

**Mostly correct, but must cite the right support facts.**

Required ingredients:

- \(q_X\), the identity/support of \((K_X(M)^\sharp_J)^*\), commutes with \(M\) and \(JMJ\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))
- DE record the compression inclusion
  \[
  q_X^\perp\,\widetilde S_X(M)\,q_X^\perp
  \subset
  q_K^\perp\,\widetilde S(M).
  \]
  ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2211.05298))

Then, if \(r=1-q_X\) and \(\Omega(r)>0\), define
\[
\Phi(T)=\frac{\Omega(rTr)}{\Omega(r)},\qquad T\in\widetilde S_X(M).
\]
This is well-defined because \(r\widetilde S_X(M)r\subset \widetilde S(M)\). It is \(M\)-central because \(r\) commutes with \(M\). Its restriction to \(M\) is normal because for \(x\in M_+\),
\[
0\le r x r\le x
\]
and hence \(\Phi|_M\) is dominated by the normal functional \(\Omega|_M/\Omega(r)\). This contradicts relative proper proximality to \(X\). Therefore \(\Omega(q_X)=1\).

### 5. **Final finite-cover GNS argument**

**Patch needed:** Since \(\widetilde S(M)\) is an operator system, not a \(C^*\)-algebra in the concrete proof, extend \(\Omega\) to a state \(\bar\Omega\) on the ambient \(C^*\)-algebra/von Neumann algebra generated by the support projections before using GNS.

Then the argument is valid:

If \(\bar\Omega(q_U)=\bar\Omega(q_W)=1\), then in the GNS representation,
\[
\pi(q_U)\xi=\xi.
\]
Hence
\[
\bar\Omega(q_Uq_Wq_U)=\bar\Omega(q_W)=1.
\]
Using the support sandwich
\[
0\le q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_{U\cap W},
\]
we get \(\bar\Omega(q_{U\cap W})=1\). Iterate over \(U_v=\operatorname{Link}(v)\). No commutativity of the \(q_U\)’s is needed.

### 6. **\(q_\varnothing=q_K\)**

This is correct but should be explicitly proved.

For \(U=\varnothing\), \(M_\varnothing=\mathbb C1\), and \(e_\varnothing\) is the rank-one projection onto \(\mathbb C\widehat 1\). The DKEP subalgebra boundary piece for \(M_U\) is the hereditary algebra generated by left-right translates of \(e_U\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) For \(U=\varnothing\), those translates are finite-rank operators:
\[
L_a e_\varnothing L_b^*=\theta_{\widehat a,\widehat b}.
\]
Since \(M\widehat 1\) is dense in \(L^2M\), these span a norm-dense subalgebra of \(K(L^2M)\), and conversely all generators are compact. Hence
\[
X_\varnothing=K(L^2M),\qquad q_\varnothing=q_K.
\]

---

## Concise dependency list for insertion into the final proof

Use the following external inputs.

1. **Concrete relative proper proximality.**  
   For an \(M\)-boundary piece \(X\),
   \[
   S_X(M)=\{T\in B(L^2M):[T,JxJ]\in K_X^{\infty,1}(M)\ \forall x\in M\}.
   \]
   The inclusion \(M\subset M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X\) iff there is no \(M\)-central state on \(S_X(M)\) whose restriction to \(M\) is normal. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

2. **Normal-bidual formulation.**  
   For separable tracial \(M\), proper proximality relative to \(X\) is equivalent to the nonexistence of an \(M\)-central state on
   \[
   \widetilde S_X(M)
   =
   \{T\in (B(L^2M)^\sharp_J)^*:[T,a]\in (K_X(M)^\sharp_J)^*\ \forall a\in JMJ\}
   \]
   whose restriction to \(M\) is normal. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2211.05298))

3. **Support projections.**  
   Let \(q_X\) be the identity of \((K_X(M)^\sharp_J)^*\). Then \(q_X\) commutes with \(M\) and \(JMJ\). In particular \(q_X\in\widetilde S(M)\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

4. **Compression inclusion.**  
   For every boundary piece \(X\),
   \[
   q_X^\perp\widetilde S_X(M)q_X^\perp
   \subset
   q_K^\perp\widetilde S(M).
   \]
   This is the key input for proving \(\Omega(q_X)=1\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2211.05298))

5. **Obstruction state without factoriality.**  
   If \(M\) is separable, has no amenable direct summand, and is not properly proximal, then there is an \(M\)-central state \(\Omega\) on \(\widetilde S(M)\), normal on \(M\), with \(\Omega(q_K)=0\). This follows from the normal-bidual characterization plus the compact-corner support argument: positive mass on \(q_K\) would yield an amenable direct summand. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2211.05298))

6. **Relative-to-absolute support forcing.**  
   If \(M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X\) and \(\Omega\) is the obstruction state above, then \(\Omega(q_X)=1\). Proof: if \(r=1-q_X\) has positive \(\Omega\)-mass, the normalized compression \(T\mapsto \Omega(rTr)/\Omega(r)\) gives an \(M\)-central state on \(\widetilde S_X(M)\) normal on \(M\), contradiction.

7. **Support sandwich.**  
   For graph-product parabolic boundary pieces,
   \[
   q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_{U\cap W}.
   \]
   Use only this one-sided inequality; do not assume the \(q_U\)’s commute.

8. **Empty parabolic equals compact boundary.**  
   Since \(M_\varnothing=\mathbb C1\), \(e_\varnothing\) is rank-one and the boundary piece \(X_\varnothing\) is exactly \(K(L^2M)\). Thus \(q_\varnothing=q_K\).

9. **Final state extension.**  
   Before applying GNS to products \(q_Uq_Wq_U\), extend \(\Omega\) from \(\widetilde S(M)\) to a state on the ambient \(C^*\)-algebra generated by the support projections. Then the support-one GNS argument is legitimate.

With these insertions, the final proof’s dependency chain is sound, and the trivial-action issue is neutralized without proving that the graph product is a factor.

## Task t14 — Independent verification of graph-theoretic and factor/no-amenable-summand prerequisites
[unverified self-report — verification not run or not yet complete | self_declared_is_relaxation: False | self_declared_problem_solved (the agent's own claim, NOT verified): Prove the following statement:

Let $\Gamma$ be a finite simplicial graph with $|\VV(\GG)|\geq 3$, and $(M_v,\varphi_v)$ be a separable tracial von Neumann algebra for each $v\in \VV$. If $\Gamma$ is irreducible (in the sense that there does not exists subgraphs $\GG_1,\GG_2$ such that $ \VV(\GG_1)\cup \VV(\GG_2)=\VV(\GG) $ and every $(v,u)\in \EE(\GG) $ for all $v\in\VV(\GG_1),u\in \VV(\GG_2)$), and each $M_v$ has a trace zero unitary, then the graph product von Neumann algebra $M$ is properly proximal.]
Source: task_outputs.jsonl — raw agent output. The values in the metadata line above are self-declared and have not been checked. Treat the proof as a draft, not as established.
## Verdict

Under the intended **nontrivial join** convention, the graph prerequisites are sound, including the \(|V|=3\) cases. Moreover, the graph product \(M\) is a **separable nonamenable \(\mathrm{II}_1\) factor** under the stated hypotheses; in fact, a recent Rigid Graph Products theorem applies exactly and gives that \(M\) is a **prime factor**. Thus \(M\) has **no amenable direct summand**. The relative ping-pong argument also gives a direct backup proof of no amenable summand.

---

## 1. Irreducibility convention and graph lemmas

Use this convention:

\[
\Gamma \text{ is irreducible } \Longleftrightarrow
\text{there is no partition } V=V_1\sqcup V_2,\quad V_i\ne\varnothing,
\]
such that every vertex of \(V_1\) is adjacent to every vertex of \(V_2\).

This is the usual “no nontrivial join decomposition” convention. The literal problem wording must be read this way: if empty factors were allowed, every graph would be reducible vacuously; if overlapping factors were allowed in a loopless simplicial graph, an overlap point would require a loop. The graph-product literature uses finite simple loopless graphs and writes \(S(v)=\operatorname{Link}(v)\), \(B(v)=\operatorname{Star}(v)\). ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743))

### Lemma 1: \(\operatorname{Star}(v)\ne V\)

If \(\operatorname{Star}(v)=V\), then \(v\) is adjacent to every other vertex. Since \(|V|\ge3\), \(V\setminus\{v\}\ne\varnothing\), and

\[
V=\{v\}\sqcup (V\setminus\{v\})
\]

is a nontrivial join partition. Contradiction. Hence

\[
N(v):=V\setminus\operatorname{Star}(v)\ne\varnothing
\]

for every \(v\).

### Lemma 2: unique non-neighbor lemma

Suppose \(N(v)=\{w\}\). Then

\[
\operatorname{Link}(v)=V\setminus\{v,w\}
\]

is nonempty because \(|V|\ge3\). I claim there is \(z\in\operatorname{Link}(v)\) with \(z\not\sim w\).

If every \(z\in\operatorname{Link}(v)\) were adjacent to \(w\), then

\[
V=\{v,w\}\sqcup \operatorname{Link}(v)
\]

would be a nontrivial join: \(v\) is adjacent to all vertices in \(\operatorname{Link}(v)\) by definition, and \(w\) is adjacent to all of them by assumption. Contradiction.

Thus such a \(z\) exists.

### Edge cases \(|V|=3\)

For three vertices, the irreducible graphs are exactly:

1. the empty graph;
2. the graph with exactly one edge.

The triangle is reducible, and the two-edge path is reducible because the middle vertex is joined to both leaves. In the empty graph, \(|N(v)|=2\) for every \(v\). In the one-edge graph, an endpoint of the edge has a unique non-neighbor, and the other endpoint is the required \(z\in\operatorname{Link}(v)\) not adjacent to that non-neighbor. So the \(c_2=u_z u_w\) construction has no exceptional \(|V|=3\) failure.

---

## 2. Check of the \(c_2\) construction

Fix \(v\), set \(L=\operatorname{Link}(v)\), \(B=M_L\), and \(C=M_{V\setminus\{v\}}\). If \(N(v)=\{w\}\), choose \(z\in L\) with \(z\not\sim w\). Let \(u_w\in M_w\), \(u_z\in M_z\) be trace-zero unitaries and put

\[
c_1=u_w,\qquad c_2=u_z u_w.
\]

Then \(c_1,c_2\in C\), and \(E_B(c_i)=0\). Also

\[
c_1^*c_2=u_w^*u_z u_w
\]

is a graph-reduced word of type \(w,z,w\), because \(z\not\sim w\). It contains the vertex \(w\notin L\), so \(E_B(c_1^*c_2)=0\). Similarly \(E_B(c_2^*c_1)=0\), while \(E_B(c_i^*c_i)=1\). Conditional expectations onto graph-product subalgebras exist, and for reduced words the expectation onto \(M_{V_0}\) vanishes unless all letters lie in \(V_0\). ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743))

This also handles order-two unitaries: even if \(u_w=u_w^*=u_w^{-1}\), the middle \(u_z\) cannot be commuted past \(u_w\), so no cancellation occurs.

---

## 3. Structural status of \(M\)

The graph product is tracial when the vertex states are tracial, and it is generated by the vertex algebras with the graph-independence relations. Since \(V\) is finite and the \(M_v\)’s are separable, the reduced-word span from countable dense subsets is countable and \(\|\cdot\|_2\)-dense, so \(M\) is separable. ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743))

A single exact citation is available: **Rigid Graph Products, Theorem 8.11** states that if \(\Gamma\) is a finite irreducible simple graph with \(|\Gamma|\ge3\), and each \(M_v\ne\mathbb C\) has a normal faithful trace and a trace-zero unitary, then the graph product \(M_\Gamma\) is a **prime factor**; its proof also records that \(M_\Gamma\) is a \(\mathrm{II}_1\) factor and is nonamenable. This exactly matches the present hypotheses, since a trace-zero unitary forces \(M_v\ne\mathbb C\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2408.06171))

One can also verify factor/nonamenability directly from the structure theorem for graph-product von Neumann algebras. Main Theorem 0.5 says, under the state-zero-unitary hypothesis, that \(M\) is diffuse iff some vertex algebra is diffuse or the graph is not complete, and gives an explicit factoriality criterion in terms of universal vertices and co-universal nonedge pairs. ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743)) Under irreducibility with \(|V|\ge3\), the graph is not complete, has no universal vertex, and has no nonedge pair \(\{v,w\}\) adjacent to all other vertices, since either situation gives a nontrivial join. Therefore \(M\) is a diffuse finite factor, hence a \(\mathrm{II}_1\) factor.

For nonamenability, Proposition 6.3 says that a graph product with \(\dim M_v\ge2\) is amenable iff all \(M_v\) are amenable and every nonedge pair \(v,w\) has \(\dim M_v=\dim M_w=2\) and is adjacent to all other vertices. ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743)) Since irreducibility and \(|V|\ge3\) force the graph to be noncomplete, choose a nonedge \(v,w\). If \(v,w\) were adjacent to all other vertices, then \(\{v,w\}\sqcup(V\setminus\{v,w\})\) would be a nontrivial join. Thus the amenability criterion fails, so \(M\) is nonamenable.

Consequently, because \(M\) is a nonamenable factor, it has no nonzero amenable central summand.

---

## 4. DKEP obstruction prerequisite

For the DKEP obstruction used in the proof, the relevant algebraic prerequisite is **no amenable direct summand**. DKEP’s relative obstruction statement says that failure of relative proper proximality gives either an amenable central summand or an \(M\)-central state on \(S_X(M)\) normal on \(M\) and vanishing on the relevant compact part. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) Since \(M\) is a nonamenable factor, the amenable-summand alternative is ruled out.

If one phrases the obstruction in “weak compactness” language, then in the present factor case an \(M\)-central state on \(B(L^2M)\) extending the trace would be a hypertrace, hence would imply amenability. Thus nonamenability rules out that weak-compactness obstruction as well.

---

## 5. Backup: direct no-amenable-summand proof from ping-pong

Assume the relative ping-pong lemma has been proved: for every vertex \(v\), there is no \(M\)-central state on \(S_{X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}}(M)\) whose restriction to \(M\) is normal.

Suppose \(0\ne z\in Z(M)\) and \(zM\) is amenable. Let \(\psi\) be a \(zM\)-central hypertrace on \(B(zL^2M)\) extending the normalized trace on \(zM\). For \(T\in S_{X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}}(M)\), define

\[
\Phi(T)=\psi(zTz|_{zL^2M}).
\]

Then \(\Phi\) is a state, and for \(x\in M\),

\[
\Phi(xT)=\psi((zx)(zTz))
=\psi((zTz)(zx))
=\Phi(Tx).
\]

Thus \(\Phi\) is \(M\)-central. Also \(\Phi|_M\) is the normal functional \(x\mapsto \tau(z)^{-1}\tau(zx)\). This contradicts the relative ping-pong lemma. Hence \(M\) has no amenable direct summand, independently of the structural factor theorem.

---

## Caveats about wording

1. The problem’s irreducibility phrase must exclude empty join factors; otherwise the condition is vacuous/impossible in the wrong way.
2. “Subgraphs” should be read as their vertex sets; inducedness is irrelevant for the join condition.
3. The Rigid Graph Products Theorem 8.11 applies exactly to these hypotheses, but if one wants to avoid relying on that 2026 preprint, the older structure theorem plus the amenability criterion already proves that \(M\) is a nonamenable \(\mathrm{II}_1\) factor.

You are a hostile auditor of the final proof of the original graph-product proper proximality theorem. Treat the support sandwich q_U q_W q_U <= q_{U cap W} for parabolic boundary supports as a black box. Using the attached outputs, check the entire remaining logical chain: graph irreducibility conventions; existence of the B-centered ping-pong unitaries for every vertex; AFP decomposition M=M_Star(v) *_M_Link(v) M_{V minus {v}}; first-letter projections lying in S_X; the ping-pong contradiction to an M-central state on S_X; passage from concrete relative proper proximality to the normal-bidual relative statement; exclusion of amenable direct summands; existence of an absolute obstruction state Omega with Omega(q_K)=0; compression proof Omega(q_Link(v))=1; finite-cover iteration over all vertex links; and q_empty=q_K. Your goal is to find any remaining fatal gap, ambiguity, or overclaim. In particular, watch for accidental use of global normal-bidual multiplicativity, accidental use of q_U q_W equality, failure to extend the state before GNS, and confusion between normality of Omega on M versus normality on the ambient normal bidual. If the proof is sound, provide a concise final proof skeleton that can guide the write-up. End with a SOLVER_REPORT listing proven results, failed attempts, caveats, and exact wording changes needed.

writeup_tasks.statement: Let Gamma be a finite simplicial graph with at least three vertices, irreducible in the standard nontrivial join sense, and let (M_v,tau_v) be separable tracial von Neumann algebras such that every M_v contains a unitary u_v with tau_v(u_v)=0. Then the tracial graph product von Neumann algebra M_Gamma is properly proximal.
writeup_tasks.writeup_agent_prompt: Produce a fully self-contained proof of the stated theorem. The artifact must stand alone: define the graph product M, the standard nontrivial-join interpretation of irreducible, parabolic subalgebras M_U, links and stars, DKEP boundary pieces X_U associated with M_U, the concrete operator system S_X(M), the normal-bidual system tilde S_X(M), and the support projections q_U. Use the attached prior outputs only as source material; do not refer to them in the final text. The proof should have the following structure. First state the external DKEP/DE facts precisely as lemmas: concrete relative proper proximality is absence of M-central states normal on M on S_X(M); the normal-bidual version is equivalent; if M has no amenable direct summand and is not properly proximal then there is an M-central normal-on-M obstruction state Omega on the absolute normal-bidual system with Omega(q_K)=0; q_X commutes with M and JMJ and compression by 1-q_X gives Omega(q_X)=1 whenever M is properly proximal relative to X; and for parabolic graph-product boundary supports the one-sided support sandwich q_U q_W q_U <= q_{U cap W} holds. Include a proof or detailed proof sketch of the support sandwich from t11, but do not claim global multiplicativity of the normal-bidual embedding and do not claim q_U q_W=q_{U cap W}. Second, prove the fixed-vertex link-relative ping-pong lemma: for each v, set B=M_Link(v), A=M_Star(v), C=M_{V minus {v}}, use the AFP decomposition M=A *_B C, define first-letter projections R_A,R_C and P_A=R_A-e_B, P_C=R_C-e_B, show these projections lie in S_{X_B}(M), construct a in A and c1,c2 in C with E_B(a)=0, E_B(c_i)=0, E_B(c_i^* c_j)=delta_ij, including the unique non-neighbor case c1=u_w and c2=u_z u_w, and derive the projection inequalities c1 R_A c1^* + c2 R_A c2^* <= P_C and a R_C a^* <= P_A. Then show any M-central state on S_{X_B}(M) yields contradictory inequalities, so M is properly proximal relative to X_Link(v). Third, prove M has no amenable direct summand, either by the hypertrace compression argument from t13/t14 or by citing the known graph-product nonamenable factor theorem; prefer the hypertrace compression argument to avoid unnecessary dependence. Fourth, assume M is not properly proximal and take Omega with Omega(q_K)=0. Since M is relatively properly proximal to X_Link(v) for every v, prove Omega(q_Link(v))=1 for every v by the normal-bidual compression argument. Extend Omega to an ambient C*-algebra containing the q_U, and use the support-one GNS argument plus q_U q_W q_U <= q_{U cap W} to prove inductively that Omega(q_intersection of all links)=1. Since the graph is loopless, the intersection of all vertex links is empty; prove X_empty=K(L2M) and hence q_empty=q_K. This contradicts Omega(q_K)=0. Ensure the final proof never uses the stronger equality q_U q_W=q_{U cap W}, never uses global multiplicativity, and keeps concrete S_X(M) separate from tilde S_X(M).

plan.reasoning: The main proof architecture is now stable. The fixed-vertex AFP ping-pong gives proper proximality relative to every vertex-link boundary piece. The support sandwich q_U q_W q_U ≤ q_{U∩W} replaces the earlier over-strong support product formula and is enough for the finite-cover argument. The final contradiction uses the DKEP/DE obstruction state Ω with Ω(q_K)=0, relative proper proximality forcing Ω(q_Link(v))=1, and the finite iteration of the sandwich down to q_empty=q_K. The only serious remaining risk is write-up precision: the proof must state the DKEP/DE normal-bidual facts accurately, extend Ω before the GNS support-one argument, and avoid global multiplicativity or support commutativity claims.

new_proven_results.sketch: Use algebraic approximate units for the dense hereditary cores of X_U and X_W. Finite graph-product propagation factors algebraic U-W-U triple products through e_J-corners with J⊂I, hence through X_I. Restricted multiplicativity of the normal-bidual compression on C*(M,JMJ,e_U,e_W), plus weak-star order limits, gives the sandwich inequality.

new_proven_results.statement: Assuming the standard DKEP/DE obstruction and compression package, link-relative proper proximality for every vertex link implies absolute proper proximality.
new_proven_results.sketch: If M were not properly proximal and had no amenable direct summand, an obstruction state Ω would satisfy Ω(q_K)=0. Relative proper proximality to X_Link(v) forces Ω(q_Link(v))=1 for each v. The support-one GNS argument and q_U q_W q_U≤q_{U∩W} imply Ω(q_intersection of all links)=1. Since the graph is loopless, this intersection is empty, and q_empty=q_K, contradiction.

new_proven_results.statement: The graph-theoretic and ping-pong unitary construction is valid under the standard nontrivial join interpretation of irreducibility.
new_proven_results.sketch: Irreducibility implies Star(v)≠V for every v. If v has a unique non-neighbor w, irreducibility gives z in Link(v) not adjacent to w; then u_w and u_z u_w give the two required B-orthogonal C-unitaries, including order-two cases.

## Task t13 — Hostile audit of the final DKEP obstruction and normal-bidual hypotheses
[unverified self-report — verification not run or not yet complete | self_declared_is_relaxation: False | self_declared_problem_solved (the agent's own claim, NOT verified): Prove the following statement:

Let $\Gamma$ be a finite simplicial graph with $|\VV(\GG)|\geq 3$, and $(M_v,\varphi_v)$ be a separable tracial von Neumann algebra for each $v\in \VV$. If $\Gamma$ is irreducible (in the sense that there does not exists subgraphs $\GG_1,\GG_2$ such that $ \VV(\GG_1)\cup \VV(\GG_2)=\VV(\GG) $ and every $(v,u)\in \EE(\GG) $ for all $v\in\VV(\GG_1),u\in \VV(\GG_2)$), and each $M_v$ has a trace zero unitary, then the graph product von Neumann algebra $M$ is properly proximal.]
Source: task_outputs.jsonl — raw agent output. The values in the metadata line above are self-declared and have not been checked. Treat the proof as a draft, not as established.
## Verdict

**Accept with dependency patches.** The final argument is salvageable and does **not** need a separate proof that the graph product is a nonamenable factor. However, it must **not** directly apply DKEP Lemma 8.6 to the trivial action unless factor/center-ergodicity is first established. The safer and sufficient route is:

1. prove **no amenable direct summand** by the ping-pong argument;
2. use the **general normal-bidual characterization** of relative proper proximality, not DKEP 8.6 in its center-ergodic action form;
3. derive the obstruction state \(\Omega\) with \(\Omega(q_K)=0\) from no amenable direct summand;
4. extend states to an ambient \(C^*\)-algebra before the final GNS finite-cover argument.

DKEP Lemma 8.5 is stated for actions with \(\Gamma\curvearrowright Z(M)\) ergodic, and Lemma 8.6 says “using the same notation,” so it inherits that hypothesis. For the trivial action, that means \(Z(M)=\mathbb C\). Thus, a literal citation of Lemma 8.6 for the graph product would be a gap unless the proof establishes factoriality. But factoriality is unnecessary: the no-amenable-direct-summand argument plus the general DE/DKEP normal-bidual formulation suffices. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

---

## Gaps and patches

### 1. **Trivial-action / center-ergodicity issue**

**Gap if written naively:** “Apply DKEP Lemma 8.6 to the trivial action” is not justified unless \(M\) is a factor. DKEP define properly proximal actions with weak compactness and compact-vanishing alternatives, then Lemma 8.5 assumes \(\Gamma\curvearrowright Z(M)\) is ergodic; Lemma 8.6 is a variant “using the same notation,” so it keeps that hypothesis. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

**Patch:** Do **not** cite Lemma 8.6 directly. Cite the general bidual characterization: for a separable tracial \(M\) and boundary piece \(X\), \(M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X\) iff there is no \(M\)-central state on \(\widetilde S_X(M)\) whose restriction to \(M\) is normal. This is stated as DE Lemma 3.5. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2211.05298))

### 2. **Concrete relative proper proximality \(\Rightarrow\) normal-bidual relative proper proximality**

**Gap:** The ping-pong proof gives no \(M\)-central state on the concrete operator system \(S_{X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}}(M)\). The later compression argument produces a state on \(\widetilde S_X(M)\), not on concrete \(S_X(M)\).

**Patch:** Insert: by DKEP Theorem 6.2, the absence of an \(M\)-central state normal on \(M\) is precisely concrete relative proper proximality; by DE Lemma 3.5, this is equivalent to the absence of such a state on \(\widetilde S_X(M)\). DKEP define \(S_X(M)\) and relative proper proximality in exactly this form. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

### 3. **Obstruction state with \(\Omega(q_K)=0\)**

**Gap:** The final proof needs an obstruction state on \(\widetilde S(M)\) satisfying \(\Omega(q_K)=0\). This is stronger than merely “\(M\) is not properly proximal.”

**Patch:** State and prove/cite the following derived lemma:

> If \(M\) is separable, has no amenable direct summand, and is not properly proximal, then there exists an \(M\)-central state \(\Omega\) on \(\widetilde S(M)\), normal on \(M\), such that \(\Omega(q_K)=0\).

Derivation: non-proper proximality gives \(\Omega\) by DE Lemma 3.5 with \(X=K(L^2M)\). If \(\Omega(q_K)>0\), normalizing the \(q_K\)-corner gives an \(M\)-central state on \(B(L^2M)\) normal on \(M\), hence an amenable direct summand, contradiction. DE use exactly this support argument: if a state has positive mass on \(q_K\), restricting through the compact-corner normal operator system produces amenability. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2211.05298))

### 4. **Compression argument \(\Omega(q_X)=1\)**

**Mostly correct, but must cite the right support facts.**

Required ingredients:

- \(q_X\), the identity/support of \((K_X(M)^\sharp_J)^*\), commutes with \(M\) and \(JMJ\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))
- DE record the compression inclusion
  \[
  q_X^\perp\,\widetilde S_X(M)\,q_X^\perp
  \subset
  q_K^\perp\,\widetilde S(M).
  \]
  ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2211.05298))

Then, if \(r=1-q_X\) and \(\Omega(r)>0\), define
\[
\Phi(T)=\frac{\Omega(rTr)}{\Omega(r)},\qquad T\in\widetilde S_X(M).
\]
This is well-defined because \(r\widetilde S_X(M)r\subset \widetilde S(M)\). It is \(M\)-central because \(r\) commutes with \(M\). Its restriction to \(M\) is normal because for \(x\in M_+\),
\[
0\le r x r\le x
\]
and hence \(\Phi|_M\) is dominated by the normal functional \(\Omega|_M/\Omega(r)\). This contradicts relative proper proximality to \(X\). Therefore \(\Omega(q_X)=1\).

### 5. **Final finite-cover GNS argument**

**Patch needed:** Since \(\widetilde S(M)\) is an operator system, not a \(C^*\)-algebra in the concrete proof, extend \(\Omega\) to a state \(\bar\Omega\) on the ambient \(C^*\)-algebra/von Neumann algebra generated by the support projections before using GNS.

Then the argument is valid:

If \(\bar\Omega(q_U)=\bar\Omega(q_W)=1\), then in the GNS representation,
\[
\pi(q_U)\xi=\xi.
\]
Hence
\[
\bar\Omega(q_Uq_Wq_U)=\bar\Omega(q_W)=1.
\]
Using the support sandwich
\[
0\le q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_{U\cap W},
\]
we get \(\bar\Omega(q_{U\cap W})=1\). Iterate over \(U_v=\operatorname{Link}(v)\). No commutativity of the \(q_U\)’s is needed.

### 6. **\(q_\varnothing=q_K\)**

This is correct but should be explicitly proved.

For \(U=\varnothing\), \(M_\varnothing=\mathbb C1\), and \(e_\varnothing\) is the rank-one projection onto \(\mathbb C\widehat 1\). The DKEP subalgebra boundary piece for \(M_U\) is the hereditary algebra generated by left-right translates of \(e_U\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) For \(U=\varnothing\), those translates are finite-rank operators:
\[
L_a e_\varnothing L_b^*=\theta_{\widehat a,\widehat b}.
\]
Since \(M\widehat 1\) is dense in \(L^2M\), these span a norm-dense subalgebra of \(K(L^2M)\), and conversely all generators are compact. Hence
\[
X_\varnothing=K(L^2M),\qquad q_\varnothing=q_K.
\]

---

## Concise dependency list for insertion into the final proof

Use the following external inputs.

1. **Concrete relative proper proximality.**  
   For an \(M\)-boundary piece \(X\),
   \[
   S_X(M)=\{T\in B(L^2M):[T,JxJ]\in K_X^{\infty,1}(M)\ \forall x\in M\}.
   \]
   The inclusion \(M\subset M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X\) iff there is no \(M\)-central state on \(S_X(M)\) whose restriction to \(M\) is normal. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

2. **Normal-bidual formulation.**  
   For separable tracial \(M\), proper proximality relative to \(X\) is equivalent to the nonexistence of an \(M\)-central state on
   \[
   \widetilde S_X(M)
   =
   \{T\in (B(L^2M)^\sharp_J)^*:[T,a]\in (K_X(M)^\sharp_J)^*\ \forall a\in JMJ\}
   \]
   whose restriction to \(M\) is normal. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2211.05298))

3. **Support projections.**  
   Let \(q_X\) be the identity of \((K_X(M)^\sharp_J)^*\). Then \(q_X\) commutes with \(M\) and \(JMJ\). In particular \(q_X\in\widetilde S(M)\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

4. **Compression inclusion.**  
   For every boundary piece \(X\),
   \[
   q_X^\perp\widetilde S_X(M)q_X^\perp
   \subset
   q_K^\perp\widetilde S(M).
   \]
   This is the key input for proving \(\Omega(q_X)=1\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2211.05298))

5. **Obstruction state without factoriality.**  
   If \(M\) is separable, has no amenable direct summand, and is not properly proximal, then there is an \(M\)-central state \(\Omega\) on \(\widetilde S(M)\), normal on \(M\), with \(\Omega(q_K)=0\). This follows from the normal-bidual characterization plus the compact-corner support argument: positive mass on \(q_K\) would yield an amenable direct summand. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2211.05298))

6. **Relative-to-absolute support forcing.**  
   If \(M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X\) and \(\Omega\) is the obstruction state above, then \(\Omega(q_X)=1\). Proof: if \(r=1-q_X\) has positive \(\Omega\)-mass, the normalized compression \(T\mapsto \Omega(rTr)/\Omega(r)\) gives an \(M\)-central state on \(\widetilde S_X(M)\) normal on \(M\), contradiction.

7. **Support sandwich.**  
   For graph-product parabolic boundary pieces,
   \[
   q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_{U\cap W}.
   \]
   Use only this one-sided inequality; do not assume the \(q_U\)’s commute.

8. **Empty parabolic equals compact boundary.**  
   Since \(M_\varnothing=\mathbb C1\), \(e_\varnothing\) is rank-one and the boundary piece \(X_\varnothing\) is exactly \(K(L^2M)\). Thus \(q_\varnothing=q_K\).

9. **Final state extension.**  
   Before applying GNS to products \(q_Uq_Wq_U\), extend \(\Omega\) from \(\widetilde S(M)\) to a state on the ambient \(C^*\)-algebra generated by the support projections. Then the support-one GNS argument is legitimate.

With these insertions, the final proof’s dependency chain is sound, and the trivial-action issue is neutralized without proving that the graph product is a factor.

## Task t15 — Final hostile rewrite of the parabolic support-sandwich lemma

Let $\Gamma$ be a finite simplicial graph with $|\VV(\GG)|\geq 3$, and $(M_v,\varphi_v)$ be a separable tracial von Neumann algebra for each $v\in \VV$. If $\Gamma$ is irreducible (in the sense that there does not exists subgraphs $\GG_1,\GG_2$ such that $ \VV(\GG_1)\cup \VV(\GG_2)=\VV(\GG) $ and every $(v,u)\in \EE(\GG) $ for all $v\in\VV(\GG_1),u\in \VV(\GG_2)$), and each $M_v$ has a trace zero unitary, then the graph product von Neumann algebra $M$ is properly proximal.]
Source: task_outputs.jsonl — raw agent output. The values in the metadata line above are self-declared and have not been checked. Treat the proof as a draft, not as established.
## Lemma (support sandwich)

Let \(M=\star_{\Gamma}(M_v,\tau_v)\) be a finite tracial graph-product von Neumann algebra over a finite graph \(\Gamma=(V,E)\). For \(S\subset V\), let \(M_S\subset M\) be the parabolic subalgebra, \(E_S:M\to M_S\) the trace-preserving conditional expectation, and \(e_S:L^2(M)\to L^2(M_S)\) the Jones projection. Let \(X_S\) be the DKEP boundary piece associated with \(M_S\), and let
\[
q_S\in \mathcal N:=(B(L^2M)^\sharp_J)^*
\]
be the identity/support projection of the normal-bidual compact corner
\[
(K_{X_S}(M)^\sharp_J)^*\subset \mathcal N .
\]
Then for all \(U,W\subset V\), with \(I=U\cap W\),
\[
\boxed{q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_I.}
\]

No commutativity of \(q_U,q_W\) is asserted or used.

---

## DE/DKEP inputs used

1. **Subalgebra boundary pieces.** For \(B\subset M\), DKEP define \(X_B\) as the norm-closed span of left/right \(M\)-translates of the large corner \(e_BB(L^2M)e_B\); equivalently it is the hereditary \(C^*\)-subalgebra generated by \(xJyJe_B\). They also define \(K_X(M)\) and \(K_X^{\infty,1}(M)\), with \(K_X^{\infty,1}(M)\) the \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-closure of \(K_X(M)\), equivalently of \(X\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

2. **Normal-bidual map.** Write \(\iota:B(L^2M)\to B(L^2M)^{**}\) for the universal representation and
\[
\iota_{\rm nor}(T)=p_{\rm nor}\iota(T)p_{\rm nor}.
\]
DE explicitly note that \(\iota_{\rm nor}\) is not globally a \(*\)-homomorphism. We use multiplicativity only on \(C^*(M,JMJ,e_U,e_W)\), where \(p_{\rm nor}\) reduces the universal images of the generators. DE Lemma 3.8 gives \(\iota(e_N)p_{\rm nor}=p_{\rm nor}\iota(e_N)\) for every von Neumann subalgebra \(N\subset M\), hence for \(N=M_U,M_W\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2211.05298))

3. **Support limits.** DE Lemma 3.7 says that if \(X_0\subset K_X(M)\) is \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-dense in \(K_X^{\infty,1}(M)\), and \((h_\lambda)\) is an approximate unit of \(X_0\) with \(\iota(h_\lambda)\) commuting with \(p_{\rm nor}\), then \(\iota_{\rm nor}(h_\lambda)\to q_X\) weak-* in \((K_X(M)^\sharp_J)^*\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2211.05298))

4. **Graph-product normal forms.** We use the relative reduced-word normal form and the conditional expectation formula: every word admits a relative decomposition, and if \(x,\tilde x\) are reduced relative to \((V_1,V_2)\), then
\[
E_{V_2}(\tilde x^*\,y\,x)=\tau(\tilde x^*x)\,E_J(y),\qquad y\in M_{V_1},
\]
with \(J\subset V_1\cap V_2\) the set of vertices adjacent to all middle letters; if the middle words are not equivalent, the expression is \(0\). ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743))

## Proof

Let \(H=L^2(M)\). For \(a,b\in M\), write
\[
L_aR_b(\widehat x)=\widehat{axb},\qquad R_b=Jb^*J.
\]
Let \(\mathcal A\subset M\) be the algebraic graph-product \(*\)-algebra spanned by reduced Wick words, and let
\[
\mathcal C=\operatorname{span}\{L_aR_b:a,b\in\mathcal A\}.
\]
For \(S\subset V\), set \(\mathcal A_S=\mathcal A\cap M_S\) and
\[
\mathcal C_S=\operatorname{span}\{L_aR_b:a,b\in\mathcal A_S\}.
\]

### 1. Algebraic approximate units for \(q_S\)

For \(S\subset V\), define
\[
\mathscr L_S=\{L_aR_b e_S:a,b\in\mathcal A\},
\]
and let
\[
D_S=\overline{\operatorname{span}}\{sTt^*:s,t\in\mathscr L_S,\ T\in B(H)\}^{\|\cdot\|}
\subset X_S.
\]
This is the hereditary \(C^*\)-subalgebra of \(X_S\) generated by the algebraic left-right translates of \(e_S\).

The algebraic core \(D_S\) is \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-dense in \(K_{X_S}^{\infty,1}(M)\). Indeed, if \(x,y\in M\) and \(a,b\in\mathcal A\) are bounded \(\|\cdot\|_2\)-approximants, then
\[
\|L_xR_y e_S-L_aR_b e_S\|_{\infty,2}
\le
\|y\|_\infty\|x-a\|_2+\|a\|_\infty\|y-b\|_2,
\]
and the same estimate holds for adjoints. Using
\[
\|ATB\|_{\infty,1}\le \|A^*\|_{\infty,2}\,\|T\|\,\|B\|_{\infty,2},
\]
one approximates every generator \(sTt^*\) of \(X_S\) by algebraic ones. Hence \(D_S\) is \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-dense in \(K_{X_S}^{\infty,1}(M)\).

For finite \(F\subset\mathscr L_S\) and \(\varepsilon>0\), put
\[
A_{F,S}=\sum_{s\in F}ss^*,\qquad
h^S_{F,\varepsilon}=A_{F,S}(A_{F,S}+\varepsilon)^{-1}.
\]
The net \((h^S_{F,\varepsilon})\) is a positive contractive approximate unit for \(D_S\). Moreover,
\[
h^S_{F,\varepsilon}\in C^*(M,JMJ,e_S).
\]
Since \(p_{\rm nor}\) commutes with the universal images of \(M,JMJ\), and by DE Lemma 3.8 also with \(\iota(e_S)\), DE Lemma 3.7 gives
\[
\iota_{\rm nor}(h^S_{F,\varepsilon})\xrightarrow{\ \sigma\text{-weak}\ }q_S.
\tag{1}
\]

### 2. Restricted multiplicativity

Let
\[
A_{U,W}=C^*(M,JMJ,e_U,e_W)\subset B(H).
\]
The projection \(p_{\rm nor}\) commutes with \(\iota(A_{U,W})\). Therefore, for \(x,y\in A_{U,W}\),
\[
\iota_{\rm nor}(xy)
=p_{\rm nor}\iota(x)\iota(y)p_{\rm nor}
=p_{\rm nor}\iota(x)p_{\rm nor}\iota(y)p_{\rm nor}
=\iota_{\rm nor}(x)\iota_{\rm nor}(y).
\tag{2}
\]
This is the only multiplicativity used below.

### 3. Finite propagation through intersections

We prove the key algebraic propagation estimate:
\[
\boxed{
e_S\mathcal C e_T
\subset
\operatorname{span}\{\mathcal C_S\,e_J\,\mathcal C_T:J\subset S\cap T\}.
}
\tag{FP}
\]

It suffices to prove this for \(C=L_aR_b\), where \(a,b\) are reduced Wick words. Use the relative normal form for \(a\) with respect to \((S,T)\):
\[
a=a_Sa_0a_T,
\]
where \(a_S\in\mathcal A_S\), \(a_T\in\mathcal A_T\), and the middle word \(a_0\) is reduced relative to \((S,T)\). Similarly decompose \(b\) with respect to \((T,S)\):
\[
b=b_Tb_0b_S,
\]
where \(b_T\in\mathcal A_T\), \(b_S\in\mathcal A_S\), and \(b_0\) is reduced relative to \((T,S)\). Thus \(a_0^*\) is also reduced relative to \((T,S)\).

For \(z\in\mathcal A_T\),
\[
E_S(azb)
=
a_S\,E_S\!\left(a_0(a_Tzb_T)b_0\right)b_S.
\]
Apply the graph-product conditional expectation formula with \(V_1=T\), \(V_2=S\), \(y=a_Tzb_T\), \(\tilde x=a_0^*\), and \(x=b_0\). If the middle words are not equivalent, the expectation is \(0\). If they are equivalent, then
\[
E_S\!\left(a_0(a_Tzb_T)b_0\right)
=
\tau(a_0b_0)\,E_J(a_Tzb_T),
\]
where \(J\subset S\cap T\) is the set of vertices adjacent to all letters of the middle word. Hence, as operators \(L^2(M_T)\to L^2(M_S)\),
\[
e_SL_aR_b e_T
=
\tau(a_0b_0)\,
L_{a_S}R_{b_S}\,e_J\,L_{a_T}R_{b_T}.
\]
Here we used \(e_JL_{a_T}R_{b_T}e_T=e_JL_{a_T}R_{b_T}\), since \(J\subset T\). Linearity gives (FP).

### 4. Algebraic \(U\)-\(W\)-\(U\) triples land in \(X_I\)

Let \(I=U\cap W\). Define
\[
\mathcal P_S
=
\operatorname{span}\{C_0e_SC_1e_S\cdots e_SC_n:
n\ge1,\ C_i\in\mathcal C\}.
\]
Each \(h^S_{F,\varepsilon}\) belongs to the norm closure of \(\mathcal P_S\), because \(A_{F,S}=\sum ss^*\in\mathcal P_S\), \(\mathcal P_S\) is an algebra, and \(t(t+\varepsilon)^{-1}\) is uniformly approximable by polynomials with zero constant term.

Now define the algebraic \(I\)-corner class
\[
\mathcal Z_I
=
\operatorname{span}\{A e_J T e_K B:
A,B\in\mathcal C,\ J,K\subset I,\ T\in B(H)\}.
\]
Since \(e_J,e_K\le e_I\), we have \(e_JTe_K\in e_IB(H)e_I\). Therefore
\[
\mathcal Z_I\subset X_I.
\tag{3}
\]

We need two elementary facts.

First, for \(S=U\) or \(S=W\),
\[
\mathcal P_S\mathcal Z_I\subset\mathcal Z_I,
\qquad
\mathcal Z_I\mathcal P_S\subset\mathcal Z_I.
\tag{4}
\]
Indeed, \(I\subset S\). If \(J\subset I\) and \(C\in\mathcal C\), then (FP) gives
\[
e_SC e_J\in \operatorname{span}\{\mathcal C_S e_L\mathcal C_J:L\subset J\},
\]
and similarly
\[
e_JC e_S\in \operatorname{span}\{\mathcal C_J e_L\mathcal C_S:L\subset J\}.
\]
Thus multiplying a generator \(Ae_JTe_KB\) of \(\mathcal Z_I\) on the left or right by one \(e_SC\)-block keeps all intermediate projections \(e_L\) with \(L\subset I\). Iteration gives (4).

Second, for \(A,B\in\mathcal C\) and \(T\in B(H)\),
\[
e_UAe_W\,T\,e_WBe_U\in\mathcal Z_I.
\tag{5}
\]
By (FP),
\[
e_UAe_W=\sum_r A_r e_{J_r}B_r,\qquad
e_WBe_U=\sum_s C_s e_{K_s}D_s,
\]
with \(J_r,K_s\subset I\). Therefore
\[
e_UAe_W\,T\,e_WBe_U
=
\sum_{r,s}
A_r e_{J_r}(B_rTC_s)e_{K_s}D_s
\in\mathcal Z_I.
\]

Now take monomials \(P\in\mathcal P_U\), \(Q\in\mathcal P_W\), \(R\in\mathcal P_U\). Isolate the last \(e_U\)-block of \(P\), the first and last \(e_W\)-blocks of \(Q\), and the first \(e_U\)-block of \(R\). The middle part has the form covered by (5), hence lies in \(\mathcal Z_I\). The remaining prefix and suffix preserve \(\mathcal Z_I\) by (4). Thus
\[
\mathcal P_U\mathcal P_W\mathcal P_U\subset\mathcal Z_I\subset X_I.
\tag{6}
\]
By norm closure,
\[
h^U_\lambda h^W_\mu h^U_\lambda\in X_I\subset K_{X_I}(M)
\tag{7}
\]
for the approximate units chosen above.

### 5. Weak-* order limit

Set
\[
a_\lambda=\iota_{\rm nor}(h^U_\lambda),
\qquad
b_\mu=\iota_{\rm nor}(h^W_\mu).
\]
By (1),
\[
a_\lambda\to q_U,\qquad b_\mu\to q_W
\]
weak-*.

Because \(h^U_\lambda,h^W_\mu\in A_{U,W}\), restricted multiplicativity (2) gives
\[
a_\lambda b_\mu a_\lambda
=
\iota_{\rm nor}(h^U_\lambda h^W_\mu h^U_\lambda).
\]
By (7), the operator \(h^U_\lambda h^W_\mu h^U_\lambda\) is a positive contraction in \(K_{X_I}(M)\). Therefore its normal-bidual image lies in the \(q_I\)-corner and
\[
0\le a_\lambda b_\mu a_\lambda\le q_I.
\tag{8}
\]

Fix \(\lambda\) and let \(\mu\to\infty\). Separate normality of multiplication in \(\mathcal N\) yields
\[
0\le a_\lambda q_W a_\lambda\le q_I.
\tag{9}
\]
Finally, let \(\lambda\to\infty\). Since each \(a_\lambda\) is a positive contraction in the \(q_U\)-corner,
\[
0\le a_\lambda\le q_U.
\]
Together with \(a_\lambda\to q_U\) weak-*, this implies \(a_\lambda\to q_U\) strongly: for every normal positive functional \(\omega\),
\[
\omega\!\left((q_U-a_\lambda)^2\right)
\le
\omega(q_U-a_\lambda)\to0.
\]
Hence
\[
a_\lambda q_W a_\lambda\to q_Uq_Wq_U
\]
weak-*. The order interval \([0,q_I]\) is weak-* closed, so (9) gives
\[
q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_I.
\]

This proves the support-sandwich lemma. The proof never uses global multiplicativity of \(\iota_{\rm nor}\), never asserts \(q_Uq_W=q_I\), and never requires the false product inclusion \(X_UX_W\subset X_I\).

\[
q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_{U\cap W}

as the black-box support input. The proof should **not** claim to be self-contained after the support sandwich alone: it still needs the normal-bidual relative-proper-proximality characterization, support commutation, compression inclusion, and the compact-corner obstruction state with \(\Omega(q_K)=0\).

The main wording risks are real but repairable: clarify irreducibility as “no nontrivial join partition,” avoid direct use of action Lemma 8.6 unless factoriality is proved, extend \(\Omega\) before the GNS finite-cover argument, and never use commutativity/equality of the \(q_U\)’s.

## Hostile audit by step

### 1. Graph irreducibility convention

This must be stated as:

\[
V=V_1\sqcup V_2,\qquad V_1,V_2\neq\varnothing,
\]
with every vertex of \(V_1\) adjacent to every vertex of \(V_2\).

If empty or overlapping subgraphs are allowed literally, the problem statement becomes ambiguous or vacuous. Under the nontrivial join convention, the graph facts used later are correct:

- \(\operatorname{Star}(v)\neq V\) for every \(v\).
- \(N(v)=V\setminus\operatorname{Star}(v)\neq\varnothing\).
- If \(N(v)=\{w\}\), then \(\operatorname{Link}(v)\neq\varnothing\), and there is \(z\in\operatorname{Link}(v)\) not adjacent to \(w\).

No hidden \(|V|=3\) exception remains.

---

### 2. Ping-pong unitaries

For fixed \(v\), with \(L=\operatorname{Link}(v)\), \(B=M_L\), \(C=M_{V\setminus\{v\}}\), the construction of \(c_1,c_2\in C\) is valid.

- If there are two non-neighbors \(w_1,w_2\), take \(c_i=u_{w_i}\).
- If there is a unique non-neighbor \(w\), choose \(z\in L\) not adjacent to \(w\), and take
  \[
  c_1=u_w,\qquad c_2=u_z u_w.
  \]

Then
\[
E_B(c_i)=0,\qquad E_B(c_i^*c_j)=\delta_{ij}1.
\]

The order-two case is harmless: even if \(u_w=u_w^*=u_w^{-1}\), the word \(u_w^*u_zu_w\) cannot reduce because \(z\) and \(w\) are not adjacent.

---

### 3. AFP decomposition

The decomposition
\[
M=M_{\operatorname{Star}(v)} *_ {M_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}} M_{V\setminus\{v\}}
\]
is a standard graph-product fact and is used correctly. Also
\[
M_{\operatorname{Star}(v)}\cong M_v\bar\otimes M_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}.
\]

No extra independence or factoriality assumption is needed here.

---

### 4. First-letter projections in \(S_X\)

The argument for \(R_A,R_C,P_A,P_C\in S_{X_L}(M)\) is sound.

For \(x\in A^\circ\), right multiplication preserves the first-\(A\) sector, so
\[
[R_A,\rho(x)]=0.
\]
For \(x\in C^\circ\), similarly
\[
[R_C,\rho(x)]=0.
\]
The cross commutators reduce to
\[
[e_B,\rho(x)].
\]
Since \(e_B\in X_L\) and the \(X_L\)-compact ideal is stable under \(JMJ\)-multipliers, these commutators lie in \(K_{X_L}^{\infty,1}(M)\). Thus the DKEP generator criterion gives the desired membership.

No global normal-bidual multiplicativity is being used at this stage.

---

### 5. Ping-pong contradiction

The projection inequalities are correct:

\[
c_1R_Ac_1^*+c_2R_Ac_2^*\le P_C,
\qquad
aR_Ca^*\le P_A.
\]

For an \(M\)-central state \(\varphi\) on \(S_{X_L}(M)\), set

\[
\alpha=\varphi(e_B),\quad \beta=\varphi(P_A),\quad \gamma=\varphi(P_C).
\]

Then

\[
2(\alpha+\beta)\le \gamma,
\qquad
\alpha+\gamma\le\beta,
\qquad
\alpha+\beta+\gamma=1,
\]
which is impossible. Therefore there is no \(M\)-central state on \(S_{X_L}(M)\), hence \(M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X_L\).

This proves the concrete relative statement. The passage to the normal-bidual relative formulation must be cited separately.

---

### 6. Concrete-to-normal-bidual passage

This is acceptable if the final proof explicitly invokes the DKEP/DE equivalence:

\[
M \text{ properly proximal relative to } X
\iff
\widetilde S_X(M) \text{ has no } M\text{-central state normal on }M.
\]

Do **not** silently identify concrete and normal-bidual operator systems.

---

### 7. No amenable direct summand

The hypertrace argument is valid and avoids any need to prove factoriality.

If \(0\neq z\in Z(M)\) and \(zM\) is amenable, a \(zM\)-central hypertrace \(\psi\) on \(B(zL^2M)\) gives, for fixed \(v\),

\[
\Phi(T)=\psi(zTz|_{zL^2M}),\qquad T\in S_{X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}}(M).
\]

Then \(\Phi\) is \(M\)-central and normal on \(M\), contradicting the ping-pong result. Hence \(M\) has no amenable direct summand.

The proof should not rely on a factor theorem unless separately cited; it is unnecessary.

---

### 8. Absolute obstruction state

The final proof must explicitly cite the following obstruction lemma:

If \(M\) is separable, has no amenable direct summand, and is not properly proximal, then there exists an \(M\)-central state
\[
\Omega:\widetilde S(M)\to\mathbb C
\]
normal on \(M\), such that
\[
\Omega(q_K)=0.
\]

This is the correct replacement for any direct appeal to a center-ergodic action lemma. In particular, do **not** apply a trivial-action DKEP lemma that assumes center ergodicity unless factoriality has already been proved.

---

### 9. Compression proof: \(\Omega(q_{\operatorname{Link}(v)})=1\)

This step is sound with the standard support facts.

Let \(X=X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}\), \(q=q_X\), \(r=1-q\). If \(\Omega(r)>0\), define

\[
\Phi(T)=\frac{\Omega(rTr)}{\Omega(r)},\qquad T\in \widetilde S_X(M).
\]

This is legitimate because the DKEP/DE compression inclusion gives

\[
r\,\widetilde S_X(M)\,r\subset \widetilde S(M).
\]

The state \(\Phi\) is \(M\)-central because \(r\) commutes with \(M\). Its restriction to \(M\) is normal because, for \(x\in M_+\),

\[
0\le r x r\le x,
\]
so \(\Phi|_M\) is dominated by the normal functional \(\Omega|_M/\Omega(r)\).

This contradicts relative proper proximality to \(X\). Hence

\[
\Omega(q_{\operatorname{Link}(v)})=1
\]
for every \(v\).

No normality of \(\Omega\) on the ambient normal-bidual algebra is used.

---

### 10. Finite-cover step

Before applying GNS, the state must be extended from the operator system \(\widetilde S(M)\) to the ambient \(C^*\)-algebra generated by the support projections. After extension, the argument uses only the one-sided sandwich.

If \(\bar\Omega(q_U)=\bar\Omega(q_W)=1\), then in the GNS representation,

\[
\bar\Omega(q_Uq_Wq_U)=1.
\]

By the support sandwich,

\[
0\le q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_{U\cap W},
\]
so

\[
\bar\Omega(q_{U\cap W})=1.
\]

Iterating over \(U_v=\operatorname{Link}(v)\) gives

\[
\bar\Omega\left(q_{\bigcap_v \operatorname{Link}(v)}\right)=1.
\]

Since the graph is loopless,

\[
\bigcap_v \operatorname{Link}(v)=\varnothing,
\]
so

\[
\bar\Omega(q_\varnothing)=1.
\]

No commutativity or equality formula for the \(q_U\)’s is used.

---

### 11. Empty parabolic equals compact support

This must be proved explicitly.

For \(U=\varnothing\), \(M_\varnothing=\mathbb C1\), and \(e_\varnothing\) is the rank-one projection onto \(\mathbb C\widehat 1\). Its left-right \(M\)-translates span rank-one operators

\[
\theta_{\widehat a,\widehat b},
\]
with \(a,b\in M\). Since \(M\widehat 1\) is dense in \(L^2M\), the resulting hereditary boundary piece is exactly \(K(L^2M)\). Hence

\[
q_\varnothing=q_K.
\]

Thus the finite-cover conclusion gives \(\Omega(q_K)=1\), contradicting \(\Omega(q_K)=0\).

## Concise final proof skeleton

1. Interpret irreducibility as no nontrivial join partition.
2. Fix \(v\), set
   \[
   L=\operatorname{Link}(v),\quad B=M_L,\quad A=M_{\operatorname{Star}(v)},\quad C=M_{V\setminus\{v\}}.
   \]
   Use the AFP decomposition \(M=A*_B C\).
3. Construct \(a\in A^\circ\) and \(c_1,c_2\in C^\circ\) with
   \[
   E_B(c_i^*c_j)=\delta_{ij}1.
   \]
4. Show first-letter projections \(R_A,R_C,P_A,P_C\in S_{X_L}(M)\).
5. Prove
   \[
   c_1R_Ac_1^*+c_2R_Ac_2^*\le P_C,\qquad aR_Ca^*\le P_A.
   \]
6. Apply any \(M\)-central state to obtain impossible inequalities. Hence \(M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}\) for every \(v\).
7. Use the hypertrace compression argument to exclude amenable direct summands.
8. If \(M\) were not properly proximal, use the DKEP/DE obstruction to get \(\Omega\) on \(\widetilde S(M)\), normal on \(M\), with \(\Omega(q_K)=0\).
9. Relative proper proximality plus compression gives
   \[
   \Omega(q_{\operatorname{Link}(v)})=1
   \]
   for every \(v\).
10. Extend \(\Omega\) to the ambient \(C^*\)-algebra and iterate the support sandwich to get
    \[
    \Omega(q_\varnothing)=1.
    \]
11. Since \(q_\varnothing=q_K\), this contradicts \(\Omega(q_K)=0\). Therefore \(M\) is properly proximal.

---

writeup_tasks.writeup_agent_prompt:
Write a fully self-contained proof of the stated theorem. The reader will not have access to the solver history, so do not refer to previous tasks, attached notes, or 'the problem above'. State every convention and all notation inline. Use the standard convention that irreducible means no nontrivial join partition V=V1 disjoint union V2 with both parts nonempty and all cross edges present; note that this is the intended interpretation of the graph hypothesis. The theorem should be stated for tracial graph products over a finite simplicial graph Γ with |V|≥3 and trace-zero unitaries in every vertex algebra.

Your proof should be organized as follows.

1. Definitions and external facts. Define the tracial graph product M=M_Γ and parabolic subalgebras M_U for U⊆V. Define Link(v), Star(v). Define DKEP proper proximality in the form needed here: for the absolute compact boundary X=K(L2M), S_X(M) is the operator system of T with [T,JxJ] in K_X^{∞,1} for all x∈M, and M is properly proximal iff there is no M-central state on S_X(M) whose restriction to M is normal. Also define relative boundary pieces X_U associated to the Jones projection e_U for M_U⊂M, and relative proper proximality with respect to X_U. Explain that q_U denotes the normal-bidual support projection attached to X_U, and define q_K by the same support construction for X=K(L2M). State the DE/DKEP normal-bidual facts you use: (a) if separable tracial M has no amenable direct summand and is not properly proximal, there is an M-central normal-on-M obstruction state Ω on the absolute normal-bidual system with Ω(q_K)=0; (b) if M is properly proximal relative to X_U, then every such Ω satisfies Ω(q_U)=1, by compression with 1−q_U; (c) q_U commutes with the left and right M-actions, and the compression inclusion needed for (b) is valid. Do not claim Ω is normal on the whole ambient normal bidual.

2. Graph preliminaries. Prove that irreducibility implies Star(v)≠V for every vertex v. Also prove that if v has a unique non-neighbor w, then there exists z∈Link(v) not adjacent to w. These facts are needed to choose ping-pong unitaries. Mention that overlap in a join partition is impossible for a loopless graph, and empty join factors are excluded by convention.

3. Link-relative AFP ping-pong lemma. For a fixed vertex v, put B=M_Link(v), A=M_Star(v), and C=M_{V\{v}}. State the standard graph-product AFP decomposition M=A *_B C with trace-preserving expectations. Let e_B be the Jones projection. In the AFP L2-decomposition, define R_A as the projection onto L2(B) plus reduced words whose first letter is from A⊖B, R_C analogously, and P_A=R_A−e_B, P_C=R_C−e_B. Prove R_A,R_C,P_A,P_C belong to S_{X_B}(M): check commutators with right multiplication by centered elements of A and C, observe that the only crossing terms factor through L2(B), hence lie in K_{X_B}^{∞,1}, and invoke the generator criterion for S_X.

Then choose a∈A unitary with E_B(a)=0, namely the trace-zero unitary from M_v. Choose c1,c2∈C unitaries with E_B(c_i)=0 and E_B(c_i^* c_j)=δ_ij 1. If v has at least two non-neighbors, take trace-zero unitaries from two such vertex algebras. If v has unique non-neighbor w, use the graph lemma to choose z∈Link(v) not adjacent to w and set c1=u_w, c2=u_z u_w; explain why E_B(u_w^* u_z u_w)=0 even if u_w has order two, because the graph word w,z,w is reduced and contains w outside Link(v). Prove the projection inequalities c1 R_A c1^* + c2 R_A c2^* ≤ P_C and a R_C a^* ≤ P_A. If φ were an M-central state on S_{X_B}(M), set α=φ(e_B), β=φ(P_A), γ=φ(P_C). Then 2(α+β)≤γ and α+γ≤β, while α+β+γ=1, a contradiction. Conclude that M is properly proximal relative to X_Link(v) for every vertex v.

4. No amenable direct summand. Prove or cite from the link-relative lemma that M has no amenable direct summand: if zM were a nonzero amenable central summand, a hypertrace on B(zL2M) would give, by T↦Ψ(zTz) after normalization, an M-central state on S_{X_Link(v)}(M) normal on M, contradicting the previous lemma. Alternatively you may cite the known graph-product factor/nonamenability theorem, but the hypertrace argument is preferred because it avoids unnecessary factoriality.

5. Parabolic support sandwich. Prove the technical lemma: for all U,W⊆V, with I=U∩W, the normal-bidual supports satisfy q_U q_W q_U ≤ q_I. This is the key point. Present it carefully but do not overclaim. Use the following structure. Let A_alg be the algebraic span of reduced graph-product words. Let the algebraic hereditary core for X_U be generated by operators x J y J e_U J y' J x' or equivalently finite sums built from L_x R_y e_U with x,y∈A_alg. State that this core is dense in the DKEP ∥·∥∞,1 support topology and admits positive contraction approximate units h_U whose normal-bidual images converge weak-star to q_U; cite DE's approximate-unit support lemma. State and prove a finite-propagation sublemma: for algebraic multipliers a,b, the operator e_U L_a R_b e_W factors through a finite sum of Jones projections e_J with J⊆U∩W, up to left-right algebraic multipliers. Prove this from graph-product reduced normal forms and the conditional expectation formula E_U(a y b) for y∈M_W: only letters supported in U∩W can survive the expectation, and the surviving link set J is contained in U∩W. Then conclude that algebraic U-W-U triple products h_U h_W h_U lie in the boundary piece X_I, or at least in its ∥·∥∞,1-closed compact ideal. Finally use restricted multiplicativity of the normal-bidual compression only on C*(M, JMJ, e_U, e_W): the normal support projection commutes with M, JMJ, e_U, and e_W, so compression is a *-homomorphism on this generated C*-algebra. Taking weak-star order limits gives q_U q_W q_U≤q_I. Explicitly say that you do not prove or use q_U q_W=q_{U∩W}, commutation of q_U and q_W, or X_U X_W⊂X_{U∩W}.

6. Empty piece. Prove q_empty=q_K with the convention for q_K above. Since M_empty=C, e_empty is the rank-one projection onto C·hat(1); its left-right M-translates span finite-rank operators on the dense subspace M·hat(1), so the hereditary boundary piece X_empty is K(L2M). Hence its support is q_K.

7. Final obstruction argument. Suppose M is not properly proximal. By separability and the no-amenable-direct-summand result, take the DKEP/DE obstruction state Ω with Ω(q_K)=0. Since M is properly proximal relative to X_Link(v) for every v, the compression argument gives Ω(q_Link(v))=1 for every vertex v. Extend Ω, if necessary, to a state on the ambient C*-algebra generated by the support projections q_U so that products such as q_U q_W q_U make sense in the GNS representation. Use the elementary support-one fact: if a state ψ has ψ(p)=1 for a projection p, then ψ(x)=ψ(pxp), equivalently π(p)ξ=ξ in the GNS representation. Therefore, if ψ(q_U)=ψ(q_W)=1, then ψ(q_U q_W q_U)=1; by q_U q_W q_U≤q_{U∩W}, positivity gives ψ(q_{U∩W})=1. Iterate over the finite family U_v=Link(v). Since the graph has no loops, ∩_{v∈V} Link(v)=empty: each vertex x is not in Link(x). Hence Ω(q_empty)=1. By q_empty=q_K this contradicts Ω(q_K)=0. Conclude M is properly proximal.

The final proof should be polished and referee-grade. It may cite the standard graph-product AFP decomposition and the DKEP/DE normal-bidual framework as published external facts, but the link ping-pong, graph-theoretic unitary construction, support sandwich, and finite-cover contradiction must be explained in enough detail to stand alone. Avoid all of the following: applying an action-level DKEP lemma requiring center-ergodicity; claiming global multiplicativity of the normal-bidual map; claiming q_U and q_W commute; claiming q_U q_W=q_{U∩W}; claiming ambient normality of Ω; or running the GNS product argument before extending Ω to an ambient C*-algebra state.
